_Thought for 3m 40s_

I would **not** assume we can just close symmetric zero-capture from the current ingredients.

My honest read is: **probably not as a clean theorem of the form “all zeros of both directional kernels lie in a compact interior box”**. The current draft/convo still treats zero-capture as the live turning-boundary theorem, and nothing in the latest push obviously removes the core difficulty that a zero sequence could escape toward a non-common endpoint of \(I_1\) or \(I_2\), not just toward the shared boundary. That is exactly why the current turning-boundary theorem was made conditional in the first place. turn80file7

What **is** now in place is stronger local control:

- finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundaries are locally sterile for pair-pair birth;
- generic turning boundaries have local support ordering / boundary-near exclusion;
- pair-pair overlap across a generic turning boundary yields a compact interior zero of **one of** the two directional kernels;
- the mirrored first-zero lemma and the symmetric conditional theorem reduce the remaining issue to **symmetric zero-capture** for \(K_{21}\) and \(K_{12}\). turn80file4turn80file5

So the aggressive thing to do now is **not** try to prove full symmetric zero-capture in one jump. I think the better move is to attack a theorem that is both weaker and more closely tailored to the global obstruction.

The right replacement target is:

> **pair-pair-generated zero-capture**, not full zero-capture.

In other words:

If a finite-slope line is \(2\)-secant to both adjacent branches across a generic turning boundary, then the directional-kernel zero produced by the compact support-overlap endpoint argument is already realized on a compact interior pair, and the first-zero/tangency contradiction can be run **inside that compact overlap family**, without needing a theorem about all other zeros of the kernel on the whole open rectangle.

That avoids the hardest part of zero-capture entirely: you no longer need to control arbitrary stray zeros near far endpoints that may be irrelevant to the pair-pair obstruction.

So my blunt answer is:

- **Can we aggressively attack symmetric zero-capture?** Yes.
- **Do I think we can cleanly close the full theorem right now?** No, not from the current ingredients alone.
- **Do I think we can likely bypass full zero-capture with a more targeted compact first-contact theorem tied to support overlap?** Yes, and that is where I would push next.

The theorem I would try to prove next is this:

> Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent across a generic turning boundary. Suppose a finite-slope line is \(2\)-secant to both branches. Then, after the support-overlap truncation already in the draft, there exists a compact interior overlap family for which the overlap endpoint produces a directional kernel zero that is first in the relevant branch-ordering **within that compact family**, and hence yields the tangency contradiction.

If that works, you do not need global zero-capture for all kernel zeros. You only need a compact first-contact theorem for zeros arising from actual support overlap.

That is a much more plausible frontier than “prove every zero of \(K_{21}\) and \(K_{12}\) on the whole adjacent rectangle is trapped in a compact interior box.”

So my recommendation is: **do not try to close full symmetric zero-capture as the next move**. Attack the narrower compact-overlap/first-contact theorem instead. It is much closer to the actual no-four-secant obstruction, and it uses the machinery you now already have.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:07:52)

_Thought for 8m 11s_

Yes — I think there is a real way to **bypass full symmetric zero-capture**.

The key is to replace “capture all zeros of the kernel on the whole open rectangle” with a much weaker and more relevant statement:

> for a **fixed compact opposite branch** \(J_1\Subset I_1\), tangencies from the adjacent branch \(I_2\) that are sufficiently close to the shared turning boundary cannot hit \(J_1\) at all.

That gives a **strip exclusion lemma**, and once you have that, the compact first-contact argument works on the actual compact overlap family coming from pair-pair, with no need to capture arbitrary stray zeros elsewhere.

So the attack I think works is this.

---

# 1. Prove a strip exclusion lemma

Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent across a generic turning boundary \(h_b\), and work in the affine chart
\[
x(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad y(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Near \(h_b\), the draft already gives
\[
x'(h_b)=0,\qquad x''(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad y'(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
so after using \(y\) as local parameter, the normalized curve is locally
\[
x=\chi(y)
\]
with \(\chi''(0)\neq 0\). Thus \(x\) has a strict local extremum at the boundary, and the tangent slope
\[
m(y)=\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{\chi'(y)}
\]
blows up one-sidedly as \(y\to 0^\pm\).

Now let \(J_1\Subset I_1\) be any compact subinterval away from the shared boundary.

## Lemma (strip exclusion for \(K_{21}\))
There exists a one-sided neighborhood \(U_2\subset I_2\) of the shared boundary such that
\[
K_{21}(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in U_2,\ t\in J_1).
\]

### Proof
Write the two branches in the local \(y\)-coordinate. Since \(J_1\) is compact away from the boundary, there is a fixed \(\delta>0\) such that for all \(t\in J_1\),
\[
|x(t)-x(h_b)|\ge 2\delta.
\]
For \(s\in I_2\) sufficiently near \(h_b\),
\[
|x(s)-x(h_b)|\le \delta,
\]
so
\[
|x(t)-x(s)|\ge \delta
\qquad
(t\in J_1).
\]

The tangent line at \(s\) has slope \(m(s)\), and the condition that it passes through \(t\) is
\[
y(t)-y(s)=m(s)\bigl(x(t)-x(s)\bigr).
\]
But as \(s\to h_b\) on the \(I_2\)-side, \(m(s)\to \pm\infty\), while
\[
y(t)-y(s)
\]
remains uniformly bounded for \(t\in J_1\), and
\[
x(t)-x(s)
\]
stays bounded away from \(0\). Therefore the right-hand side has arbitrarily large magnitude and fixed sign, while the left-hand side stays bounded. So equality is impossible for \(s\) sufficiently close to \(h_b\).

Hence
\[
K_{21}(s,t)\neq 0
\]
for all \(s\in U_2,\ t\in J_1\).

The same argument gives the mirror statement:

## Lemma (strip exclusion for \(K_{12}\))
For every compact \(J_2\Subset I_2\), there exists a one-sided neighborhood \(U_1\subset I_1\) of the boundary such that
\[
K_{12}(t,s)=\det(F(t),F'(t),F(s))\neq 0
\qquad
(t\in U_1,\ s\in J_2).
\]

This is the crucial replacement for full zero-capture.

---

# 2. Use strip exclusion to get a compact first-contact theorem

Now suppose the bridge lemma has already produced a compact interior zero of one of the two directional kernels, say \(K_{21}\), on a compact pair
\[
J_2\Subset I_2,\qquad J_1\Subset I_1.
\]
Enlarge \(J_2\) slightly toward the shared boundary so that its boundary-facing collar lies in the strip \(U_2\) from the previous lemma, while still remaining compact and interior.

Define the compact zero set
\[
Z_{21}:=\{(s,t)\in J_2\times J_1:\ K_{21}(s,t)=0\}.
\]
If \(Z_{21}\neq\varnothing\), let
\[
A_{21}:=\{s\in J_2:\exists\, t\in J_1 \text{ with } K_{21}(s,t)=0\}.
\]
Since \(Z_{21}\) is compact, \(A_{21}\) is compact. Since the boundary-facing collar of \(J_2\) is zero-free against all \(t\in J_1\), the set \(A_{21}\) is bounded away from that collar. So \(A_{21}\) has a boundary-nearest element \(s_*\) that is still an interior point of \(J_2\). Pick \(t_*\in J_1\) with
\[
K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]

Now argue exactly as in the old first-zero lemma, but only inside this compact family:

if
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)\neq 0,
\]
then the implicit function theorem gives a local zero curve
\[
t=t(s)
\]
through \((s_*,t_*)\), so for \(s\) slightly boundary-nearer than \(s_*\), still inside \(J_2\), we get another zero \((s,t(s))\in Z_{21}\), contradicting the minimality of \(s_*\) in \(A_{21}\).

Therefore
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]
Together with
\[
K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0,
\]
this means the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\) is also tangent at \(\Gamma(t_*)\).

So from a compact directional-kernel zero we get a **common tangent line** across the adjacent branches, without any global zero-capture theorem.

The same argument mirrors for \(K_{12}\).

---

# 3. This gives the actual bypass theorem

So the theorem to prove next is:

## Theorem (compact first-contact exclusion for pair-pair-generated directional zeros)
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent across a generic turning boundary. Suppose a finite-slope line is \(2\)-secant to both branches. Then the support-overlap truncation and overlap-endpoint lemma produce a compact interior zero of one of \(K_{21}\) or \(K_{12}\). For either direction, the strip exclusion lemma and compact first-contact argument force a common tangent line across the two adjacent branches. This contradicts adjacent interior slope-range separation (or endpoint tangent exclusion in the endpoint case). Therefore no such adjacent pair-pair line exists.

That is the payoff. It gives you **adjacent pair-pair exclusion across generic turning boundaries without full symmetric zero-capture**.

---

# 4. Why this is better than full zero-capture

Because it only uses the zeros that actually arise from a genuine support-overlap / pair-pair family. It does **not** require any theorem about all other possible kernel zeros on the whole open rectangle \(I_2\times I_1\) or \(I_1\times I_2\).

So the remaining burden becomes:

- prove the strip exclusion lemma carefully,
- package the compact first-contact theorem for both directions,
- then replace the current “conditional symmetric directional-kernel exclusion” with this more targeted unconditional adjacent pair-pair exclusion theorem.

That is much more plausible than full symmetric zero-capture.

---

# 5. What I would do next

I would now try to freeze exactly these three statements in paper notation:

1. **Strip exclusion for \(K_{21}\) against a compact opposite branch**  
   and its mirror for \(K_{12}\);

2. **Compact first-contact implies tangency**  
   for zeros of \(K_{21}\) or \(K_{12}\) on a compact box with a zero-free boundary-facing collar;

3. **Adjacent pair-pair exclusion across a generic turning boundary**  
   by combining the bridge lemma with the two items above.

This is the first route that looks like a genuine closure of the turning-boundary geometry without needing the full old zero-capture theorem.

If you want, I’ll write those three as exact LaTeX insertions for the current draft.

# 1) Insert the strip-exclusion and compact first-contact lemmas, plus the new unconditional adjacent pair-pair exclusion theorem

## Where to insert

## Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{lemma}[Strip exclusion for \(K_{21}\) against a compact opposite branch]
\label{lem:strip-exclusion-k21}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a generic turning boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Let \(J_1\Subset I_1\) be a compact subinterval. Then there exists a one-sided
neighborhood \(U_2\subset I_2\) of the shared boundary point \(h_b\) such that
\[
K_{21}(s,t):=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in U_2,\ t\in J_1),
\]
where \(F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h))\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Work in the affine chart
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
At a generic turning boundary one has
\[
x'(h_b)=0,\qquad x''(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad y'(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Hence \(y\) is a valid local coordinate and the normalized curve may be written
near \(h_b\) as a graph
\[
x=\chi(y)
\]
with
\[
\chi'(y_b)=0,\qquad \chi''(y_b)\neq 0.
\]
Thus \(x\) has a strict local extremum at the boundary, while the tangent slope
\[
m(h)=\frac{dy/dh}{dx/dh}=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
blows up one-sidedly as \(h\to h_b\) from either adjacent interval.

Because \(J_1\Subset I_1\) is compact away from \(h_b\), there exists
\(\delta>0\) such that
\[
|x(t)-x(h_b)|\ge 2\delta
\qquad
(t\in J_1).
\]
After shrinking to a small one-sided neighborhood \(U_2\subset I_2\) of \(h_b\),
we may also assume
\[
|x(s)-x(h_b)|\le \delta
\qquad
(s\in U_2).
\]
Hence
\[
|x(t)-x(s)|\ge \delta
\qquad
(s\in U_2,\ t\in J_1).
\]

Fix a preliminary compact neighborhood \(U_2^0\Subset I_2\) of \(h_b\) and set
\[
B:=\sup\{|y(t)-y(s)|:\ t\in J_1,\ s\in U_2^0\}<\infty.
\]
Since \(m(s)\to \pm\infty\) as \(s\to h_b\) in \(I_2\), we may shrink further to
\(U_2\subset U_2^0\) so that
\[
|m(s)|>\frac{2B}{\delta}
\qquad
(s\in U_2).
\]

If \(K_{21}(s,t)=0\), then the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s)\) passes through
\(\Gamma(t)\), equivalently
\[
y(t)-y(s)=m(s)\bigl(x(t)-x(s)\bigr).
\]
Taking absolute values gives
\[
B\ge |y(t)-y(s)|=|m(s)|\,|x(t)-x(s)|>\frac{2B}{\delta}\cdot \delta=2B,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore \(K_{21}(s,t)\neq 0\) on \(U_2\times J_1\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Strip exclusion for \(K_{12}\) against a compact opposite branch]
\label{lem:strip-exclusion-k12}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a generic turning boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Let \(J_2\Subset I_2\) be a compact subinterval. Then there exists a one-sided
neighborhood \(U_1\subset I_1\) of the shared boundary point \(h_b\) such that
\[
K_{12}(t,s):=\det(F(t),F'(t),F(s))\neq 0
\qquad
(t\in U_1,\ s\in J_2),
\]
where \(F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h))\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
This is the left-right mirror of
Lemma~\ref{lem:strip-exclusion-k21}, exchanging the roles of \(I_1\) and \(I_2\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Compact first-contact for \(K_{21}\)]
\label{lem:compact-first-contact-k21}
Let \(J_1\Subset I_1\), \(J_2\Subset I_2\) be compact subintervals across a
generic turning boundary. Assume there is a relatively open collar
\(V_2\subset J_2\) at the boundary-facing end of \(J_2\) such that
\[
K_{21}(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in V_2,\ t\in J_1).
\]
Assume also that the zero set of \(K_{21}\) in \(J_2\times J_1\) is nonempty.
Then there exists a zero \((s_*,t_*)\in J_2\times J_1\) such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(s_*\) is boundary-nearest in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering among all
\(s\in J_2\) for which \(K_{21}(s,t)=0\) for some \(t\in J_1\);
\item
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Define
\[
A_{21}:=\{\,s\in J_2:\ \exists\, t\in J_1 \text{ with } K_{21}(s,t)=0\,\}.
\]
Since the zero set of \(K_{21}\) in \(J_2\times J_1\) is compact and nonempty,
the set \(A_{21}\) is compact and nonempty. By hypothesis,
\[
A_{21}\cap V_2=\varnothing.
\]
Hence \(A_{21}\) has a boundary-nearest element \(s_*\) in the \(x=v_5/c\)
ordering on \(J_2\). Choose \(t_*\in J_1\) with
\[
K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]

Suppose
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)\neq 0.
\]
Then by the implicit function theorem there is a \(C^1\) local zero curve
\[
t=t(s)
\]
through \((s_*,t_*)\), defined for \(s\) near \(s_*\), such that
\[
K_{21}(s,t(s))=0.
\]
Because \(s_*\notin V_2\) and \(V_2\) is an open collar at the boundary-facing
end of \(J_2\), there exist nearby points \(s\in J_2\) that are boundary-nearer
than \(s_*\) in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering. For such \(s\), the point
\((s,t(s))\) is also a zero of \(K_{21}\), contradicting the minimality of
\(s_*\). Therefore
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Compact first-contact for \(K_{12}\)]
\label{lem:compact-first-contact-k12}
Let \(J_1\Subset I_1\), \(J_2\Subset I_2\) be compact subintervals across a
generic turning boundary. Assume there is a relatively open collar
\(V_1\subset J_1\) at the boundary-facing end of \(J_1\) such that
\[
K_{12}(t,s)\neq 0
\qquad
(t\in V_1,\ s\in J_2).
\]
Assume also that the zero set of \(K_{12}\) in \(J_1\times J_2\) is nonempty.
Then there exists a zero \((t_*,s_*)\in J_1\times J_2\) such that:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(t_*\) is boundary-nearest in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering among all
\(t\in J_1\) for which \(K_{12}(t,s)=0\) for some \(s\in J_2\);
\item
\[
\partial_s K_{12}(t_*,s_*)=0.
\]
\end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Define
\[
A_{12}:=\{\,t\in J_1:\ \exists\, s\in J_2 \text{ with } K_{12}(t,s)=0\,\}.
\]
Since the zero set of \(K_{12}\) in \(J_1\times J_2\) is compact and nonempty,
the set \(A_{12}\) is compact and nonempty. By hypothesis,
\[
A_{12}\cap V_1=\varnothing.
\]
Hence \(A_{12}\) has a boundary-nearest element \(t_*\) in the \(x=v_5/c\)
ordering on \(J_1\). Choose \(s_*\in J_2\) with
\[
K_{12}(t_*,s_*)=0.
\]

Suppose
\[
\partial_s K_{12}(t_*,s_*)\neq 0.
\]
Then by the implicit function theorem there is a \(C^1\) local zero curve
\[
s=s(t)
\]
through \((t_*,s_*)\), defined for \(t\) near \(t_*\), such that
\[
K_{12}(t,s(t))=0.
\]
Because \(t_*\notin V_1\) and \(V_1\) is an open collar at the boundary-facing
end of \(J_1\), there exist nearby points \(t\in J_1\) that are boundary-nearer
than \(t_*\) in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering. For such \(t\), the point
\((t,s(t))\) is also a zero of \(K_{12}\), contradicting the minimality of
\(t_*\). Therefore
\[
\partial_s K_{12}(t_*,s_*)=0.
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}[Adjacent pair-pair exclusion across a generic turning boundary]
\label{thm:adjacent-pair-pair-exclusion-turning}
Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals meeting at a generic turning boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]
Then no finite-slope affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to both \(\Gamma(I_1)\) and \(\Gamma(I_2)\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Assume for contradiction that a finite-slope line
\[
L:\ y=mx+b
\]
is \(2\)-secant to both adjacent branches. By
Lemma~\ref{lem:pair-pair-gives-directional-kernel-zero}, after removing a
small boundary neighborhood there exist compact subintervals
\[
J_1\Subset I_1,\qquad J_2\Subset I_2
\]
and a zero of one of the two directional kernels.

First suppose there exist \(s_0\in J_2\), \(t_0\in J_1\) such that
\[
K_{21}(s_0,t_0)=0.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:strip-exclusion-k21}, there exists a one-sided neighborhood
\(U_2\subset I_2\) of \(h_b\) such that
\[
K_{21}(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in U_2,\ t\in J_1).
\]
Choose a compact interval \(\widetilde J_2\Subset I_2\) containing \(s_0\) and
whose boundary-facing collar \(V_2\) lies in \(U_2\). Then the zero set of
\(K_{21}\) in \(\widetilde J_2\times J_1\) is nonempty, and
Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-contact-k21} yields a zero
\[
(s_*,t_*)\in \widetilde J_2\times J_1
\]
such that
\[
\partial_t K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]
Together with \(K_{21}(s_*,t_*)=0\), this means that the tangent line at
\(\Gamma(s_*)\) is also tangent at \(\Gamma(t_*)\). Since
\(s_*\in I_2\) and \(t_*\in I_1\) are interior points, this gives a common
interior tangent slope across the adjacent branches, contradicting
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}.

Now suppose instead there exist \(t_0\in J_1\), \(s_0\in J_2\) such that
\[
K_{12}(t_0,s_0)=0.
\]
By Lemma~\ref{lem:strip-exclusion-k12}, there exists a one-sided neighborhood
\(U_1\subset I_1\) of \(h_b\) such that
\[
K_{12}(t,s)\neq 0
\qquad
(t\in U_1,\ s\in J_2).
\]
Choose a compact interval \(\widetilde J_1\Subset I_1\) containing \(t_0\) and
whose boundary-facing collar \(V_1\) lies in \(U_1\). Then the zero set of
\(K_{12}\) in \(\widetilde J_1\times J_2\) is nonempty, and
Lemma~\ref{lem:compact-first-contact-k12} yields a zero
\[
(t_*,s_*)\in \widetilde J_1\times J_2
\]
such that
\[
\partial_s K_{12}(t_*,s_*)=0.
\]
Together with \(K_{12}(t_*,s_*)=0\), this means that the tangent line at
\(\Gamma(t_*)\) is also tangent at \(\Gamma(s_*)\). Again this gives a common
interior tangent slope across the adjacent branches, contradicting
Proposition~\ref{prop:adjacent-slope-range-separation}.

Both cases are impossible. Therefore no finite-slope line is \(2\)-secant to
both adjacent branches across a generic turning boundary.
\end{proof}
```

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:25:09)

_Thought for 5m 8s_

I can draft the theorem you would need, but I do **not** think we can honestly mark it proved from the current ingredients yet.

The exact propagation theorem that would let you replace `thm:partial-global-no-secant` by the new route is this:

```latex
\begin{theorem}[Propagation to an adjacent turning pair]
\label{thm:propagation-to-adjacent-turning-pair}
Let
\[
I_1<I_2<\cdots<I_N
\]
be the ECT intervals on a connected generic component of the normalized curve,
and assume:

\begin{enumerate}
\item every internal boundary between adjacent intervals is either
a generic turning boundary
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0,
\]
or a finite-order boundary of type
\[
W(h_b)=0,\qquad M(h_b)\neq 0;
\]

\item on every connected open subinterval with \(c\neq 0\) and \(M\neq 0\),
one has \(W\not\equiv 0\);

\item every finite-order boundary of type \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) is locally sterile
for pair-pair birth in the sense of
Theorem~\ref{thm:finite-order-w-zero-local-sterility};
\end{enumerate}

Fix a finite-slope affine line
\[
L_{m,b}:\ y=mx+b.
\]
If \(L_{m,b}\) is \(2\)-secant to \(\Gamma(I_a)\) and to \(\Gamma(I_b)\) for
some \(a<b\), then there exists an index \(j\) with
\[
a\le j<b
\]
such that:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \(I_j<I_{j+1}\) meet at a generic turning boundary; and
\item \(L_{m,b}\) is \(2\)-secant to both \(\Gamma(I_j)\) and \(\Gamma(I_{j+1})\).
\end{enumerate}
\end{theorem}

\begin{corollary}[Global no-four-secant from adjacent turning exclusion]
\label{cor:global-no-four-secant-from-adjacent-turning}
Assume the hypotheses of
Theorem~\ref{thm:propagation-to-adjacent-turning-pair}, and assume in addition
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-pair-pair-exclusion-turning}. Then no finite-slope
affine line in the chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
If such a line existed, Theorem~\ref{thm:propagation-to-adjacent-turning-pair}
would produce an adjacent pair across a generic turning boundary to which the
line is \(2\)-secant on both sides, contradicting
Theorem~\ref{thm:adjacent-pair-pair-exclusion-turning}.
\end{proof}
```

That is the **right** theorem if your goal is to replace the old `thm:partial-global-no-secant` route with the new turning-boundary route.

But I want to be clear: the hard content is not the corollary. It is the propagation theorem itself. And inside that theorem, there is one genuinely nontrivial sublemma.

The sublemma you need is this:

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Turning birth forces adjacent persistence]
\label{lem:turning-birth-forces-adjacent-persistence}
Under the hypotheses of
Theorem~\ref{thm:propagation-to-adjacent-turning-pair},
let \(L_{m,b}\) be a finite-slope line and let
\[
\Sigma_{m,b}:=\{\,j:\ L_{m,b}\text{ is \(2\)-secant to }\Gamma(I_j)\,\}.
\]
Suppose \(j\) is the leftmost index in \(\Sigma_{m,b}\), and suppose the
boundary between \(I_j\) and \(I_{j+1}\) is generic turning. Then
\[
j+1\in \Sigma_{m,b}.
\]
Equivalently, once \(2\)-secancy is present at the first interval of a minimal
bad block, it persists across the first internal generic turning boundary.
\end{lemma}
```

That lemma is the real hinge. If you had it, the propagation theorem would be very believable:

- choose a pair-pair line hitting two intervals with minimal span;
- let \(a\) be the leftmost and \(b\) the rightmost \(2\)-secant intervals;
- finite-order \(W=0\) local sterility and no \(W\)-flat intervals tell you that the change from “not \(2\)-secant” to “\(2\)-secant” cannot be born at a \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary;
- so the first birth must occur at a generic turning boundary;
- the lemma above then forces persistence into the next interval, giving an adjacent turning pair with \(2+2\) secancy.

That is exactly what would let the new adjacent theorem replace the old partial global theorem.

So my honest recommendation is:

- **do not insert the propagation theorem as proved yet**;
- insert it only as a **target theorem / roadmap theorem** if you want;
- the actual next math attack should be on  
  **Lemma \(\ref{lem:turning-birth-forces-adjacent-persistence}\)**.

That is the precise new frontier. If you want, I can now try to attack that lemma directly and see whether it really goes through or whether another obstruction appears.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:27:30)

I think the first thing to check is whether the lemma is even *true as stated*. There is an immediate tension: if a line is already 2-secant to \(I_j\) and the next boundary is generic turning, “\(j+1\in\Sigma\)” would force an adjacent \(2+2\) pair, which your new turning-boundary theorem forbids. So I’m testing whether the lemma needs extra hypotheses or should be reformulated.

_Thought for 2m 11s_

I do **not** think that lemma is true as stated.

The proposed lemma was:

“Let Sigma_(m,b) be the set of indices j such that the line y = m x + b is 2-secant to branch I_j. If j is the leftmost index in Sigma and the boundary between I_j and I_(j+1) is a generic turning boundary, then j+1 is also in Sigma.”

That is too strong.

Why it fails:

What you currently know at a generic turning boundary is only a **local exclusion of adjacent pair-pair**. You do **not** know a global persistence law for fixed-slope intercept intervals. In support-interval language, for a fixed slope m, each branch has an interval B_j(m) of allowed intercepts. The new turning-boundary theorem says adjacent branches cannot both have b in the interior of their support intervals. But that does not force overlap to persist from B_j(m) into B_(j+1)(m).

Combinatorially, there is no contradiction in having, for one fixed slope m,

B_j(m) = (0,2),
B_(j+1)(m) = (3,4),
B_(j+2)(m) = (1,2).

Then b = 1.5 gives 2-secancy on j and j+2, but not on j+1. That already shows the persistence statement is not a consequence of “adjacent interiors are disjoint.”

And this is not just an abstract interval trick. Geometrically, you can realize that behavior with three strictly convex branches in x-order, joined by generic turning boundaries, where a line of fixed slope cuts the first and third branches twice but misses the middle branch as a 2-secant. Nothing in the current local turning-boundary theorem forbids that.

There is also a structural warning sign: if the lemma were true, then together with your new theorem excluding adjacent pair-pair across a generic turning boundary, you would immediately get something much stronger than you currently have — essentially a direct global no-four-secant result on any chain of turning boundaries — without needing the old support-ordering machinery. That is exactly the kind of “too good to be true” jump that usually signals the propagation lemma is overstated.

So I would not try to prove that lemma. I think the right conclusion is:

The “first birth persists into the next branch” statement is false, or at least far stronger than what the current ingredients justify.

What is true, and much closer to your actual machinery, is weaker:

If a line is 2-secant to two non-adjacent branches, then as you move from left to right across the chain of intervals, there is a first boundary where the fixed intercept b enters the support set. At that boundary, the entry cannot happen through a finite-order W=0, M ≠ 0 boundary by the local sterility theorem. So the first entry must occur at a generic turning boundary, and it occurs by **tangential birth** on one side, not necessarily by immediate 2-secancy on the next branch.

That weaker statement is exactly compatible with your current bridge lemmas:

- not “j+1 is also 2-secant,”
- but “at the first relevant turning boundary, one gets support contact / a directional kernel zero.”

That is a very different propagation mechanism.

So the corrected next target should be something like this:

For a fixed line y = m x + b, suppose it is 2-secant to two distinct branches, and choose such a pair with minimal index gap. Then there exists an internal generic turning boundary between them such that b lies in the support set of both adjacent branches at that boundary, with one side realizing b as an endpoint of its support interval. Equivalently, the overlap is born there by tangency, giving a directional kernel zero.

That statement is believable from the present ingredients. It matches:

- your finite-order W=0 sterility theorem,
- your compact support-overlap truncation lemma,
- your overlap-endpoint-to-directional-kernel-zero lemma,
- and the new unconditional adjacent pair-pair exclusion across a generic turning boundary.

But it does **not** give immediate adjacent 2-secancy.

So my math verdict is:

- the lemma you proposed is the wrong propagation lemma;
- the true propagation mechanism is “first entry into support at a turning boundary gives tangency / kernel zero,” not “first entry gives immediate 2-secancy on the next branch.”

That is the direction I would push now. It is also much more consistent with the current draft’s actual proven tools.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:32:17)

_Thought for 1m 21s_

If you can prove

\[
\text{“first entry into support at a turning boundary } \Longrightarrow \text{ tangency / directional-kernel zero,”}
\]

then what it gives you is **not** immediate global no-four-secant, but it gives you the missing bridge from a hypothetical non-adjacent pair-pair line to an **adjacent turning-boundary obstruction**.

That is exactly the kind of bridge the current draft still lacks. The draft’s own bottleneck language is still essentially “adjacent kernel sign regularity would imply global no-four-secant,” so a theorem that forces any first support entry to manifest as an adjacent turning-boundary tangency would be a real replacement for that missing adjacent step. turn87file2

Concretely, it would give this chain:

1. Assume a finite-slope line \(L_{m,b}\) is \(2\)-secant to two distinct ECT branches.

2. Choose such a pair with minimal index gap.

3. Move from left to right across the chain of branches and look at the fixed-slope support intervals
   \[
   \mathcal B_{I_j}(m)=\phi_m(I_j),\qquad \phi_m(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)-m v_5(h)}{c(h)}.
   \]

4. Since the line is not \(2\)-secant to the earlier branches, there is a **first boundary** where the intercept \(b\) enters the support picture.

5. Your finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) theorem plus the no-\(W\)-flatness result say this first entry cannot be explained by generic \(W=0\) local birth.

6. Therefore the first entry must occur at a **generic turning boundary**.

7. If your new theorem says that this first entry forces tangency / a zero of one directional kernel, then the current turning-boundary machinery becomes relevant right there.

8. If, in addition, you have the new unconditional adjacent pair-pair exclusion across generic turning boundaries, then that first-entry event is impossible.

9. Hence no such non-adjacent pair-pair line can exist.

So the payoff is:

**it would let you propagate a hypothetical global pair-pair obstruction down to an adjacent turning-boundary contradiction.**

That is the right shape of a replacement for the old `thm:partial-global-no-secant` route.

But there is one important subtlety:

It only works if the “first entry” theorem is strong enough to produce **adjacent turning-boundary tangency in a way that interacts with the new unconditional adjacent theorem**, not just some vague support contact. In practice, you want the output to be one of these:

- a genuine directional-kernel zero on an adjacent pair, or
- an actual adjacent pair-pair line across that turning boundary.

If you get only “support endpoint contact” with no way to upgrade it, that is not enough.

So the right corrected propagation theorem is not:

> “2-secancy persists into the next branch.”

That one is too strong and likely false.

It is instead:

> “For a minimal-gap pair-pair line, the first support entry occurs at an adjacent generic turning boundary and is realized by tangency / directional-kernel contact there.”

If you can prove **that**, then together with your current unconditional adjacent turning theorem, you really do get a plausible new global route.

So the short answer is:

**What it gives you is a reduction of any global pair-pair obstruction to an adjacent turning-boundary event.**  
That is exactly the missing bridge needed to replace the older conditional adjacent-kernel route.

\[
\text{first entry into support at a turning boundary } \Longrightarrow \text{ tangency / directional-kernel zero},
\]
then together with the new unconditional adjacent turning theorem, you may be able to replace the old partial-global no-secant route. In that sense, the new propagation theorem could close the **global geometric bridge** without needing any further \(W'\)-type work. That is real progress. turn87file2

But the second role is **the septic residual determinant itself**.

Your current \(W=0\) cleanup still depends on the order-7 package in a nontrivial way:

- the no-\(W\)-flatness theorem is stated under the hypothesis that the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically nonzero; 
- the draft still treats the full total septic fixed-sector pair \((u_7,v_7)\) as not yet fully explicit; turn87file7
- the current “state of the argument” remark still lists the full order-7 fixed-sector algebra among the remaining audited issues. 

So even if the new propagation theorem closes the **geometry**, it does **not** automatically close the **algebraic status of \(\Delta_7\)**.

That means the honest answer is:

### If your goal is only to replace the old global no-secant route
Then maybe **no**, you would not need more order-7 work *for that geometric step itself*.

### If your goal is a finished proof document
Then **yes**, you still likely need the order-7 total pair, unless you are happy to leave the paper saying something like:

> all the \(W=0\) reductions and no-\(W\)-flatness results are obtained under the current direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice nonvanishing hypothesis.

That is the key distinction.

Right now the paper’s septic layer is not yet fully self-contained. So my actual recommendation is:

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:36:04)

I’m testing the propagation theorem the same way we tested the earlier “persistence into the next branch” lemma: first I want to know if it’s actually true in the form we want. The main danger is that “first entry into support” might only give a single intersection, not the tangency/kernal event we need.

_Thought for 2m 36s_

I tried to push it, and I do **not** think this propagation theorem is true from the current ingredients. In fact, I think the natural version of it is probably **false**.

Here is the math reason.

For a fixed slope \(m\), each branch \(I_j\) has a support interval
\[
B_j(m)=\phi_m(I_j),\qquad \phi_m(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)-m v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\]
and, when \(m\) lies in the interior tangent-slope range of that branch, a \(2\)-secant intercept set
\[
S_j(m)\subset B_j(m),
\]
which is the open part of the support interval beyond the tangent intercept. A line \(y=mx+b\) is \(2\)-secant to branch \(I_j\) exactly when
\[
b\in S_j(m).
\]

Your new adjacent turning-boundary theorem gives:

- for adjacent branches across a generic turning boundary,
  \[
  S_j(m)\cap S_{j+1}(m)=\varnothing.
  \]

That is strong, but it does **not** imply any propagation law of the form

- “if \(b\in S_a(m)\cap S_b(m)\) with \(a<b\), then there is a first boundary where entry into support forces tangency/kernel.”

Why not? Because nothing you currently have forces the family of sets \(S_j(m)\) or \(B_j(m)\) to vary monotonically with \(j\).

A purely interval-theoretic countermodel already shows the problem.

Take three branches and one fixed slope \(m\), with
\[
S_1(m)=(0,2),\qquad S_2(m)=\varnothing,\qquad S_3(m)=(0,2).
\]
Then \(b=1\) gives a line that is \(2\)-secant to branches \(1\) and \(3\), but not to branch \(2\). There is no adjacent \(2+2\) pair.

More subtly, even if you track **support**, not \(2\)-secancy, you can have
\[
B_1(m)=(0,3),\qquad B_2(m)=(4,5),\qquad B_3(m)=(1,2),
\]
and take \(b=1.5\). Then support is absent on branch \(2\) and present again on branch \(3\), but the “first entry” into support at the \(2|3\) boundary does **not** come from tangency at the boundary. It just appears because the global range of \(\phi_m\) on branch \(3\) happens to contain \(1.5\).

That means the statement

> “first entry into support at a turning boundary gives tangency / kernel zero”

is too strong. First entry into support can happen because the **global** range on the new branch contains \(b\), without any local boundary tangency phenomenon.

And the same issue survives if you strengthen “support” to “2-secancy”: first entry into \(S_j(m)\) can still happen on branch \(j\) without being born at the adjacent boundary. It can just be a global feature of that branch.

So the obstacle is structural:

### What you have now
- local \(W=0\) sterility,
- local adjacent turning pair-pair exclusion,
- compact directional-kernel/tangency machinery.

### What you do **not** yet have
- any monotone propagation law for the intercept sets \(B_j(m)\) or \(S_j(m)\) as \(j\) varies,
- any theorem that says nonadjacent \(2\)-secancy must create an adjacent turning-boundary event.

And without one of those, the new propagation theorem does not go through.

So my honest conclusion is:

## The “new propagation theorem” is the wrong next lemma

At least in the form
\[
\text{nonadjacent pair-pair} \Longrightarrow \text{first support entry at a turning boundary gives tangency/kernel zero},
\]
I do not think it is justified, and I suspect it is false.

---

## What would make a propagation theorem true?

You would need some **global order structure** on the fixed-slope intercept sets as you move along the branch chain. For example, something like:

- monotonic ordering of the tangent intercept functions \(\tau_j(m)\) across adjacent branches, or
- discrete order-3 sign regularity of the determinant kernel \(D(s,t,u)\), or
- the old stronger adjacent support-ordering theorem.

That is exactly why the earlier determinant mean-value / discrete sign-regularity route was so attractive: it would give a real propagation mechanism. Without that, adjacent exclusion does not automatically globalize.

---

## So where does this leave us?

It leaves you with two distinct things:

1. You now have a strong **local adjacent theorem** at generic turning boundaries.
2. You still do **not** have a theorem that reduces **nonadjacent** pair-pair to the adjacent case.

So the right next attack is **not** this propagation lemma.

The right next attack is to prove some genuine global propagation principle, for fixed \(m\), for the support/intercept sets or for the determinant kernel \(D(s,t,u)\).

In plain terms: you still need something like the old support-ordering route or the determinant-sign route. The new adjacent theorem is powerful, but by itself it is not enough to leap to the full global no-four-secant statement.

So my recommendation is:

- **stop pushing this propagation lemma**;
- treat it as disproven / unjustified at the current level;
- pivot back to proving a real global propagation statement, most likely through the determinant kernel \(D(s,t,u)\) or a fixed-slope support-order theorem.

If you want, the next useful move is for me to help isolate the **weakest global propagation statement** that *would* be enough and still might be true.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:48:08)

_Thought for 7m 27s_

I pushed on it, and my honest conclusion is:

**the current local machinery is still not enough to produce a real global propagation theorem.**

More bluntly: the new \(W=0\) sterility and the new adjacent turning-boundary exclusion are both real, but they do **not** force a fixed line to “propagate” through the branch chain in any way strong enough to get global no-four-secant.

Here is the math diagnosis.

---

## 1. Why the support-order propagation still fails

Fix a slope \(m\). For each branch \(I_j\), define
\[
\phi_m(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)-m v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad B_j(m)=\phi_m(I_j).
\]
A line \(y=mx+b\) meets the branch iff
\[
b\in B_j(m),
\]
and it is \(2\)-secant iff \(b\) lies in the \(2\)-secant subset \(S_j(m)\subset B_j(m)\).

What we now know locally is:

- finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundaries are locally sterile;
- generic turning boundaries do not allow an **adjacent \(2+2\)** pair.

What we do **not** know is any global monotonicity of the sets \(B_j(m)\) or \(S_j(m)\) with respect to \(j\).

And without that, nonadjacent \(2+2\) can “skip” intermediate branches in principle. The interval-theoretic toy model
\[
S_1(m)=(0,2),\qquad S_2(m)=\varnothing,\qquad S_3(m)=(0,2)
\]
already shows what local adjacent exclusion does **not** give you. There is nothing in the current local theorems that rules out this kind of global reappearance.

So the hoped-for propagation statement
\[
\text{nonadjacent pair-pair} \Longrightarrow \text{adjacent turning-boundary event}
\]
is still not derivable from the present support-overlap results.

---

## 2. Why the “first entry into support” theorem is too strong

I also checked the candidate statement

\[
\text{first entry of }b\text{ into support at a turning boundary}
\Longrightarrow
\text{tangency / directional-kernel zero}.
\]

This is too strong. A fixed \(b\) can first belong to \(B_j(m)\) simply because the global range of \(\phi_m\) on branch \(j\) contains \(b\); that first entry need not be created locally at the boundary. So support can “appear” on a later branch without any boundary tangency phenomenon at all.

So that theorem is not the right propagation mechanism either.

---

## 3. Determinant-kernel propagation also stalls with current inputs

The other natural route is to work directly with
\[
D(s,t,u)=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u)),\qquad F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
\]
For fixed \(s,t\), the function
\[
u\mapsto D(s,t,u)
\]
is a scalar linear combination of \(c,v_5,\Delta_7\), so on each ECT interval it has at most two zeros.

But that still does **not** give a global propagation law. A scalar combination can have two zeros on one interval, none on the next, then two again later, unless you know some stronger sign-regularity or oscillation theorem across boundaries.

And that is exactly the thing we do **not** have yet.

---

## 4. So what *would* be a real global propagation statement?

At this point, I think the only plausible global bridge is a genuinely new theorem of one of these two forms:

### A. A fixed-slope support-order theorem
For each finite slope \(m\), the support intervals \(B_j(m)\) are globally ordered across the branch chain, so they cannot disappear and reappear.

This is conceptually enough, but right now it looks too strong to get from the present local ingredients alone.

### B. A determinant mean-value / total-positivity theorem
Something like:
\[
D(s,t,u)=\frac12\,W(\xi)\,(t-s)(u-s)(u-t)
\]
for some intermediate \(\xi\), or at least a positive-kernel representation of \(D\) in terms of \(W\).

If you had that, then the whole picture would change:

- on any interval where \(W\) keeps sign, \(D\) would have discrete order-3 sign regularity;
- finite-order \(W=0\) sterility would tell you zeros cannot be born there in bad ways;
- turning-boundary adjacent exclusion would then be enough to localize the only possible birth mechanism.

That really would globalize.

But this is a **new global theorem**, not something the current draft already implies.

---

## 5. My honest conclusion after trying this

So the result of the attempt is:

- the proposed propagation theorem does **not** fall out of the present local work;
- the local work is still valuable, but it has reduced the problem to a truly global statement;
- the next serious attack should be on a **new global determinant theorem**, not on another local boundary lemma.

If I had to choose the next frontier, it would be this:

> Try to prove an exact or integral mean-value formula for  
> \[
> D(s,t,u)=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))
> \]
> in terms of the projective Wronskian \(W\).

Because right now, without some theorem of that kind, the branch-to-branch propagation you want is just not available.

So: I did try the propagation direction, and my conclusion is that **we have reached the point where a genuinely new global statement is needed**. The local attacks have gone about as far as they can go on their own.

The exact next thing I would attack is the determinant mean-value theorem, not another support-overlap lemma.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:49:58)

I’m testing the determinant mean-value route as a theorem, not a slogan. The two things I want to settle are: whether there is an exact Cauchy/Peano-type formula for \(\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))\) in terms of \(W\), and, if not exact, whether there is at least a representation strong enough to force the sign behavior you need.

_Thought for 2m 6s_

Good news: the determinant mean-value theorem is **actually true**.

For the draft’s triple
\[
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),
\qquad
W(h)=\det(F(h),F'(h),F''(h)),
\]
which is exactly the projective-Wronskian setup already isolated in the draft/convo, turn89file7turn88file0 define
\[
D(s,t,u):=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u)).
\]

Then for any distinct \(s,t,u\) in an interval where \(F\in C^2\), there exists
\[
\xi\in (\min\{s,t,u\},\max\{s,t,u\})
\]
such that
\[
D(s,t,u)=\frac12\,W(\xi)\,(t-s)(u-s)(u-t).
\]

So the exact aggressive formula from convo4 is real. turn88file2turn88file8

## Why it is true

Take the comparison triple
\[
G(x)=\left(1,x,\frac{x^2}{2}\right).
\]
Then
\[
\det(G(s),G(t),G(u))=\frac12\,(t-s)(u-s)(u-t),
\]
and
\[
\det(G(x),G'(x),G''(x))=1.
\]

Now apply the generalized Cauchy mean-value theorem for \(3\times 3\) determinants (the order-3 analogue of the ordinary scalar Cauchy mean-value theorem) to the two triples \(F\) and \(G\). It gives some intermediate \(\xi\) such that
\[
\frac{\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))}{\det(G(s),G(t),G(u))}
=
\frac{\det(F(\xi),F'(\xi),F''(\xi))}{\det(G(\xi),G'(\xi),G''(\xi))}.
\]
Since the denominator on the right is \(1\), this becomes
\[
D(s,t,u)
=
\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))
=
\frac12\,W(\xi)\,(t-s)(u-s)(u-t).
\]

So the theorem is not special to the RH draft; it is a general order-3 determinant mean-value theorem for any \(C^2\) triple.

## What this gives immediately

This gives an exact intervalwise discrete sign law:

if \(W\) has fixed nonzero sign on the whole convex hull of \(\{s,t,u\}\), then
\[
\operatorname{sgn} D(s,t,u)
=
\operatorname{sgn} W \cdot \operatorname{sgn}\bigl((t-s)(u-s)(u-t)\bigr).
\]

In particular, if \(s<t<u\) all lie in one connected interval where \(W\neq 0\) and \(W\) keeps one sign, then
\[
D(s,t,u)\neq 0.
\]

So on such an interval, the triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is not just infinitesimally ECT; it is **discretely sign-regular of order 3**. That is exactly the stronger structure convo4 was hoping for. turn88file3turn88file6

This immediately kills:

- same-interval 3-collinearity,
- same-interval 3-secant lines,
- and therefore any same-interval 4-secant pathology.

So the theorem genuinely strengthens the current ECT proposition.

## What it does **not** yet give

It does **not** immediately solve the global problem.

The reason is that \(\xi\) lies somewhere between \(s,t,u\). If the ordered triple crosses a boundary where \(W\) changes sign, the formula alone does not tell you which side \(\xi\) lands on. So for triples spanning multiple \(W\)-sign intervals, the sign of \(D\) is still not controlled.

That means:

- the theorem solves the **same-sign-hull** case completely;
- the remaining issue is now sharply reduced to a **cross-boundary sign-control problem** for \(W(\xi)\).

This is exactly the “globalizing across adjacent boundaries” gap convo4 was pointing at. turn88file2turn88file8

## So what is the new frontier?

Now the natural next target is no longer “prove a mean-value theorem.” That part is done.

The next target is:

> For ordered triples crossing one generic boundary, control the sign of
> \[
> Q(s,t,u):=\frac{2D(s,t,u)}{(t-s)(u-s)(u-t)}=W(\xi_{s,t,u}).
> \]

In other words: can \(Q\) be continued with controlled sign across

- a finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary, or
- a generic turning boundary \(M=0,\ M'\neq 0,\ N\neq 0\)?

That is now the exact global problem.

So the attack has advanced in a real way:

- the determinant mean-value theorem is true;
- same-sign interval geometry is now fully rigid;
- the only remaining obstacle is **cross-boundary sign transport** of the divided-difference kernel.

That is a much cleaner frontier than where we were before.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 10:53:36)

I’m checking whether “cross-boundary sign transport” can actually be reduced to a local theorem at one boundary, or whether the mean-value point \(\xi\) makes that impossible. The critical question is whether \(Q(s,t,u)=2D/V\) can be shown continuous and one-signed as an ordered triple crosses a single generic boundary.

_Thought for 2m 44s_

Here is the math attack. It **works at generic turning boundaries**.

Let
\[
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),\qquad
D(h_1,h_2,h_3)=\det(F(h_1),F(h_2),F(h_3)).
\]

Write
\[
x(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad y(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Then
\[
D(h_1,h_2,h_3)
=
c(h_1)c(h_2)c(h_3)\,
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&x_1&y_1\\
1&x_2&y_2\\
1&x_3&y_3
\end{pmatrix},
\]
with \(x_i=x(h_i)\), \(y_i=y(h_i)\).

Now suppose \(h_b\) is a **generic turning boundary**:
\[
M(h_b)=0,\qquad M'(h_b)\neq 0,\qquad N(h_b)\neq 0.
\]

Then near \(h_b\),

- \(y'(h)=N(h)/c(h)^2\) is nonzero, so \(y\) is a valid local coordinate;
- in that coordinate, the normalized curve is
  \[
  x=\chi(y),
  \]
  with
  \[
  \chi'(y_b)=0,\qquad \chi''(y_b)\neq 0.
  \]
In fact,
\[
\chi''(y_b)=\frac{c(h_b)^2\,M'(h_b)}{N(h_b)^2},
\]
so \(\chi''\) has fixed nonzero sign on a small neighborhood.

That is the key local fact.

Now for any three nearby points \(h_1,h_2,h_3\), if we write \(y_i=y(h_i)\), then
\[
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&x_1&y_1\\
1&x_2&y_2\\
1&x_3&y_3
\end{pmatrix}
=
(y_2-y_1)(y_3-y_1)(y_3-y_2)\,\chi[y_1,y_2,y_3],
\]
where \(\chi[y_1,y_2,y_3]\) is the scalar second divided difference of \(\chi\).

By the ordinary scalar divided-difference mean-value theorem,
\[
\chi[y_1,y_2,y_3]=\frac12\,\chi''(\eta)
\]
for some \(\eta\) between the \(y_i\). Since \(\chi''\) has fixed nonzero sign near \(y_b\), the sign of \(\chi[y_1,y_2,y_3]\) is fixed.

Also \(c\) has fixed sign near \(h_b\), and \(y\) is monotone, so
\[
\operatorname{sgn}\bigl((y_2-y_1)(y_3-y_1)(y_3-y_2)\bigr)
\]
is just a fixed factor times
\[
\operatorname{sgn}\bigl((h_2-h_1)(h_3-h_1)(h_3-h_2)\bigr).
\]

So we get:

## Local turning-boundary sign transport theorem
There is a neighborhood \(U\) of a generic turning boundary \(h_b\) and a fixed sign \(\sigma_{\mathrm{turn}}\in\{\pm1\}\) such that for any distinct \(h_1,h_2,h_3\in U\),
\[
\operatorname{sgn} D(h_1,h_2,h_3)
=
\sigma_{\mathrm{turn}}\,
\operatorname{sgn}\bigl((h_2-h_1)(h_3-h_1)(h_3-h_2)\bigr).
\]

Equivalently: **across a generic turning boundary, the triple \(F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is locally discretely sign-regular of order 3.**

That is a real theorem.

What it gives immediately:

- no three nearby points across a generic turning boundary are collinear;
- no 3-secant or 4-secant line can be created locally there;
- the determinant kernel has the same kind of rigid sign behavior across a turning boundary that it has on a single \(W\)-sign interval.

So the “cross-boundary sign transport” problem is **solved locally for turning boundaries**.

Now the important caveat:

This does **not** solve the same problem across \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundaries. In that case the local model is an affine inflection, and we already saw the sign of the determinant can change across the boundary. So discrete sign-regularity is false there in general.

But that is okay, because the finite-order \(W=0\) theorem gives something different: **local sterility**. So the picture is now:

- on each interval where \(W\) keeps sign: exact determinant sign law by the mean-value theorem;
- across a generic turning boundary: local determinant sign law by the argument above;
- across a finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary: no sign law, but no local pair-pair birth.

That is the real output of the attack.

So the obstacle has shifted again.

The remaining hard part is no longer “can we transport the sign across a turning boundary?”  
We can.

The remaining hard part is:

**how do we globalize across a chain that may include both turning boundaries and \(W=0\) sterile boundaries?**

That is a more precise problem. It is not a purely local sign-transport problem anymore. It is a **global chain/continuation problem** combining:

- sign-regular blocks,
- turning-boundary sign-regular bridges,
- and \(W=0\) sterile transitions.

That is a much tighter frontier than before.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 11:00:55)

_Thought for 3m 26s_

Yes. The strongest attack here is to replace the mean-value point \(\xi\) by an **exact positive-kernel formula**.

That is the real upgrade.

Let
\[
D(s,t,u):=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u)),\qquad F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7),
\]
and
\[
W(h):=\det(F(h),F'(h),F''(h)).
\]

For ordered \(s<t<u\), define
\[
Q(s,t,u):=\frac{D(s,t,u)}{(t-s)(u-s)(u-t)}.
\]

Then the exact formula is

\[
Q(s,t,u)=\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,W(x)\,dx,
\]
where
\[
M_{s,t,u}(x)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x-s}{(t-s)(u-s)}, & s\le x\le t,\\[1.2ex]
\dfrac{u-x}{(u-t)(u-s)}, & t\le x\le u,\\[1.2ex]
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

This kernel is nonnegative and strictly positive on \((s,u)\setminus\{t\}\), and
\[
\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,dx=\frac12.
\]

So \(Q(s,t,u)\) is not just “\(W(\xi)/2\)” for some unknown \(\xi\); it is a **positive average of \(W\)** over \([s,u]\).

That is the right global object.

## Why this is true

This is the order-3 Hermite–Genocchi / Peano kernel formula.

For the scalar divided difference of a \(C^2\) function \(f\),
\[
f[s,t,u]=\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,f''(x)\,dx.
\]

Now
\[
\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))
\]
is multilinear in the columns, and after dividing by the Vandermonde
\[
(t-s)(u-s)(u-t),
\]
it is exactly the order-2 divided-difference determinant attached to the triple \(F\). The corresponding second derivative object is the Wronskian \(W\). That gives the formula above.

You can also check normalization on
\[
G(x)=\left(1,x,\frac{x^2}{2}\right):
\]
here \(W\equiv 1\), and the formula becomes
\[
Q_G(s,t,u)=\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,dx=\frac12,
\]
which is correct.

## What this buys you

This is better than the pointwise mean-value theorem because it turns the global question into a **variation-diminishing question for a positive kernel transform of \(W\)**.

The immediate consequences are:

1. If \(W\) has fixed sign on \([s,u]\), then \(Q(s,t,u)\) has that sign.  
   This recovers the same-sign interval sign law.

2. Across a generic turning boundary, since \(W\) stays nonzero and locally one-signed, you get the same local sign transport as before.

3. At a finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundary, \(Q\) is now seen as a weighted average of a sign-changing function \(W\) with a positive triangular kernel. So the local one-zero/no-zero behavior you already proved is the local manifestation of this averaging formula.

So the chain problem is now sharply reduced to:

> How many sign changes can the transform
> \[
> u\mapsto Q(s,t,u)=\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,W(x)\,dx
> \]
> have, in terms of the sign changes of \(W\)?

That is the right global propagation question.

## What I think is the next real theorem

The theorem to try now is **not** another boundary-local statement. It is:

> For fixed \(s<t\), the map \(u\mapsto Q(s,t,u)\) is variation-diminishing with respect to the sign changes of \(W\).

In other words, if \(W\) has only \(k\) sign changes on \([t,\infty)\), then \(u\mapsto Q(s,t,u)\) has at most \(k\) sign changes on \((t,\infty)\).

This is plausible because \(M_{s,t,u}(x)\) is a positive B-spline kernel, and those are classically variation diminishing.

If you could prove even a weak version of that, you would have a genuine global propagation statement.

## The honest limitation

I do **not** think the current local theorems alone finish this.

What we have now is:

- local \(W=0\) sterility,
- local turning-boundary sign transport,
- unconditional adjacent turning pair-pair exclusion.

What we still do **not** have is a theorem that says the positive-kernel transform of \(W\) cannot lose and regain sign in a way that produces nonadjacent pair-pair.

So the attack has succeeded in one important way:

**the global problem is now no longer mysterious.**  
It is a concrete positive-kernel / variation-diminishing problem for \(W\).

That is the right next frontier.

If you want, the next thing I would do is push directly on this statement:

> For fixed \(s<t\), prove that \(u\mapsto Q(s,t,u)\) is a B-spline transform of \(W\) and therefore variation diminishing.

That is now the mathematically cleanest global route.
## User (2026-04-19 11:02:18)

ok push on that statement

## Assistant (2026-04-19 11:02:22)

I’m checking whether the “variation diminishing” step is actually true, not just aesthetically plausible. The danger is that although \(Q(s,t,u)\) is a positive average of \(W\), the kernel itself depends on \(u\), so standard fixed-kernel arguments do not automatically apply.

_Thought for 3m 27s_

I pushed on it, and here is the honest outcome:

The **exact positive-kernel formula is real**, but the **naive variation-diminishing conclusion is not enough** and, in the strong form you want, is basically false.

The clean way to see it is this.

For ordered \(s<t<u\), define
\[
Q(s,t,u):=\frac{D(s,t,u)}{(t-s)(u-s)(u-t)},
\qquad
D(s,t,u)=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u)).
\]

For a \(C^2\) triple \(F\), the right exact formula is
\[
Q(s,t,u)=\int_s^u M_{s,t,u}(x)\,W(x)\,dx,
\]
where
\[
W(x)=\det(F(x),F'(x),F''(x)),
\]
and
\[
M_{s,t,u}(x)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{x-s}{(t-s)(u-s)}, & s\le x\le t,\\[1.2ex]
\dfrac{u-x}{(u-t)(u-s)}, & t\le x\le u,\\[1.2ex]
0, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
So \(Q\) really is a positive average of \(W\).

That part is good.

Now fix \(s<t\) and think of \(u\mapsto Q(u):=Q(s,t,u)\). Define
\[
R(u):=(u-s)(u-t)Q(u)=D(s,t,u)/(t-s).
\]
Then from the kernel formula one gets
\[
R(u)=\frac{u-t}{t-s}\int_s^t (x-s)W(x)\,dx+\int_t^u (u-x)W(x)\,dx,
\]
hence
\[
R''(u)=W(u),
\qquad
R(t)=0,
\qquad
R'(t)=\frac1{t-s}\int_s^t (x-s)W(x)\,dx.
\]

So the global problem is equivalent to studying solutions of
\[
R''=W
\]
with those initial data.

That is the right reformulation.

But now comes the important point:

A positive-kernel formula by itself does **not** force the kind of one-way sign transport you want.

Here is a concrete counterexample in the scalar graph model \(F(x)=(1,x,f(x))\), for which \(W=f''\).

Take \(s=0\), \(t=1\), and let
\[
f''(x)=
\begin{cases}
1, & 0\le x\le 1,\\
-4, & 1\le x\le 2,\\
10, & x\ge 2.
\end{cases}
\]
Then \(R''=f''\), \(R(1)=0\), and
\[
R'(1)=\int_0^1 x\,dx=\frac12.
\]

So on \(1<u<2\),
\[
R(u)=\frac12(u-1)-2(u-1)^2
      =(u-1)\Bigl(\frac12-2(u-1)\Bigr),
\]
which is positive near \(u=1\) and crosses zero at
\[
u=1+\frac14=\frac54.
\]

At \(u=2\),
\[
R(2)=-\frac32,
\qquad
R'(2)=-\frac72.
\]
For \(u>2\),
\[
R(u)=-\frac32-\frac72(u-2)+5(u-2)^2,
\]
which crosses zero again at
\[
u=3.
\]

So \(Q(u)\) is:

- positive just to the right of \(u=1\),
- negative for \(u\) between \(5/4\) and \(3\),
- positive again for \(u>3\).

In other words, the sign of \(Q\) can **disappear and then reappear**.

So the hoped-for statement

> “\(Q\) is a positive-kernel transform of \(W\), therefore sign transport is monotone enough to globalize the proof”

is not true in the form we need.

The kernel is positive, but it depends on \(u\), and the induced family is not automatically giving the strong variation-diminishing property that would force global no-four-secant.

So the result of the attack is:

- the Peano / Hermite–Genocchi kernel formula is correct and useful;
- it gives the exact ODE-type reformulation \(R''=W\);
- but the generic “B-spline transform \(\Rightarrow\) enough variation control” route does **not** close the global theorem.

That means the determinant-kernel route still needs some **extra structure special to your triple**, beyond positivity of the kernel.

The natural possibilities are:

- a stronger theorem about the sign pattern of \(W\) itself,
- a special oscillation/disconjugacy property of the third-order system behind \(F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\),
- or a genuinely stronger support-order / total-positivity statement for the branch chain.

So the attack did not fail completely — it clarified the exact barrier:

## What we now know
The global problem is equivalent to controlling the sign of solutions of
\[
R''=W
\]
with the specific initial data above.

## What we do not yet have
A theorem showing those solutions cannot lose and regain sign in the way needed for nonadjacent pair-pair configurations.

So the next serious frontier is not “prove the kernel formula” — that is done.

The next serious frontier is:

> exploit **special structure of \(W\)** from the actual RH draft, not just generic positivity of the Peano kernel.

Without that extra structure, this route stalls.
## User (2026-04-19 11:42:23)

## Assistant (2026-04-19 11:42:28)

I’m checking the current draft for the exact identities and new order-7 closure first, because the right “special structure of \(W\)” attack depends on what is now genuinely proved about \(\Delta_7\), \(W\), and the normalized curve. Then I’ll try to extract the strongest non-generic property of \(W\) that could actually drive the global argument.

_Thought for 6m 58s_

I think there **is** a real special structure of \(W\) in the current draft, and it is much stronger than “\(Q(s,t,u)\) is a positive average of \(W\).”

The key new fact is that your order-7 quotient closure is now effectively in place at the canonical level: the draft now has
\[
\Delta_7=\Delta_{7,K_1},
\]
and says no further order-7 local invariant is missing for the later geometric arguments. So \(\Delta_7\) is now the right canonical septic object to build geometry from, not just a provisional detector. See the current draft here: [proof_of_rh(92).tex](sandbox:/mnt/data/proof_of_rh(92).tex).

That lets you do something much cleaner than the old \((\rho_1,\rho_2)\) picture.

---

## 1. The right coordinates are not \((\rho_1,\rho_2)\), but \((\mu,\lambda)\)

On a region where
\[
v_5\neq 0,\qquad M\neq 0,
\]
define
\[
\mu:=\frac{c}{v_5}=\frac{1}{\rho_1},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}=\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}.
\]

These are the natural projective coordinates attached to the triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).

The reason they are special is that a line in the original affine chart
\[
\rho_2=m\,\rho_1+b
\]
becomes
\[
\lambda=m+b\,\mu.
\]

So in the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-plane, the secant problem becomes an **ordinary affine-line intersection problem**.

That is already a big structural simplification. The RH draft is not giving you an arbitrary planar curve. It is giving you a curve whose line geometry becomes linear after dividing by \(v_5\).

---

## 2. \(W\) is exactly the curvature of \(\lambda\) as a function of \(\mu\)

This is the real special structure.

You already have
\[
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,
\qquad
W=\det(F,F',F''),
\qquad
F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
\]

Since
\[
\mu=\frac{c}{v_5},
\]
we get
\[
\mu'=-\frac{M}{v_5^2}.
\]

Since
\[
\Delta_7=\lambda v_5,
\]
you can substitute this into the exact identity for \(W\). After a straightforward simplification, the \(\lambda\)-terms cancel and you get

\[
W
=
v_5\,M'\,\lambda'
-
M\bigl(v_5\lambda''+2v_5'\lambda'\bigr).
\]

Equivalently,
\[
W
=
-\frac{M^2}{v_5}\left(\frac{v_5^2}{M}\lambda'\right)'.
\]

And the cleanest form is the geometric one:
\[
\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2}
=
-\frac{v_5^3}{M^3}\,W.
\]

That is the real identity to exploit.

So \(W\) is not just some arbitrary projective Wronskian floating around. On \(v_5\neq 0,\ M\neq 0\), it is exactly the second derivative of \(\lambda\) with respect to \(\mu\), up to a nowhere-zero factor.

This means:

- \(W=0\) iff \(\lambda\) is affine in \(\mu\),
- \(\operatorname{sgn} W\) controls convexity/concavity of the graph \(\lambda=\lambda(\mu)\),
- the no-\(W\)-flatness theorem is just the statement that \(\lambda\) cannot be affine on an open interval,
- finite-order \(W=0\) is just an ordinary finite-order inflection of the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\).

That is much more rigid than the Peano-kernel viewpoint.

---

## 3. This recovers the earlier \(W=0\) results in a more conceptual way

Your existing local theorem for finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundaries said that such a boundary is locally sterile for pair-pair birth.

In \((\mu,\lambda)\)-coordinates, that theorem becomes almost tautological:

- when \(W\) has a finite zero of order \(r\), \(\lambda''(\mu)\) has a finite zero of order \(r\),
- so the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\) has an ordinary even/odd-order inflection,
- and the scalar divided-difference analysis for a graph over \(\mu\) gives exactly the no-zero / at-most-one-zero statement you already proved.

So the special structure of \(W\) is already unifying the local \(W=0\) story.

Likewise, the no-\(W\)-flatness theorem becomes clearer:

if \(W\equiv 0\), then \(\lambda\) is affine in \(\mu\), say
\[
\lambda=A+B\mu.
\]
Multiplying by \(v_5\) gives
\[
\Delta_7=A\,v_5+B\,c,
\]
which is exactly the affine dependence already ruled out in the draft.

So the \(\lambda\)-line is not just a side remark. It is the **correct projective coordinate system** for understanding \(W\).

---

## 4. The determinant \(D(s,t,u)\) also simplifies in these coordinates

Because
\[
(c,v_5,\Delta_7)=v_5(\mu,1,\lambda),
\]
you get
\[
D(s,t,u)
=
-v_5(s)v_5(t)v_5(u)\,
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1&\mu(s)&\lambda(s)\\
1&\mu(t)&\lambda(t)\\
1&\mu(u)&\lambda(u)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

So after factoring out the nonzero \(v_5\)-product, \(D\) is just the ordinary 3-point determinant of the graph \(\lambda=\lambda(\mu)\).

Then
\[
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1&\mu_1&\lambda_1\\
1&\mu_2&\lambda_2\\
1&\mu_3&\lambda_3
\end{pmatrix}
=
(\mu_2-\mu_1)(\mu_3-\mu_1)(\mu_3-\mu_2)\,\lambda[\mu_1,\mu_2,\mu_3].
\]

And the scalar mean-value theorem gives
\[
\lambda[\mu_1,\mu_2,\mu_3]=\frac12\,\lambda''(\eta)
\]
for some intermediate \(\eta\).

So the determinant sign problem is not a mysterious 3-vector problem anymore. It is just the sign of the second divided difference of the scalar function \(\lambda(\mu)\).

That is the special structure you wanted.

---

## 5. What this suggests as the next real theorem

The obvious next target is no longer a theorem about \(W\) directly. It is a theorem about the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\).

A plausible statement is:

> On each connected interval where \(v_5\neq 0\) and \(M\neq 0\), the normalized graph \(\lambda=\lambda(\mu)\) is strictly convex or strictly concave whenever \(W\) has fixed sign.

That part is basically already proved by the identity above.

The harder global question then becomes:

> How do these convex/concave graph pieces glue across turning boundaries \(M=0\)?

That is where the turning-boundary theorems you already proved come in. In the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-picture, a turning boundary is exactly where \(\mu'=0\), so the \(\mu\)-parameter breaks. That means the global obstruction is now very sharply localized:

- away from \(M=0\), the problem is ordinary graph convexity in \((\mu,\lambda)\),
- at \(M=0\), the graph coordinate degenerates, and that is the only place where global propagation can really fail.

That is a much better reduction than the generic Peano-kernel view.

---

## 6. So what should we attack next?

The best next theorem to try is this:

> **Turning-boundary compatibility of the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-graph pieces.**  
> Show that if a finite-slope affine line in the original \((\rho_1,\rho_2)\)-chart meets two nonadjacent graph pieces \(\lambda=\lambda(\mu)\) twice each, then some adjacent turning-boundary pair already produces the same obstruction.

In plain language: convert the global problem into a statement about piecewise convex/concave graph pieces in the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-plane, joined at turning points where \(\mu\) has an extremum.

That is now the most natural form of the remaining geometry.

---

## 7. One more practical note

Your current draft still contains at least one stale sentence in the \(\lambda\)-remark saying “what remains open is the lower/mixed septic sectors to the full total pair \((u_7,v_7)\).” After the quotient-septic closure theorem, that is no longer the right way to phrase the state of \(\Delta_7\). What remains gauge-dependent is only the raw representative of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\), not the canonical quotient class or \(\Delta_7\) itself.

So the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-reformulation is even more justified now than that remark suggests.

---

My bottom line:

The special structure of \(W\) is this:

\[
\boxed{
\mu=\frac{c}{v_5},\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},\qquad
\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2}
=
-\frac{v_5^3}{M^3}W
}
\]

That is the real projective geometry hidden in the RH draft. It is much more informative than the generic positive-kernel formula, and I think it is the right coordinate system for the next phase.

If you want, the next thing I’d do is try to prove the determinant formula and local theorems directly in \((\mu,\lambda)\)-coordinates and then restate the remaining global gap in that language.

I’m switching the attack into the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-coordinates now, because that is where the geometry looks most rigid. The first job is to derive the exact identities cleanly, then see what they actually imply globally rather than just locally.

_Thought for 4m 46s_

I pushed on the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-route, and there is a **real exact structure** there.

Assume we are on a region where
\[
v_5\neq 0,\qquad M\neq 0.
\]
Then define
\[
\mu:=\frac{c}{v_5},\qquad \lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}.
\]
This is the right projective coordinate system for the triple
\[
F=(c,v_5,\Delta_7),
\]
because
\[
F=v_5\,(\mu,1,\lambda).
\]

That immediately turns the affine-line geometry into ordinary line geometry in the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-plane. A line in the old \((\rho_1,\rho_2)\)-chart,
\[
\rho_2=m\rho_1+b,
\qquad
\rho_1=\frac{v_5}{c},
\quad
\rho_2=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
becomes
\[
\lambda=m+b\,\mu.
\]
So the original slope \(m\) becomes the intercept in \((\mu,\lambda)\), and the original intercept \(b\) becomes the slope.

That part is already useful.

The stronger thing is that \(W\) becomes exactly the curvature of \(\lambda\) as a function of \(\mu\).

Write \(v:=v_5\). Since
\[
\mu=\frac{c}{v},\qquad \lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v},
\]
we have
\[
\mu'=\frac{c'v-cv'}{v^2}=-\frac{M}{v^2}.
\]
Also
\[
F=v(\mu,1,\lambda),
\]
so
\[
W=\det(F,F',F'')=v^3\det(G,G',G''),
\qquad
G:=(\mu,1,\lambda).
\]
A direct determinant computation gives
\[
\det(G,G',G'')=\mu''\lambda'-\mu'\lambda''.
\]
But for a graph \(\lambda=\lambda(\mu)\),
\[
\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2}
=
\frac{\mu'\lambda''-\lambda'\mu''}{(\mu')^3}.
\]
Combining these,
\[
W
=
-v^3(\mu')^3\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2}.
\]
Since \(\mu'=-M/v^2\), this simplifies to
\[
W=\frac{M^3}{v^3}\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2},
\]
hence the exact identity
\[
\boxed{\frac{d^2\lambda}{d\mu^2}=\frac{v_5^3}{M^3}\,W.}
\]

That is the special structure.

It means:

- \(W=0\) iff \(\lambda\) is affine in \(\mu\);
- on any component where \(v_5\neq 0\), \(M\neq 0\), and \(W\) has fixed sign, the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\) is strictly convex or strictly concave;
- finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\) boundaries are just ordinary finite-order inflections of the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\);
- the no-\(W\)-flatness theorem becomes exactly: \(\lambda\) cannot be affine on an open interval, because that would force
  \[
  \Delta_7=A\,v_5+B\,c.
  \]

So your earlier \(W=0\) analysis becomes much more conceptual in these coordinates.

The determinant also simplifies exactly. Since
\[
F_i=v_i(\mu_i,1,\lambda_i),
\]
for three points \(s,t,u\),
\[
D(s,t,u)=\det(F(s),F(t),F(u))
=
-v_s v_t v_u\,
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&\mu_s&\lambda_s\\
1&\mu_t&\lambda_t\\
1&\mu_u&\lambda_u
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D(s,t,u)
=
-v_s v_t v_u\,(\mu_t-\mu_s)(\mu_u-\mu_s)(\mu_u-\mu_t)\,
\lambda[\mu_s,\mu_t,\mu_u],
\]
where \(\lambda[\mu_s,\mu_t,\mu_u]\) is the scalar second divided difference of the single function \(\lambda(\mu)\).

So the whole \(D\)-problem is no longer a mysterious 3-vector problem. It is exactly the divided-difference geometry of the graph \(\lambda(\mu)\).

The tangent kernel simplifies too. For
\[
K_{21}(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t)),
\]
a direct computation gives
\[
K_{21}(s,t)
=
v(s)^2v(t)\Bigl(\lambda'(s)(\mu(t)-\mu(s))-\mu'(s)(\lambda(t)-\lambda(s))\Bigr).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
K_{21}(s,t)
=
v(s)^2v(t)\,\mu'(s)\,(\mu(t)-\mu(s))
\left(
\frac{d\lambda}{d\mu}(s)-\frac{\lambda(t)-\lambda(s)}{\mu(t)-\mu(s)}
\right).
\]

So \(K_{21}=0\) is exactly the classical statement:

> the secant slope from \(s\) to \(t\) in the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-graph equals the tangent slope at \(s\).

And similarly \(K_{12}=0\) means the secant slope equals the tangent slope at \(t\).

This is a very clean reinterpretation of the turning-boundary kernel geometry.

Now, what does this buy globally?

It gives a complete conceptual description of the local pieces:

- on \(W\)-sign pieces: \(\lambda(\mu)\) is strictly convex/concave;
- at finite-order \(W=0,\ M\neq 0\): \(\lambda(\mu)\) has an ordinary inflection;
- at turning boundaries \(M=0\): \(\mu'=0\), so the \(\mu\)-projection has a local extremum and the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-curve has a vertical tangent.

So the global geometry becomes:

> a chain of graph pieces \(\lambda(\mu)\), each strictly convex or concave, joined across vertical-tangent turning points and separated by ordinary inflections.

That is much stronger and much more geometric than the Peano-kernel viewpoint.

But I do **not** think it closes the proof by itself.

The remaining problem is now very sharply stated:

a fixed affine line in the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-plane,
\[
\lambda=m+b\mu,
\]
can intersect one convex/concave graph piece at most twice, and the local theorems control what happens near inflections and turning points. But nothing we have yet prevents the same line from meeting one graph piece twice, missing some intermediate pieces, and then meeting a later piece twice. So the nonadjacent propagation problem is still real.

In other words, the \((\mu,\lambda)\)-reformulation does not yet produce the missing global theorem. What it does produce is the **right reduced global question**:

> For the piecewise graph \(\lambda(\mu)\) built from the RH draft, can one prove a global line-incidence theorem beyond ordinary local convexity?

That is now the exact frontier.

The strongest next thing to try is not another local boundary lemma. It is a global statement about these graph pieces. The most plausible target is:

- either a global ordering theorem for tangent slope ranges of the graph pieces in \((\mu,\lambda)\),
- or a global support-order theorem for the lines \(\lambda=m+b\mu\),
- or a total-positivity / variation-diminishing statement special to the actual \(\lambda(\mu)\) coming from \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).

So the attack succeeded in one important way:

it turned \(W\) from a generic determinant into the exact graph curvature of \(\lambda\) over \(\mu\), and it turned the tangent-separator kernels into ordinary secant-versus-tangent comparisons on that graph.

That is real special structure.

It also makes the remaining gap cleaner:

the missing theorem is now a **global line-incidence theorem for a piecewise convex/concave graph with vertical-tangent seams**, not a generic kernel-positivity problem.

If you want, the next thing I would do is attack the most plausible global statement in this language: whether the tangent-slope intervals of successive \((\mu,\lambda)\)-graph pieces are globally ordered or nested in a way that would rule out nonadjacent \(2+2\) intersections.
