_Thought for 2m 47s_

Yes. I pushed on exactly that question, and the clean conclusion is:

**at the current defect-free lifted level, there is no extra algebraic condition beyond coplanarity.**

That is the real \(4\)-point attack result.

On a good \(v_5\)-patch, the lifted coordinates are
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
y=\frac{u_5}{c},\qquad
\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5},
\qquad
Q_v=(y,x,\sigma_v).
\]
The current defect-free order-\(\le 7\) theorem says that a bad \(4\)-point core becomes an affine dependence of the four lifted points \(Q_v(h_j)\), i.e. coplanarity in \(\mathbf C^3\). In the current formulation, there is no second independent scalar equation left over after passing to \(Q_v\); the bad-core condition has already been compressed into that affine circuit statement. turn187file0

More concretely, if
\[
\sum_{j=1}^4 b_j=0,\qquad
\sum_{j=1}^4 b_j\,y_j=0,\qquad
\sum_{j=1}^4 b_j\,x_j=0,\qquad
\sum_{j=1}^4 b_j\,\sigma_{v,j}=0,
\]
with
\[
(y_j,x_j,\sigma_{v,j})=Q_v(h_j),
\]
then on a good patch \(c(h_j)\neq 0\), so setting
\[
a_j:=\frac{b_j}{c(h_j)}
\]
recovers the corresponding defect-free finite-core coefficients at the lifted order-\(7\) level. Thus, as far as the current defect-free lifted formulation goes, **coplanarity already is the whole condition**. There is no additional compatibility equation hiding inside the same package. turn187file0

That means the nonmixed odd-order local exact branch sheet is not something you can hope to kill by saying “coplanarity is not enough for a true bad \(4\)-point core.” At the present lifted order-\(7\) level, coplanarity is exactly what the bad \(4\)-point core becomes. So on that route, the unique nonmixed odd-order branch sheet is a **real candidate**, not a fake one. The same is true a fortiori for the mixed normal form: once the generalized-Vandermonde branch exists, the defect-free lifted theory does not add another scalar obstruction on top of it. 

So the attack changes direction:

the missing “extra algebraic condition beyond coplanarity” will **not** come from further squeezing the same defect-free lifted \(Q_v\)-equations. It can only come from going one layer higher, exactly as the other chat found for the \(2\)-point branch: a **source-level multi-body interaction remainder**. 

For \(4\) points, the right object would be a raw corrected source-level map
\[
\mathfrak Q\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^4 a_j\delta_{h_j}\right)
\]
and then the pure \(4\)-body remainder obtained by inclusion–exclusion:
\[
\mathcal I_4
=
\mathcal F_4
-\sum \mathcal F_3
+\sum \mathcal F_2
-\sum \mathcal F_1.
\]
If that source-level object exists and is symmetric, vanishes when any amplitude is zero, and collapses on every partial diagonal \(h_i=h_j\), then that would impose a genuinely new algebraic divisibility condition — something like a factor of
\[
\prod_{j=1}^4 a_j
\quad\text{and}\quad
\prod_{1\le i<j\le 4}(h_i-h_j)^2
\]
on the pure \(4\)-body remainder. **That** would be an extra algebraic condition beyond coplanarity. But the current paper does not yet have that source-level \(4\)-body object. 

So the honest state of the \(4\)-point attack is:

- the defect-free lifted order-\(7\) geometry reduces true bad \(4\)-point cores to coplanarity of \(Q_v\);
- therefore there is no extra algebraic condition available at that same level;
- the nonmixed odd-order local exact branch sheet remains genuinely viable there;
- and any stronger obstruction must come from a new source-level pure \(4\)-body interaction theorem, not from more manipulation of the current coplanarity condition. turn187file0

So the clean next attack on \(4\)-pair is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{define and study the pure source-level }4\text{-body interaction remainder } \mathcal I_4.
}
\]

That is the first place an “extra condition beyond coplanarity” can actually appear.

_Thought for 2m 35s_

Yes. There is a real \(4\)-point attack now, and it gives a useful answer.

The clean source-level analogue of the \(2\)-point strategy is this:

Assume there is a raw corrected quotient-germ map on finite atomic sources, and define the **pure \(4\)-body remainder** by inclusion–exclusion:
\[
\mathcal I_4
:=
\mathcal F_4
-\sum \mathcal F_3
+\sum \mathcal F_2
-\sum \mathcal F_1.
\]
If this source-level object is symmetric in the four labeled atoms, vanishes when any amplitude is zero, and collapses on every partial diagonal \(h_i=h_j\), then the same midpoint/separation logic as in the \(2\)-point branch forces a strong factorization:
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I_4
=
\Bigl(\prod_{j=1}^4 a_j\Bigr)
\Bigl(\prod_{1\le i<j\le 4}(h_i-h_j)^2\Bigr)\,
\mathcal J_4
}
\]
for some analytic \(\mathcal J_4\). This is the natural \(4\)-point analogue of the quadratic-remainder theorem the other chat isolated for the \(2\)-point branch. 

That gives the extra algebraic condition you asked for: a true bad \(4\)-point core would not merely lie on a coplanarity branch of the lifted curve \(Q_v\); it would have to lie on such a branch while also making a source-level \(4\)-body remainder vanish to **discriminant-squared order**.

So the right test is:

> does this extra \(4\)-body condition actually kill the local dangerous \(4\)-point branches?

I attacked that on both local model types.

---

## 1. Test on the nonmixed odd-order branch sheet

Near a nonmixed odd-order \(T_v\)-zero, we already reduced the local exact branch sheet to the normalized model
\[
Q_v(x)=(x^n,x,x^2),
\qquad n \ \text{even},
\]
with the exact opposite-side branch parameterized on the scale
\[
a=-t,\qquad b=-(1-\rho)t,\qquad u=pt,\qquad v=q(t,\rho,p)t,
\]
for small \(t>0\). The full exact lifted \(4\)-point coplanarity determinant scales as
\[
\Delta_4 \sim t^{\,n+3}.
\]
This is the model order we already identified when classifying the nonmixed odd-order sheet. 

Now on that same cone, every pair difference is \(O(t)\), so the discriminant-squared factor in the hypothetical source-level remainder scales like
\[
\prod_{i<j}(h_i-h_j)^2 \sim t^{12}.
\]
Therefore the pure \(4\)-body correction scales like
\[
\mathcal I_4 = O(t^{12})
\]
up to the amplitude factor.

So the comparison is:

- geometric coplanarity branch term: \(t^{\,n+3}\),
- source-level pure \(4\)-body correction: \(t^{12}\).

Hence:

### If \(n+3<12\), i.e. \(n\le 8\),
the pure \(4\)-body correction is **higher order** than the branch equation.

That means it cannot kill the local branch; it only perturbs it. Since the odd-order nonmixed branch sheet already has a nondegenerate implicit-function parameterization, the sheet persists under such a higher-order perturbation.

So the low-order dangerous nonmixed cases
\[
n=4,\ 6,\ 8
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\ord T_v = 1,\ 3,\ 5
\]
are **robust** against any extra \(4\)-body obstruction of discriminant-squared type.

Only when
\[
n\ge 10
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\ord T_v\ge 7
\]
could the extra \(4\)-body term enter at equal or lower order and potentially matter.

This is already a strong conclusion: the source-level \(4\)-body correction, even if it exists exactly as hoped, cannot eliminate the low-order nonmixed odd branch sheets.

---

## 2. Test on the mixed normal form

At a mixed point \(K_v=T_v=0\), the normalized model is
\[
Q_v(x)\sim (x^n,\ x,\ x^m),
\qquad m\ge 3,\quad n>m,
\]
and the leading \(4\)-point coplanarity determinant is the generalized Vandermonde for exponents
\[
0,\ 1,\ m,\ n.
\]
Its total scaling order on a shrinking opposite-side quadruple is
\[
t^{\,m+n+1},
\]
because the Vandermonde contributes \(t^6\) and the Schur factor contributes degree \(m+n-5\). This is exactly the mixed order count already implicit in the other chat’s monomial analysis. 

Again, the source-level pure \(4\)-body correction contributes order
\[
t^{12}.
\]

So:

### If \(m+n+1<12\), i.e. \(m+n\le 10\),
the discriminant-squared \(4\)-body correction is again **higher order** than the mixed coplanarity equation.

Therefore it cannot remove those mixed dangerous local branches either; it only deforms them.

So all low-order mixed dangerous patterns with
\[
m+n\le 10
\]
are also robust against this kind of extra algebraic condition.

Only in the high-order mixed regime
\[
m+n\ge 11
\]
could the source-level \(4\)-body remainder enter at competitive order.

---

## 3. What this means

This is the real attack result:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Even if a pure source-level \(4\)-body remainder exists with discriminant-squared factorization, it cannot kill the low-order dangerous \(4\)-point branches.}
}
\]

So the extra algebraic condition you wanted **does exist in principle**, but after testing it on the actual local models, it turns out to be too high-order to eliminate the low-order dangerous branches.

That means:

- it is still worth formulating the pure \(4\)-body remainder theorem, because it is the correct source-level structure;
- but it will only matter in sufficiently high-order local regimes;
- the genuinely dangerous low-order nonmixed odd sheets and low-order mixed sheets must be ruled out, if at all, by **RH-specific jet restrictions**, not by generic discriminant-squared \(4\)-body algebra.

---

## 4. The sharpened roadmap for \(4\)-pair

So the \(4\)-point attack now splits very cleanly.

First, it is still worth trying to formalize the source-level \(4\)-body remainder theorem, because it is the right structural statement.

But second, the test above shows that the decisive local question is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can the actual zeta geometry realize the low-order dangerous nonmixed odd patterns }n=4,6,8,
\text{ or low-order dangerous mixed patterns with }m+n\le 10?
}
\]

That is the real next frontier.

So my honest conclusion is:

- the extra \(4\)-body compatibility condition is conceptually real;
- but on the low-order dangerous local branches, it is too weak to kill them;
- therefore the \(4\)-point branch will now stand or fall on **RH-specific local jet restrictions**, not on generic higher-body divisibility alone.

S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5},

defined where \(K_v=W_x(1,x,S)\neq 0\). Since
\[
T_v=W_x(1,Y,x,S)
\]
with
\[
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},
\]
one has, up to a fixed sign,
\[
\mathcal L[Y]=\frac{T_v}{K_v}.

After affine normalization \(S=x^2\), this becomes
\[
\mathcal L[Y]=\frac{1}{2}Y'''.
\]
So the dangerous nonmixed branch is now exactly the statement that \(\mathcal L[Y]\) has an odd-order zero. And because the source-level \(4\)-body correction is too high-order to kill low-order branches, the only cases that matter are the low odd orders
\[
\ord \mathcal L[Y]\in\{1,3,5\},

equivalently
\[
\ord T_v\in\{1,3,5\}
\qquad\text{with }K_v\neq 0.

On the **mixed** side, we already have the normal form
\[
Q_v(x)\sim (x^n,\ x,\ x^m),
\qquad m\ge 3,\ n>m,

and the orders satisfy
\[
m=r+2,\qquad n=s-r+3,
\]
where
\[
r=\ord K_v,\qquad s=\ord T_v.
\]

\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),

\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5).
\]
So the mixed problem has now been reduced to a **finite list of three order pairs**. That is the sharpest finite reduction we have on that side. 

I also attacked whether the natural extra algebraic condition from a putative pure source-level \(4\)-body remainder could kill those low-order branches. It cannot. If such a remainder has the natural discriminant-squared factor
\[
\prod_{1\le i<j\le 4}(h_i-h_j)^2,
\]
then on a shrinking branch it contributes order \(t^{12}\), while the geometric branch equations occur at order \(t^{n+3}\) in the nonmixed case and \(t^{m+n+1}\) in the mixed case. So it is too high-order to affect the low-order dangerous regimes:
\[
n=4,6,8
\quad\text{or}\quad
m+n\le 10.
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any closure of the \(4\)-point branch must come from RH-specific jet identities, not from generic local branch geometry or generic \(4\)-body divisibility.}
}
\]

\]
cannot have odd-order zeros of orders \(1,3,5\).

## Assistant (2026-04-19 18:52:43)

I’m going into the current draft and notes for the exact definitions of \(Y=\frac{u_5}{c}\), \(S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}\), \(K_v\), and \(T_v\), plus the normalizations already in the file. The goal is to see whether the paper already contains enough actual identities to derive low-order jet constraints, or whether we only have the abstract invariants without a usable relation between them.

_Thought for 3m 2s_

Here is the strongest exact derivation I can get from the **current lifted definitions**.

On a good \(v_5\)-patch, write
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},\qquad
S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}.
\]
The notes already isolate the repeated-node bridge and the mixed/nonmixed normal forms. turn171file0

From the current draft’s definitions, the key Wronskians are
\[
K_v=W_x(1,x,S),
\qquad
T_v=W_x(1,Y,x,S).
\]

Now compute them explicitly.

Since
\[
W_x(1,x,S)=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1&x&S\\
0&1&S'\\
0&0&S''
\end{pmatrix},
\]
we get the exact identity
\[
\boxed{K_v=S''.}
\]

And for the \(4\)-point lifted Wronskian,
\[
W_x(1,Y,x,S)=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1&Y&x&S\\
0&Y'&1&S'\\
0&Y''&0&S''\\
0&Y'''&0&S'''
\end{pmatrix},
\]
so
\[
\boxed{T_v=S''\,Y'''-S'''\,Y''.}
\]

Since \(K_v=S''\), this can be rewritten in two very useful ways:
\[
\boxed{T_v=K_v\,Y'''-(K_v)'Y'',}
\]
and
\[
\boxed{T_v=K_v^2\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{K_v}\right).}
\]

That last formula is the cleanest coordinate-free nonmixed identity we have.

---

## 1. Nonmixed reduction

If \(K_v(0)\neq 0\), then after affine normalization we may take
\[
S=x^2,
\qquad
K_v\equiv 2,
\]
so the exact identity becomes
\[
\boxed{T_v=2\,Y'''.}
\]

So the dangerous nonmixed low-order cases are now completely explicit:

- \(\ord_0 T_v=1\) means
  \[
  Y'''(0)=0,\qquad Y^{(4)}(0)\neq 0;
  \]
- \(\ord_0 T_v=3\) means
  \[
  Y^{(3)}(0)=Y^{(4)}(0)=Y^{(5)}(0)=0,\qquad Y^{(6)}(0)\neq 0;
  \]
- \(\ord_0 T_v=5\) means
  \[
  Y^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=Y^{(7)}(0)=0,\qquad Y^{(8)}(0)\neq 0.
  \]

So the **nonmixed jet theorem target** is now exactly:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that the actual normalized lifted germ }Y=\frac{u_5}{c}
\text{ cannot have odd-order zeros of }Y''' \text{ of orders }1,3,5.
}
\]

More invariantly, without fixing \(S=x^2\), it is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that }\frac{T_v}{K_v}=Y'''-\frac{K_v'}{K_v}Y''
\text{ cannot have odd-order zeros of orders }1,3,5\text{ when }K_v\neq 0.
}
\]

---

## 2. Mixed reduction

Now suppose \(K_v(0)=0\). Write
\[
r:=\ord_0 K_v.
\]
Then
\[
K_v(x)=\kappa x^r+O(x^{r+1}),
\qquad \kappa\neq 0.
\]

Because \(K_v=S''\), integrating twice gives
\[
S(x)=\frac{\kappa}{(r+1)(r+2)}x^{r+2}+O(x^{r+3}),
\]
after removing affine terms. So the mixed exponent \(m\) is
\[
\boxed{m=r+2.}
\]

Now write the first term of \(Y\) not already in \(\operatorname{span}\{1,x,S\}\) as
\[
Y(x)=\alpha x^n+O(x^{n+1}),
\qquad \alpha\neq 0,
\qquad n>r+2.
\]

Using
\[
T_v=K_vY'''-K_v'Y'',
\]
we get
\[
K_vY'''
=
\kappa x^r \cdot \alpha n(n-1)(n-2)x^{n-3}
=
\kappa\alpha n(n-1)(n-2)x^{n+r-3},
\]
and
\[
K_v'Y''
=
\kappa r x^{r-1}\cdot \alpha n(n-1)x^{n-2}
=
\kappa\alpha r\,n(n-1)x^{n+r-3}.
\]

So
\[
\boxed{
T_v=
\kappa\alpha\,n(n-1)\bigl(n-r-2\bigr)x^{\,n+r-3}
+O(x^{n+r-2}).
}
\]

Since \(n>r+2\), the coefficient is nonzero. Therefore
\[
\boxed{\ord_0 T_v=n+r-3.}
\]

If we call
\[
s:=\ord_0 T_v,
\]
then
\[
\boxed{n=s-r+3,}
\]
which is exactly the mixed bookkeeping already found in the notes. 

So the low-order dangerous mixed cases become very explicit in \((r,n)\)-form:

- \((m,n)=(3,4)\) means
  \[
  r=1,\qquad n=4,\qquad s=2;
  \]
- \((m,n)=(3,6)\) means
  \[
  r=1,\qquad n=6,\qquad s=4;
  \]
- \((m,n)=(4,6)\) means
  \[
  r=2,\qquad n=6,\qquad s=5.
  \]

So the **mixed jet theorem target** is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that the actual lifted jets cannot realize }
(r,n)\in\{(1,4),(1,6),(2,6)\}.
}
\]

Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
(r,s)\notin\{(1,2),(1,4),(2,5)\}.
}
\]

---

## 3. What this buys us

This is the sharpest reduction I can honestly derive from the current definitions.

It turns the \(4\)-pair problem into a **finite low-order jet obstruction list**:

### Nonmixed:
rule out odd-order zeros of
\[
Y''' \quad\text{of orders }1,3,5
\]
after normalization \(S=x^2\).

### Mixed:
rule out the three explicit low-order jet pairs
\[
(r,n)=(1,4),\ (1,6),\ (2,6).
\]

And the exact identities behind that reduction are
\[
K_v=S'',
\qquad
T_v=S''Y'''-S'''Y''=K_vY'''-K_v'Y''=K_v^2\Bigl(\frac{Y''}{K_v}\Bigr)'.
\]

---

## 4. What I cannot derive yet

I cannot honestly go further and prove those exclusions from the current draft alone, because that would require new **RH-specific identities relating the jets of**
\[
Y=\frac{u_5}{c}
\quad\text{and}\quad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5},
\]
and the current file does not yet provide such identities.

So the attack result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the \(4\)-pair endgame is now reduced to a finite list of explicit low-order jet identities to prove impossible.}
}
\]

That is real progress, even though it is not closure.

\boxed{K_v=(x')^3\,S''(x).}
\]
In particular, \(K_v\) and \(S''\) have the same zero set and the same vanishing order on a good patch. This is the precise version of the “\(K_v\) is \(S''\) up to a nowhere-zero factor” statement already used in the file. turn198file18

For \(T_v\), use the reparametrization formula for Wronskians of four functions:
\[
W_h(1,Y,x,S)=(x')^6\,W_x(1,Y,x,S).
\]
A direct determinant expansion gives
\[
W_x(1,Y,x,S)
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&Y&x&S\\
0&Y'&1&S'\\
0&Y''&0&S''\\
0&Y'''&0&S'''
\end{pmatrix}
=
S''Y'''-S'''Y''.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{T_v=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr).}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{T_v=(x')^6\,S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right).}
\]
Since \(K_v=(x')^3S''\), this can also be written as
\[
\boxed{T_v=K_v^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right).}
\]
That is the cleanest identity in the file for the lifted \(4\)-point branch. It is stronger than the bare statement “\(T_v=W_h(1,Y,x,S)\).” turn171file0

This immediately gives the exact nonmixed reduction. If \(K_v(0)\neq 0\), then after affine normalization of the lower triple \((1,x,S)\) you may take
\[
S=x^2,
\qquad
S''=2,
\qquad
S'''=0,
\]
so the identity becomes
\[
\boxed{T_v=2(x')^6\,Y'''.}
\]
Thus at a nonmixed point, the dangerous low-order branch cases are exactly the odd-order zeros of \(Y'''\). In particular:
\[
\ord_0 T_v=1 \iff Y'''(0)=0,\ Y^{(4)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\ord_0 T_v=3 \iff Y^{(3)}(0)=Y^{(4)}(0)=Y^{(5)}(0)=0,\ Y^{(6)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\ord_0 T_v=5 \iff Y^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=Y^{(7)}(0)=0,\ Y^{(8)}(0)\neq 0.
\]
So the nonmixed \(4\)-point obstruction has been reduced to a jet theorem for one scalar germ \(Y=u_5/c\) after normalizing \(S\) to \(x^2\). turn171file0

For the mixed case, let
\[
r:=\ord_0 K_v.
\]
Since \(K_v=(x')^3S''\), one has
\[
S''(x)=\kappa x^r+O(x^{r+1}),
\qquad \kappa\neq 0.
\]
After removing affine terms and integrating twice,
\[
\boxed{S(x)=\frac{\kappa}{(r+1)(r+2)}x^{r+2}+O(x^{r+3}).}
\]
So the mixed exponent \(m\) is exactly
\[
\boxed{m=r+2.}
\]

Now write the first term of \(Y\) not absorbed by \(\operatorname{span}\{1,x,S\}\) as
\[
Y(x)=\alpha x^n+O(x^{n+1}),
\qquad \alpha\neq 0,\qquad n>r+2.
\]
Substituting into
\[
T_v=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr)
\]
gives
\[
S''Y'''-S'''Y''
=
\kappa\alpha\,n(n-1)\bigl(n-r-2\bigr)x^{n+r-3}+O(x^{n+r-2}),
\]
so
\[
\boxed{\ord_0 T_v=n+r-3.}
\]
If \(s:=\ord_0 T_v\), this becomes
\[
\boxed{n=s-r+3,}
\]
which is exactly the mixed bookkeeping relation already recorded in the recent notes. \; \; turn198file3

The same identity also yields a cleaner invariant quantity:
\[
\boxed{
\ord_0\!\left(\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)\right)=s-2r.
}
\]
So the low-order dangerous mixed cases are not abstract anymore. They are exactly
\[
(r,s)\in\{(1,2),(1,4),(2,5)\},
\]
equivalently
\[
(m,n)\in\{(3,4),(3,6),(4,6)\},
\]
equivalently
\[
\ord_0\!\left(\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)\right)\in\{0,2,1\}.
\]
That is the finite list of low-order mixed jet obstructions currently visible from the file. \; \; turn198file3

So the exact identities you can really extract from the current draft are:

\[
\boxed{K_v=(x')^3S''}
\]
\[
\boxed{T_v=(x')^6(S''Y'''-S'''Y'')}
\]
\[
\boxed{T_v=K_v^2\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)}
\]
and therefore the two sharp reductions:
- nonmixed: dangerous \(4\)-point branches are exactly low odd-order zeros of \(Y'''\) after normalizing \(S=x^2\);
- mixed: dangerous \(4\)-point branches are exactly the three low-order order pairs \((r,s)=(1,2),(1,4),(2,5)\). turn198file2turn198file3

What the current file still does **not** provide is an RH-specific relation forcing those low-order patterns to be impossible. But it does now reduce the \(4\)-pair endgame to a finite list of explicit low-order jet identities to kill.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 19:05:45)

_Thought for 14s_

Yes — this helps a lot, and I think the core claim is right.

The key result is the **realization lemma**: at the current lifted level, arbitrary analytic local germs \((\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)\) can be realized on a good \(v_5\)-patch by taking, for example,
\[
c\equiv 1,\qquad v_5=1+\xi,\qquad u_5=\widetilde Y(\xi),\qquad \Delta_7=(1+\xi)\widetilde S(\xi).
\]
Then
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}=1+\xi,\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c}=\widetilde Y(\xi),\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}=\widetilde S(\xi),
\]
with \(M=1\neq 0\). So after shifting \(x\mapsto x-1\), the lifted germ is exactly
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\widetilde S(\xi)).
\]
That means the current lifted variables \((c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)\) by themselves are too flexible to forbid the dangerous low-order \(4\)-point models. I agree with that conclusion. 

So the important negative theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No obstruction to the low-order dangerous \(4\)-point patterns can come from identities involving only }
c,\ v_5,\ u_5,\ \Delta_7
\text{ and their derivatives.}
}
\]

That is genuinely useful, because it tells us where **not** to keep hunting. The current lifted jet package alone cannot rule out:

- nonmixed dangerous models
  \[
  Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^2),\qquad n=4,6,8,
  \]
- or mixed dangerous models
  \[
  Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^m),\qquad (m,n)=(3,4),(3,6),(4,6).
  \]

All of those are realizable at the present lifted level. So any real \(4\)-point closure must come from **deeper structure** than the current lifted variables encode. 

I also agree with the sharpened formulations:

For the **nonmixed** branch, after normalization to
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2),
\]
the whole problem is exactly to exclude odd-order zeros of
\[
\widetilde Y'''
\]
of orders \(1,3,5\), equivalently leading terms of \(\widetilde Y\) in degrees \(4,6,8\). That is the sharpest nonmixed target. 

For the **mixed** branch, the problem is exactly to exclude the three explicit low-order pairs
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5).
\]
That is the sharpest mixed target. 

The “dream identity”
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi)
\]
would indeed kill the entire low-order dangerous list in one shot, because it forbids even degrees in \(\widetilde Y\). But I agree with the note: there is nothing in the current draft that yields that parity identity at the lifted level. If such an identity exists, it has to come from deeper RH-specific structure. 

So my bottom line is:

- I think the other chat found the right **negative theorem**.
- It means the lifted \(4\)-point attack is now fully reduced to a search for **deeper source-level or matrix-level identities**.
- It also means we should stop expecting a closure from more manipulation of \(c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7\) alone.

So the next serious \(4\)-point attack is no longer “derive more lifted identities.” It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{go below the lifted variables and look for RH-specific source-level / matrix-level symmetry or parity constraints.}
}
\]

If you want, the next step I’d take is to turn this into an exact paper patch: a realization lemma plus a corollary saying the current lifted variables alone cannot exclude the dangerous low-order \(4\)-point patterns.

I checked the latest TeX and the recent \(4\)-point notes. The patch below is the cleanest way to preserve the new material without overclaiming: it adds the exact \(x\)-coordinate identities for \(K_v\) and \(T_v\), the sharp nonmixed and mixed jet reductions, the realization lemma showing the current lifted variables are too flexible to exclude the dangerous low-order patterns, and updated endgame remarks saying any remaining \(4\)-point closure must come from deeper source-/matrix-level structure. ## 1) Insert the exact \(x\)-coordinate identities for \(K_v\) and \(T_v\)

### Where to insert

Insert this block **immediately after**
```latex
\begin{corollary}[Lifted order-$7$ Wronskian is not identically zero]
\label{cor:lifted-vpatch-wronskian-not-identically-zero}
...
\end{corollary}
```

and **before**
```latex
\begin{corollary}[Local defect-free four-point exclusion on lifted ECT subintervals]
\label{cor:local-four-point-exclusion-lifted-vpatch}
...
\end{corollary}
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Exact \(x\)-coordinate identities for the lifted \(4\)-point invariants]
\label{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point}
On a good \(v_5\)-patch, write
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},
\qquad
S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}.
\]
Then:
\begin{enumerate}
\item the lifted planar curvature numerator satisfies
\[
K_v=(x')^3\,S''(x);
\]
\item the lifted \(4\)-point Wronskian satisfies
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,W_x(1,Y,x,S)
=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr);
\]
\item equivalently,
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)
=K_v^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right).
\]
\end{enumerate}
In particular, \(K_v\) and \(S''\) have the same zero set and the same
vanishing order on every good \(v_5\)-patch.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By definition,
\[
K_v=x'\sigma_v''-\sigma_v' x''.
\]
Since \(S=\sigma_v\) as a function of the local coordinate \(x\), we have
\[
\sigma_v'=S' x',
\qquad
\sigma_v''=S''(x')^2+S' x'',
\]
hence
\[
K_v=x'\bigl(S''(x')^2+S'x''\bigr)-S' x' x''=(x')^3S''.
\]

Next,
\[
T_v=W_h(1,Y,x,S).
\]
Under reparametrization \(h\mapsto x(h)\), a \(4\times4\) Wronskian gains the
factor \((x')^{0+1+2+3}=(x')^6\), so
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,W_x(1,Y,x,S).
\]
Now
\[
W_x(1,Y,x,S)
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&Y&x&S\\
0&Y'&1&S'\\
0&Y''&0&S''\\
0&Y'''&0&S'''
\end{pmatrix}
=
S''Y'''-S'''Y'',
\]
which proves the second identity.

Finally,
\[
S''Y'''-S'''Y''
=
S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right),
\]
and using \(K_v=(x')^3S''\) gives
\[
(x')^6S''^{\,2}=K_v^{\,2}.
\]
This yields the third identity.
\end{proof}
```

This is the exact coordinate calculus you were using informally. It should be theorem-level.

## 2) Replace the current nonmixed \(4\)-point remark with a sharp jet-reduction remark

### Find
```latex
\begin{remark}[Nonmixed $4$-point zeros can carry preexisting close branches]
\label{rem:nonmixed-four-point-zeros-can-carry-preexisting-branches}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace it with
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact nonmixed jet reduction for the lifted \(4\)-point branch]
\label{rem:nonmixed-four-point-zeros-can-carry-preexisting-branches}
At a nonmixed lifted point
\[
K_v(0)\neq 0,
\qquad
T_v(0)=0,
\]
Proposition~\ref{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point} gives
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right).
\]
Since \(K_v(0)\neq 0\), one has \(S''(0)\neq 0\), so after analytic
reparametrization and affine target normalization preserving coplanarity one may
write the lifted germ as
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\qquad
\widetilde Y(0)=\widetilde Y'(0)=\widetilde Y''(0)=0.
\]
In these normalized coordinates,
\[
W_\xi(1,\widetilde Y,\xi,\xi^2)=2\,\widetilde Y'''.
\]
Thus the nonmixed lifted \(4\)-point problem is exactly the jet problem of
excluding odd-order zeros of \(\widetilde Y'''\).

In particular, the low-order dangerous nonmixed cases are exactly:
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0 T_v=1
\iff
\widetilde Y'''(0)=0,\ \widetilde Y^{(4)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0 T_v=3
\iff
\widetilde Y^{(3)}(0)=\widetilde Y^{(4)}(0)=\widetilde Y^{(5)}(0)=0,\
\widetilde Y^{(6)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0 T_v=5
\iff
\widetilde Y^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=\widetilde Y^{(7)}(0)=0,\
\widetilde Y^{(8)}(0)\neq 0.
\]

So the nonmixed \(4\)-point endgame has been reduced to the finite jet problem:
rule out leading terms of \(\widetilde Y\) in degrees \(4,6,8\).

This is strictly sharper than the earlier qualitative statement that
nonmixed odd-order \(T_v\)-zeros can carry preexisting close branches.
\end{remark}
```

This preserves the earlier insight but upgrades it to the exact jet statement you now have.

## 3) Replace the current mixed-order bookkeeping remark with the exact mixed jet reduction

### Find
```latex
\begin{remark}[Mixed order bookkeeping and lack of generic parity obstruction]
\label{rem:mixed-order-bookkeeping-no-generic-parity-obstruction}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace it with
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact mixed jet reduction and low-order dangerous list]
\label{rem:mixed-order-bookkeeping-no-generic-parity-obstruction}
At a mixed lifted point
\[
K_v(0)=0,
\qquad
T_v(0)=0,
\]
set
\[
r:=\operatorname{ord}_0 K_v,
\qquad
s:=\operatorname{ord}_0 T_v.
\]
By Proposition~\ref{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point},
\[
K_v=(x')^3S'',
\]
so on a good \(v_5\)-patch one has
\[
S''(x)=\kappa x^r+O(x^{r+1}),
\qquad \kappa\neq 0.
\]
After removing affine terms and integrating twice,
\[
S(x)=\frac{\kappa}{(r+1)(r+2)}x^{r+2}+O(x^{r+3}).
\]
Thus the mixed exponent \(m\) of the monomial model
\[
Q_v(x)\sim (x^n,\ x,\ x^m)
\]
is exactly
\[
m=r+2.
\]

Now let \(n\) be the first degree of \(Y(x)\) not absorbed by
\(\operatorname{span}\{1,x,S\}\), so that
\[
Y(x)=\alpha x^n+O(x^{n+1}),
\qquad \alpha\neq 0,
\qquad n>r+2.
\]
Using
\[
T_v=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr),
\]
one finds
\[
T_v
=
(x')^6\,
\kappa\alpha\,n(n-1)\bigl(n-r-2\bigr)x^{n+r-3}
+O(x^{n+r-2}),
\]
and since \(n>r+2\), the leading coefficient is nonzero. Therefore
\[
s=n+r-3,
\qquad
n=s-r+3.
\]
So the mixed exponents are exactly
\[
m=r+2,
\qquad
n=s-r+3,
\qquad
s\ge 2r.
\]

The dangerous low-order mixed models are therefore finite and explicit:
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5).
\]

Thus the mixed lifted \(4\)-point problem has also been reduced to a finite jet
obstruction list: rule out those three order pairs in the actual geometry.
\end{remark}
```

This is much sharper than the current parity-only discussion.

## 4) Insert the realization lemma and its corollary

This is the negative theorem from the other chat that tells you where **not** to keep hunting.

### Where to insert

Insert this **immediately after**
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact mixed jet reduction and low-order dangerous list]
\label{rem:mixed-order-bookkeeping-no-generic-parity-obstruction}
...
\end{remark}
```

and **before**
```latex

\label{rem:next-algebraic-target-four-point}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Realization lemma for lifted germs]
\label{prop:realization-lemma-lifted-germs}
Let \(\widetilde Y(\xi)\) and \(\widetilde S(\xi)\) be arbitrary analytic germs
at \(\xi=0\). Define
\[
c(\xi)\equiv 1,
\qquad
v_5(\xi):=1+\xi,
\qquad
u_5(\xi):=\widetilde Y(\xi),
\qquad
\Delta_7(\xi):=(1+\xi)\widetilde S(\xi).
\]
Then on a sufficiently small neighborhood of \(0\),
\[
c\neq 0,
\qquad
v_5\neq 0,
\qquad
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5=1\neq 0,
\]
so this is a good \(v_5\)-patch. The normalized lifted coordinates are
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}=1+\xi,
\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c}=\widetilde Y(\xi),
\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}=\widetilde S(\xi).
\]
After the affine shift \(x\mapsto x-1\), the lifted germ becomes
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\widetilde S(\xi)\bigr).
\]

Thus every analytic pair of germs \((\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)\) occurs, after
affine normalization, as the lifted pair \((Y,S)\) on some good \(v_5\)-patch.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
The displayed formulas are immediate from the definitions. The hypotheses
\(c\neq 0\), \(v_5\neq 0\), and \(M=1\neq 0\) hold on a sufficiently small
neighborhood of \(0\), and the formulas for \(x,Y,S\) are direct.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[No new jet obstruction is available at the current lifted level]
\label{cor:no-new-jet-obstruction-current-lifted-level}
No identity involving only
\[
c,\qquad v_5,\qquad u_5,\qquad \Delta_7
\]
and their derivatives can exclude the low-order dangerous lifted \(4\)-point
patterns listed above.

In particular, the dangerous nonmixed models
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^2),
\qquad n=4,6,8,
\]
and the dangerous mixed models
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^m),
\qquad (m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
are all realizable at the present lifted level.

Consequently, any RH-specific obstruction to these low-order \(4\)-point
patterns must use information not encoded in the current lifted variables
\[
(c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)
\]
alone. It must come from deeper source-level corrected quotient-germ structure,
matrix-level identities behind \((u_5,\Delta_7)\), or an additional symmetry
not yet visible in the present lifted formulation.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Apply Proposition~\ref{prop:realization-lemma-lifted-germs} with
\[
(\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)=(\xi^n,\xi^2)
\]
for the nonmixed cases, and with
\[
(\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)=(\xi^n,\xi^m)
\]
for the mixed cases.
\end{proof}
```

This is the single most important new “negative theorem” to add so you stop chasing impossible lifted-level identities.

---

The current remark still suggests an “extra compatibility condition beyond coplanarity” might live at the lifted level. The new realization lemma shows that is too optimistic.

```latex
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{remark}[What the four-point branch now reduces to]
\label{rem:next-algebraic-target-four-point}
The lifted \(4\)-point geometry is now sharply reduced.

On the nonmixed side, the exact invariant identity
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)
\]
shows that the low-order dangerous cases are precisely the odd-order zeros of
\(\widetilde Y'''\) of orders \(1,3,5\) after normalizing the lower triple to
\[
(1,\xi,\xi^2).
\]

On the mixed side, the dangerous low-order cases are precisely the three order
pairs
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5),
\]
equivalently
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6).
\]

By Corollary~\ref{cor:no-new-jet-obstruction-current-lifted-level}, these
patterns cannot be excluded by any identity involving only the present lifted
variables
\[
(c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)
\]
and their derivatives.

Accordingly, the next serious four-point target is no longer another lifted jet
computation. Any closure of the four-point branch must come from deeper
source-level corrected quotient-germ structure, matrix-level identities behind
\((u_5,\Delta_7)\), or a hidden RH-specific symmetry not yet encoded in the
present lifted formulation.
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\label{rem:wip-dream-parity-identity-four-point}
After nonmixed normalization
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\]
a parity identity of the form
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi)
\]
would immediately eliminate all currently dangerous nonmixed low-order jet
patterns, since it would forbid leading terms of degrees \(4,6,8\), and would
also remove the dangerous mixed sector with even \(n\). The present lifted
formalism does not supply such an identity; if it exists, it must come from a
deeper RH-specific symmetry not yet visible in the current quotient-germ
package.
\end{remark}
```

## 8) Why this patch is worth adding now

This patch preserves all the important \(4\)-point progress you now have:

- exact formulas
  \[
  K_v=(x')^3S'',\qquad
  T_v=(x')^6(S''Y'''-S'''Y'')=(x')^6S''^{\,2}\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)',
  \]
- exact nonmixed reduction to the low-order odd-zero list for \(\widetilde Y'''\),
- exact mixed reduction to the finite order list \((r,s)=(1,2),(1,4),(2,5)\),
- and the realization lemma proving no further lifted-variable-only jet identity can kill those patterns. 

That is the cleanest honest state of the \(4\)-pair endgame right now.

If you want, next I’d help you turn the “deeper source-level corrected quotient-germ structure” into a concrete theorem target for the \(4\)-body remainder, now that the lifted-level hunt has been exhausted.

Yes — this is a real advance, and I think the note has the right takeaway.

The best new idea is to stop treating \(Y\) itself as the main object and instead use the gauge-invariant pair
\[
\mathcal Z:=\frac{Y''}{S''},
\qquad
\mathcal R:=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}.
\]
That is the cleanest scalar reduction of the lifted \(4\)-point branch. It survives the affine gauge
\[
Y\mapsto Y+\alpha+\beta x+\gamma S,
\]
because \(\mathcal Z\mapsto \mathcal Z+\gamma\) and \(\mathcal R\) stays fixed. So \(\mathcal R\) is the canonical invariant to study, not \(Y\) in an arbitrary chart. 

I also agree with the sharpened bookkeeping:

For nonmixed points, after normalization
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2),
\]
you get
\[
\mathcal Z=\tfrac12 \widetilde Y'',
\qquad
\mathcal R=\tfrac12 \widetilde Y'''.
\]
So the dangerous nonmixed cases are exactly
\[
\ord_0 \mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
\]
equivalently leading terms of \(\widetilde Y\) in degrees \(4,6,8\). For mixed points, if
\[
r:=\ord_0 K_v,
\]
then
\[
\ord_0 \mathcal Z=s-2r+1,
\qquad
\ord_0 \mathcal R=s-2r,
\]
so the dangerous mixed low-order list becomes
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal R)\in\{(1,0),(1,2),(2,1)\},
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
\]
That is a very nice compression of the whole low-order \(4\)-point obstruction. 

The most important result in the note, though, is the negative one: the **realization lemma**. If arbitrary analytic \((\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)\) can be realized on a good \(v_5\)-patch using only the current lifted variables \((c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)\), then no identity built only from those variables and their derivatives can force the dream conditions
\[
\mathcal R \text{ even}
\quad\text{or}\quad
\ord_0 \mathcal Z\ge 4.
\]
So the current lifted formalism is exhausted as a source of new \(4\)-point exclusions. Any real closure must come from deeper RH-specific structure: source-level corrected quotient-germ symmetry, or matrix-level identities behind \(u_5\) and \(\Delta_7\), or something equivalent. 

So my verdict is:

- yes, \(\mathcal Z\) and especially \(\mathcal R=T_v/K_v^2\) should now be treated as the main \(4\)-point invariants;
- yes, the “dream identity” should be reformulated as
  \[
  \mathcal R \text{ even}
  \]
  in the normalized nonmixed coordinate, and
  \[
  \ord_0 \mathcal Z\ge 4
  \]
  at mixed points;
- and yes, the note proves that we should stop searching for those identities inside the current lifted-variable package.

That means the next real hunt is below the lifted level. The sharp target is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{find a source-level or matrix-level RH-specific identity that forces either }
\mathcal R \text{ even, or } \ord_0 \mathcal Z\ge 4.
}
\]

I think this should absolutely be folded into the paper patch: it preserves the strongest current \(4\)-point reduction and the strongest negative theorem about where no further progress can come from.

# 1) Insert the canonical invariants \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\)

### Where to insert

Insert this block **immediately after**
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Exact $x$-coordinate identities for the lifted $4$-point invariants]
\label{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point}
...
\end{proposition}
```

and **before**
```latex
\begin{corollary}[Local defect-free four-point exclusion on lifted ECT subintervals]
\label{cor:local-four-point-exclusion-lifted-vpatch}
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Canonical scalar invariants \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\) on a nonmixed lifted patch]
\label{prop:canonical-Z-R-invariants-lifted-four-point}
On a good \(v_5\)-patch, write
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},
\qquad
S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}.
\]
On the nonmixed subregion where \(K_v\neq 0\), define
\[
\mathcal Z:=\frac{Y''}{S''},
\qquad
\mathcal R:=\frac{d\mathcal Z}{dx}.
\]
Then:
\begin{enumerate}
\item
\[
\mathcal R=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2};
\]
\item under the affine gauge
\[
Y\longmapsto Y+\alpha+\beta x+\gamma S,
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal Z\longmapsto \mathcal Z+\gamma,
\qquad
\mathcal R\longmapsto \mathcal R.
\]
Hence \(\mathcal R\) is the canonical scalar invariant of the lifted
\(4\)-point branch on every nonmixed patch.
\end{enumerate}
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point},
\[
T_v=(x')^6\,S''^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)
=K_v^{\,2}\,\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right),
\]
so
\[
\mathcal R=\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right)=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}.
\]

For the gauge transformation, one has
\[
(Y+\alpha+\beta x+\gamma S)''=Y''+\gamma S'',
\]
hence
\[
\mathcal Z\longmapsto \frac{Y''+\gamma S''}{S''}=\mathcal Z+\gamma.
\]
Differentiating with respect to \(x\) gives \(\mathcal R\mapsto \mathcal R\).
\end{proof}
```

---

# 2) Replace the current nonmixed \(4\)-point remark with the sharper \(\mathcal R\)-reduction

### Find
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact nonmixed jet reduction for the lifted $4$-point branch]
\label{rem:nonmixed-four-point-zeros-can-carry-preexisting-branches}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact nonmixed reduction in terms of \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\)]
\label{rem:nonmixed-four-point-zeros-can-carry-preexisting-branches}
At a nonmixed lifted point
\[
K_v(0)\neq 0,
\qquad
T_v(0)=0,
\]
Propositions~\ref{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point}
and~\ref{prop:canonical-Z-R-invariants-lifted-four-point} give
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}.
\]
Since \(K_v(0)\neq 0\), one has \(S''(0)\neq 0\), so after analytic
reparametrization and affine target normalization preserving coplanarity one may
write the lifted germ as
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\qquad
\widetilde Y(0)=\widetilde Y'(0)=\widetilde Y''(0)=0.
\]
In these normalized coordinates,
\[
\mathcal Z(\xi)=\frac12\,\widetilde Y''(\xi),
\qquad
\mathcal R(\xi)=\frac12\,\widetilde Y'''(\xi).
\]

Thus the nonmixed lifted \(4\)-point problem is exactly the jet problem of
excluding odd-order zeros of \(\mathcal R\). Equivalently, it is the problem of
excluding even leading degrees of \(\widetilde Y\) in the normalized chart.

In particular, the low-order dangerous nonmixed cases are exactly:
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R=1
\iff
\widetilde Y'''(0)=0,\ \widetilde Y^{(4)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R=3
\iff
\widetilde Y^{(3)}(0)=\widetilde Y^{(4)}(0)=\widetilde Y^{(5)}(0)=0,\
\widetilde Y^{(6)}(0)\neq 0,
\]
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R=5
\iff
\widetilde Y^{(3)}(0)=\cdots=\widetilde Y^{(7)}(0)=0,\
\widetilde Y^{(8)}(0)\neq 0.
\]

Equivalently, the dangerous nonmixed low-order list is
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal Z\in\{2,4,6\}
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}.
\]

So the nonmixed \(4\)-point endgame has been reduced to a finite jet problem
for the canonical invariant \(\mathcal R\).
\end{remark}
```

---

# 3) Replace the current mixed \(4\)-point remark with the \((r,\ord\mathcal Z,\ord\mathcal R)\) bookkeeping

### Find
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact mixed jet reduction and low-order dangerous list]
\label{rem:mixed-order-bookkeeping-no-generic-parity-obstruction}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{remark}[Exact mixed reduction in terms of \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\)]
\label{rem:mixed-order-bookkeeping-no-generic-parity-obstruction}
At a mixed lifted point
\[
K_v(0)=0,
\qquad
T_v(0)=0,
\]
set
\[
r:=\operatorname{ord}_0 K_v,
\qquad
s:=\operatorname{ord}_0 T_v.
\]
By Proposition~\ref{prop:exact-x-coordinate-identities-lifted-four-point},
\[
K_v=(x')^3S'',
\]
so on a good \(v_5\)-patch one has
\[
S''(x)=\kappa x^r+O(x^{r+1}),
\qquad \kappa\neq 0.
\]
After removing affine terms and integrating twice,
\[
S(x)=\frac{\kappa}{(r+1)(r+2)}x^{r+2}+O(x^{r+3}).
\]
Thus the mixed exponent \(m\) of the monomial model
\[
Q_v(x)\sim (x^n,\ x,\ x^m)
\]
is exactly
\[
m=r+2.
\]

Now let \(n\) be the first degree of \(Y(x)\) not absorbed by
\(\operatorname{span}\{1,x,S\}\), so that
\[
Y(x)=\alpha x^n+O(x^{n+1}),
\qquad \alpha\neq 0,
\qquad n>r+2.
\]
Using
\[
T_v=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr),
\]
one finds
\[
s=n+r-3,
\qquad
n=s-r+3.
\]

Equivalently, on the nonmixed complement of \(K_v=0\),
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}
\]
satisfy the order bookkeeping
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal Z=n-r-2=s-2r+1,
\qquad
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R=s-2r.
\]

Thus the dangerous low-order mixed models are finite and explicit:
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3),\ (2,2),
\]
or
\[
(r,\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal R)=(1,0),\ (1,2),\ (2,1).
\]

So the mixed lifted \(4\)-point endgame has also been reduced to a finite jet
obstruction list, now phrased in terms of the invariant pair
\[
(\mathcal Z,\mathcal R).
\]
\end{remark}
```

---

# 4) Replace the realization-lemma corollary so it explicitly rules out lifted-variable-only control of \(\mathcal Z,\mathcal R\)

### Find
```latex
\begin{corollary}[No new jet obstruction is available at the current lifted level]
\label{cor:no-new-jet-obstruction-current-lifted-level}
...
\end{corollary}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{corollary}[No new obstruction for \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\) is available at the current lifted level]
\label{cor:no-new-jet-obstruction-current-lifted-level}
No identity involving only
\[
c,\qquad v_5,\qquad u_5,\qquad \Delta_7
\]
and their derivatives can force either of the two dream conclusions:
\[
\mathcal R \text{ is even in the normalized nonmixed coordinate},
\]
or
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal Z\ge 4
\quad\text{at every mixed point.}
\]

In particular, the dangerous nonmixed models
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^2),
\qquad n=4,6,8,
\]
and the dangerous mixed models
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^m),
\qquad (m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
are all realizable at the present lifted level.

Consequently, any RH-specific obstruction to the low-order dangerous
\(4\)-point patterns must use information not encoded in the current lifted
variables
\[
(c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)
\]
alone. It must come from deeper source-level corrected quotient-germ
structure, matrix-level identities behind \((u_5,\Delta_7)\), or an additional
symmetry not yet visible in the present lifted formulation.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
Apply Proposition~\ref{prop:realization-lemma-lifted-germs} with
\[
(\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)=(\xi^n,\xi^2)
\]
for the nonmixed cases, and with
\[
(\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)=(\xi^n,\xi^m)
\]
for the mixed cases. These realize exactly the dangerous low-order patterns for
\((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\) listed above.
\end{proof}
```

---

```latex
\begin{remark}[Work in progress: the dream identities for \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\)]
\label{rem:wip-dream-parity-identity-four-point}
The dream identity should not be formulated in terms of \(Y\) in an arbitrary
chart, but in terms of the canonical invariants
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}.

\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal Z(\xi)=\frac12\,\widetilde Y''(\xi),
\qquad
\mathcal R(\xi)=\frac12\,\widetilde Y'''(\xi).
\]
So the natural nonmixed dream identity is:
\[
\mathcal R \text{ is even in the normalized local coordinate } \xi.
\]
Equivalently, \(\mathcal Z\) is odd, or \(\widetilde Y\) is odd up to the
harmless affine gauge.

On the mixed side, a sufficient dream identity is:
\[
\operatorname{ord}_0\mathcal Z\ge 4
\quad\text{at every mixed point.}
\]
This would eliminate the entire explicit low-order dangerous mixed list.

The present lifted formalism does not supply either identity. If they exist,
they must come from deeper RH-specific structure below the current lifted
variables.
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\]
```

# Why this patch matters

This patch preserves the strongest new \(4\)-point progress:

- the exact canonical invariant
  \[
  \mathcal R=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}=\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right),
  \]
- the companion invariant
  \[
  \mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''},
  \]
- the exact low-order dangerous lists in terms of \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\),
- and the negative theorem that no further obstruction can come from the current lifted variables alone. 

So after this patch, the \(4\)-point endgame is stated in the sharpest currently honest form.

## Assistant (2026-04-19 19:24:25)

_Thought for 18s_

This is useful. I think the new identity is **real and worth keeping**, but it does **not** escape the “current lifted variables are too flexible” barrier. 

The genuinely new thing is the exact formula
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
\]
with
\[
J_f(h):=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c(h) & v_5(h) & f(h)\\
c'(h) & v_5'(h) & f'(h)\\
c''(h) & v_5''(h) & f''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
And then
\[
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}
=
\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right).
\]

That is a good compression. It replaces “mysterious behavior of \(Y\)” by a ratio of two triple Wronskians built from the original scalar data. Conceptually, that is cleaner and closer to the underlying source/matrix structure. 

I also think the derivation is right. The key identity
\[
\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\!\left(\frac{f}{c}\right)=\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_f
\]
is exactly the sort of projective-coordinate / change-of-variable formula you expect from \(x=v_5/c\). Applying it to \(f=u_5\) and \(f=\lambda=\Delta_7/v_5\) gives
\[
Y''=\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_{u_5},
\qquad
S''=\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_\lambda,
\]
so
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}.
\]
That part is solid. 

Where I’d be careful is the interpretation.

This does **not** yet provide a new obstruction beyond the current lifted-variable package, because \(J_{u_5}\) and \(J_\lambda\) are still built only from
\[
c,\ v_5,\ u_5,\ \Delta_7.
\]
So the realization-lemma warning still applies: arbitrary analytic lifted germs remain realizable at this level, and this Wronskian-ratio reformulation by itself cannot rule out the dangerous low-order \(4\)-point patterns. It gives a **better invariant to attack**, not a closure. 

That means the right conclusion is:

- yes, \(\mathcal Z=J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\) should go into the paper;
- yes, \(\mathcal R\) should be reframed as the derivative of that ratio;
- no, this does not invalidate the realization-lemma corollary;
- and yes, the next hunt should now be phrased as a search for an RH-specific relation between
  \[
  J_{u_5}\quad\text{and}\quad J_\lambda,
  \]
  not between \(Y\) and \(S\) abstractly. 

So I would treat this as a **real upgrade of the invariant package**, with the sharpened theorem targets:

For nonmixed points:
\[
\mathcal R=\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right)
\quad\text{should be even in the normalized coordinate.}
\]

For mixed points:
\[
\ord_0\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right)\ge 4.
\]

That is the sharpest version of the \(4\)-point dream identity I’ve seen so far. 

The next best move is to patch this into the draft as a refinement of the \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\) remarks and propositions.

_Thought for 33s_

Yes — this is a real upgrade, and I think it should replace the current \(4\)-point “dream identity” discussion.

The genuinely new thing is not another branch argument. It is the exact Wronskian-ratio identity
\[
\mathcal Z:=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
\]
with
\[
J_f(h):=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c(h)&v_5(h)&f(h)\\
c'(h)&v_5'(h)&f'(h)\\
c''(h)&v_5''(h)&f''(h)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and therefore
\[
\mathcal R:=\mathcal Z'
=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}
=\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right).
\]
That is a better formulation than working directly with \(Y\), because \(\mathcal Z\) and especially \(\mathcal R\) are the canonical gauge-invariant objects for the lifted \(4\)-point branch. 

I also agree with the sharpened obstruction list in that note. In terms of \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\):

- nonmixed dangerous cases are exactly
  \[
  \ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
  \]
  equivalently \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\in\{2,4,6\}\);

- mixed dangerous low-order cases are exactly
  \[
  (r,\ord_0\mathcal R)\in\{(1,0),(1,2),(2,1)\},
  \]
  equivalently
  \[
  (r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
  \]

That is the cleanest finite reduction of the \(4\)-point endgame we have so far. 

The other important part is the negative conclusion: this still does **not** evade the realization-lemma barrier. Since \(J_{u_5}\) and \(J_\lambda\) are still built only from
\[
c,\ v_5,\ u_5,\ \Delta_7,
\]
this reformulation is still inside the current lifted-variable package. So it gives a much better invariant to attack, but it does **not** by itself create a new RH-specific obstruction. The note is right that any real exclusion must still come from something deeper: source-level symmetry, or matrix-level structure behind \(u_5\) and \(\Delta_7\). 

So my verdict is:

- yes, this should go into the paper;
- yes, it improves the previous \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\) patch materially;
- no, it does not yet solve the \(4\)-point branch;
- and yes, it tells us the next search should be phrased as a structural relation between
  \[
  J_{u_5}\quad\text{and}\quad J_\lambda,
  \]
  not between \(Y\) and \(S\) abstractly. 

The strongest way to state the remaining theorem targets is now:

\[
\text{nonmixed: }\mathcal R \text{ is even in the normalized coordinate,}
\]
and
\[
\text{mixed: }\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4.

I tried attacks #2 and #3 as hard as I can from the current file and the new \(\mathcal Z,\mathcal R\) reformulation, and the honest result is:

\[
\boxed{\text{neither \#2 nor \#3 can be proved at the current \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\) level alone.}}
\]

The strongest thing I can show is a **sharpened realization lemma at the \(\mathcal Z\)-level**. On a good \(v_5\)-patch we now have
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2},
\]
with
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad Y=\frac{u_5}{c},\qquad S=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}.
\]
So instead of realizing arbitrary \((Y,S)\), one can realize **arbitrary \((\mathcal Z,S)\)**: given any analytic germ \(\widetilde S(\xi)\) and any analytic germ \(\widetilde Z(\xi)\), define \(\widetilde Y\) by
\[
\widetilde Y''(\xi)=\widetilde Z(\xi)\,\widetilde S''(\xi),
\]
then take
\[
c(\xi)\equiv 1,\qquad v_5(\xi)=1+\xi,\qquad u_5(\xi)=\widetilde Y(\xi),\qquad \Delta_7(\xi)=(1+\xi)\widetilde S(\xi).
\]
On the resulting good patch, after shifting \(x=1+\xi\) to \(\xi\), one gets exactly
\[
S=\widetilde S,\qquad \mathcal Z=\widetilde Z,\qquad \mathcal R=\widetilde Z'.
\]
So **arbitrary analytic \(\mathcal Z\)** is realizable at the current \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\) level. 

That kills attack #2 at the current level. The nonmixed dream identity was:

\[
\mathcal R \text{ even in the normalized nonmixed coordinate}
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal Z \text{ odd.}
\]

But since arbitrary analytic \(\mathcal Z\) is realizable, there is no identity involving only
\[
c,\ v_5,\ u_5,\ \Delta_7,
\]
their derivatives, or even only the Wronskian ratio
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\]
that can force \(\mathcal Z\) to be odd or \(\mathcal R\) to be even. So attack #2 does **not** close at this level. The best exact reformulation is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{To prove \#2, one needs a deeper source-level or matrix-level symmetry forcing }
\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda} \text{ to be odd after nonmixed normalization.}
}
\]
A sufficient condition would be an involution on the deeper construction that becomes \(\xi\mapsto-\xi\) and acts by
\[
J_{u_5}(-\xi)J_\lambda(\xi)+J_{u_5}(\xi)J_\lambda(-\xi)=0,
\]
or more simply \(J_{u_5}\) odd and \(J_\lambda\) even in the normalized coordinate. 

Attack #3 fails at the same level, but the obstruction becomes very explicit. The mixed dream identity was

\[
\ord_0 \mathcal Z \ge 4.
\]

At a mixed point, the current bookkeeping is
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}
\]
for the dangerous low-order list, equivalently
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal R)\in\{(1,0),(1,2),(2,1)\}.
\]
And these are all realizable directly at the \(\mathcal Z\)-level:

- choose \(\widetilde S(\xi)=\xi^3\), \(\widetilde Z(\xi)=\xi\), then \(\widetilde Y''=6\xi^2\), so \(\widetilde Y\sim \xi^4\), giving \((m,n)=(3,4)\);
- choose \(\widetilde S(\xi)=\xi^3\), \(\widetilde Z(\xi)=\xi^3\), then \(\widetilde Y\sim \xi^6\), giving \((m,n)=(3,6)\);
- choose \(\widetilde S(\xi)=\xi^4\), \(\widetilde Z(\xi)=\xi^2\), then \(\widetilde Y\sim \xi^6\), giving \((m,n)=(4,6)\).

So there is no identity at the current \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\) level that can force \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4\). The sharp reformulation is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{To prove \#3, one needs a deeper source-level or matrix-level divisibility }
\ord_0 J_{u_5}\ge \ord_0 J_\lambda +4
\text{ at mixed points.}
}
\]

Since
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\]
this is exactly the invariant form of the mixed order-gap theorem. 

So the honest attack result is:

- \#2 reduces to proving an **oddness/parity theorem for the ratio**
  \[
  \frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda};
  \]
- \#3 reduces to proving a **mixed order-gap theorem**
  \[
  \ord J_{u_5}\ge \ord J_\lambda +4;
  \]
- and neither can come from the current lifted variables or the current \(J\)-ratio formalism alone, because arbitrary analytic \(\mathcal Z\) is realizable there. 

So if the other chat is on attack #1, the most useful coordination point is this: if #1 finds any RH-specific relation between
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Theta=\frac{W_{u_5}}{W},
\]
then the only kinds of relations that would actually help are ones strong enough to imply either

\[
\mathcal Z(-\xi)=-\mathcal Z(\xi)
\]
after nonmixed normalization, or
\[
\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4
\]
at mixed points. Anything weaker will still leave the low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list open.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 00:29:04)

_Thought for 34s_

Here is the cleanest continuation I can justify.

The new note lets us sharpen the negative result one more step:

\[
\boxed{
\text{attacks \#2 and \#3 do not fail merely at the \(Y,S\) level; they already fail at the }\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\text{ level.}
}
\]

That is useful, because it tells us exactly what any successful RH-specific relation would have to do.

## 1. Stronger no-go: arbitrary analytic \(\mathcal Z\) is realizable

We already had the realization lemma for arbitrary analytic \((Y,S)\). The new note makes clear that this can be pushed one step lower.

Pick any analytic germ \(\widetilde S(\xi)\) with \(\widetilde S''\not\equiv 0\), and any analytic germ \(\widetilde Z(\xi)\). Define \(\widetilde Y\) by
\[
\widetilde Y''(\xi)=\widetilde Z(\xi)\,\widetilde S''(\xi).
\]
Then build the good patch exactly as before:
\[
c(\xi)\equiv 1,\qquad
v_5(\xi)=1+\xi,\qquad
u_5(\xi)=\widetilde Y(\xi),\qquad
\Delta_7(\xi)=(1+\xi)\widetilde S(\xi).
\]
After shifting \(x=1+\xi\) to \(\xi\), we get
\[
S=\widetilde S,\qquad
Y=\widetilde Y,
\qquad
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\widetilde Z,
\qquad
\mathcal R=\widetilde Z'.
\]

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{every analytic germ }\widetilde Z\text{ occurs as }\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\text{ on some good patch.}
}
\]

This is stronger than the earlier realization lemma. It means:

- no identity involving only the current variables can force \(\mathcal Z\) odd;
- no identity involving only the current variables can force \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4\) at mixed points.

So attacks #2 and #3 are dead at the current \(J\)-ratio level. turn200file0

## 2. Exact reformulation of what would be sufficient

This gives the sharpest exact targets.

For the nonmixed branch, the dream identity is now:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z(-\xi)=-\mathcal Z(\xi)
\quad\text{after nonmixed normalization.}
}
\]

Equivalently,
\[
\mathcal R(\xi)=\mathcal Z'(\xi)\ \text{is even.}
\]

For the mixed branch, the sufficient dream identity is:

\[
\boxed{
\ord_0 \mathcal Z \ge 4.
}
\]

Using
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\]
these become:

### Nonmixed parity target
\[
\boxed{
\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\ \text{is odd in the normalized local coordinate.}
}
\]

### Mixed order-gap target
\[
\boxed{
\ord_0 J_{u_5}\ge \ord_0 J_\lambda +4.
}
\]

That is the sharpest reformulation of attacks #2 and #3 I can give. turn203file0

## 3. What kind of RH-specific relation could actually help

The note also helps by narrowing the shape of any useful deeper identity.

A successful RH-specific relation would have to be something like one of these:

### Type A: involutive parity
After nonmixed normalization, some source-level or matrix-level symmetry induces
\[
\xi\mapsto -\xi
\]
and forces
\[
J_{u_5}(-\xi)J_\lambda(\xi)+J_{u_5}(\xi)J_\lambda(-\xi)=0.
\]
That would make \(\mathcal Z\) odd.

### Type B: divisibility at mixed points
At mixed points, the deeper structure forces
\[
J_{u_5}=J_\lambda\cdot \Xi
\]
with
\[
\ord_0 \Xi\ge 4.
\]
That would give the mixed order-gap theorem immediately.

### Type C: relation with the second curvature ratio
The note’s other useful below-lifted invariant is
\[
\Theta=\frac{W_{u_5}}{W}.
\]
So the only plausible relation still worth hunting at the current depth is a genuinely RH-specific link between
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\Theta=\frac{W_{u_5}}{W}.
\]
But it would have to be strong enough to imply either oddness of \(\mathcal Z\) in the nonmixed chart or \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4\) at mixed points. A generic differential relation between them is not enough. 

## 4. So what can I actually do next

Given that the other chat is on attack #1, the most useful continuation here is to reframe the search so it doesn’t waste time on identities that are too weak.

The exact theorem targets are now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Nonmixed target: }\mathcal Z \text{ odd after normalization.}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\text{Mixed target: }\ord_0 \mathcal Z\ge 4.
}
\]

And the only places those can still come from are:

- a source-level involution symmetry,
- a matrix-level identity forcing parity or divisibility of \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\),
- or a very rigid relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\) that implies one of the two targets.

So the real outcome of attacks #2 and #3 is not a proof, but a sharper impossibility theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No further progress on the \(4\)-point branch will come from manipulating }\mathcal Z
\text{ as a generic analytic germ.}
}
\]

It has to come from deeper RH structure acting on \(\mathcal Z\) or \(J_{u_5},J_\lambda\). 

The most useful next thing I can do is draft the exact proposition/remark patch for the paper that records this strengthened \(\mathcal Z\)-level no-go and the sharpened targets, so the draft stops suggesting the current lifted variables might still be enough.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 00:32:49)

_Thought for 2m 34s_

Here is the strongest continuation I can justify.

The new note suggests one more serious attack: not just treat
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\Theta=\frac{W_{u_5}}{W}
\]
as two separate curvature ratios, but ask whether there is **any nontrivial local relation** between them at the current level. Using the explicit formulas, I think the answer is again essentially **no**. turn203file0

## 1. Exact relation in the simplest gauge

Take the good-patch normalization
\[
c\equiv 1,\qquad v_5=x,
\]
so
\[
\mu=\frac1x,\qquad
Y=u_5,\qquad
S=\Delta_7/x,
\qquad
\nu=\frac{u_5}{v_5}=\frac{Y}{x},
\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}=S.
\]
Then the two curvature ratios become completely explicit.

First,
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}.
\]

Second, since
\[
\frac{d}{d\mu}=-x^2\frac{d}{dx},
\]
one computes
\[
\nu_{\mu\mu}=x^3Y'',
\qquad
\lambda_{\mu\mu}=x^4S''+2x^3S'.
\]
Hence
\[
\Theta=\frac{\nu_{\mu\mu}}{\lambda_{\mu\mu}}
      =\frac{Y''}{xS''+2S'}
      =\mathcal Z\cdot \frac{S''}{xS''+2S'}.
\]

So the exact relation is
\[
\boxed{
\Theta=\mathcal Z\,\frac{S''}{xS''+2S'}.
}
\]

Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{
(Z-x\Theta)\,S''=2\Theta\,S',
}
\]
where I temporarily write \(Z=\mathcal Z\).

This is the key identity.

## 2. Consequence: \((\mathcal Z,\Theta)\) are still locally flexible

Write
\[
p:=S'.
\]
Then the relation becomes the first-order linear ODE
\[
(Z-x\Theta)\,p' - 2\Theta\,p = 0.
\]

So if \(Z\) and \(\Theta\) are given analytic germs and
\[
Z-x\Theta \not\equiv 0
\]
near the basepoint, then \(p\) is determined by
\[
\frac{p'}{p}=\frac{2\Theta}{Z-x\Theta},
\]
hence there is a local analytic solution
\[
p(x)=C\exp\!\left(\int \frac{2\Theta}{Z-x\Theta}\,dx\right),
\]
and therefore an analytic \(S\). Once \(S\) is chosen, define \(Y\) by
\[
Y''=Z\,S''.
\]
Then automatically the resulting germ realizes the prescribed pair \((Z,\Theta)\).

So we get a stronger no-go statement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On the current lifted-variable package, }(\mathcal Z,\Theta)\text{ are locally almost arbitrary.}
}
\]

More precisely, any analytic pair \((Z,\Theta)\) satisfying the mild nondegeneracy
\[
Z-x\Theta\not\equiv 0
\]
is locally realizable in this gauge.

That means attack #1, if pursued only at the present \(J/W\)-ratio level, is also doomed in general. A generic relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\) will not kill the low-order dangerous \(4\)-point patterns. It would have to be an **RH-specific** relation much stronger than mere local compatibility. 

## 3. What would actually be strong enough

This clarifies what kind of relation could still matter.

A useful RH-specific relation would have to imply one of the two dream targets:

### Nonmixed
\[
\mathcal Z(-\xi)=-\mathcal Z(\xi)
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z' \text{ even.}
\]

### Mixed
\[
\ord_0 \mathcal Z \ge 4.
\]

Any weaker relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\) will still leave the low-order dangerous list open.

So the search is now very sharp:

- not “is there some formula linking \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\)?”
- but “is there an RH-specific symmetry or matrix identity forcing **oddness** of \(\mathcal Z\) after nonmixed normalization, or a **mixed order gap** \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\ge 4\)?”

## 4. Best honest conclusion

So the continuation result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{At the current level, even the pair }(\mathcal Z,\Theta)\text{ is too flexible to force the dream identity.}
}
\]

That pushes the remaining \(4\)-point hunt one layer lower again:

- source-level involution symmetry, or
- a matrix-level fixed-sector identity strong enough to force parity/divisibility of \(\mathcal Z\).

That is the cleanest place the search stands now.

\]
Then this becomes the first-order ODE
\[
(\mathcal Z-x\Theta)\,p'-2\Theta\,p=0.
\]

---

## 2. Stronger flexibility theorem

Suppose \(\mathcal Z,\Theta\) are analytic germs and
\[
\mathcal Z-x\Theta\not\equiv 0.
\]
Then wherever
\[
\frac{2\Theta}{\mathcal Z-x\Theta}
\]
is analytic, the ODE gives
\[
\frac{p'}{p}=\frac{2\Theta}{\mathcal Z-x\Theta},
\]
hence
\[
p(x)=C\exp\!\left(\int \frac{2\Theta}{\mathcal Z-x\Theta}\,dx\right),
\qquad C\neq 0,
\]
is analytic. Then define \(S\) by \(S'=p\), and define \(Y\) by
\[
Y''=\mathcal Z\,S''.
\]
This realizes the prescribed pair \((\mathcal Z,\Theta)\).

So the true strengthened realization theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{At the current level, any locally compatible analytic pair }(\mathcal Z,\Theta)\text{ is realizable.}
}
\]

That is stronger than the earlier realization lemma for arbitrary \((Y,S)\), and it kills a broad class of possible “attack #1 only” hopes. It means that a **generic relation** between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\) still will not force the dream identity. 

---

## 3. What would actually be strong enough

So the remaining search is now very sharply constrained.

For the nonmixed branch, the only kind of useful theorem would be something that directly forces

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z(-\xi)=-\mathcal Z(\xi)
}
\]
after nonmixed normalization, equivalently
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R(\xi)=\mathcal Z'(\xi)\ \text{is even.}
}
\]

For the mixed branch, the only kind of useful theorem would be something that directly forces
\[
\boxed{
\ord_0 \mathcal Z\ge 4.
}
\]

A weak differential relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\) is not enough.

---

## 4. Exact reformulation in terms of \(J\)-Wronskians

Because
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
\]
the two surviving theorem targets are now:

### Nonmixed parity target
\[
\boxed{
\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\in \xi\,\mathbf C\{\xi^2\}
\quad\text{after nonmixed normalization.}
}
\]

### Mixed order-gap target
\[
\boxed{
\ord_0 J_{u_5}\ge \ord_0 J_\lambda+4.
}
\]

That is the sharpest invariant formulation of attacks #2 and #3 I can honestly give now.

---

## 5. What this tells us about where to attack next

At this point, the only plausible places a real RH-specific identity can still live are:

### A. Source-level involution symmetry
A symmetry of the corrected quotient-germ map that becomes
\[
\xi\mapsto -\xi
\]
after nonmixed normalization and forces oddness of
\[
\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}.
\]

### B. Matrix-level divisibility / parity identity
A fixed-sector matrix identity strong enough to force either
\[
\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\in \xi\,\mathbf C\{\xi^2\}
\]
or
\[
\ord_0 J_{u_5}\ge \ord_0 J_\lambda+4.
\]

### C. A very rigid relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\)
Not just any formula, but one that directly implies oddness/divisibility of \(\mathcal Z\). For example,
\[
\mathcal Z=\Phi(\Theta)
\]
with \(\Phi\) odd and \(\Theta\) even after normalization, or something equally strong. But nothing of that kind is visible yet. 

---

## 6. My honest conclusion

So the continuation result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The current file reduces the \(4\)-point branch to parity/divisibility of }\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\text{ but does not yet contain the RH-specific identity needed to force it.}
}
\]

And more sharply:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No further progress on attacks \#2 or \#3 will come from manipulating }\mathcal Z,\Theta
\text{ as generic analytic curvature ratios.}
}
\]

The only real next step is to go below them — source-level symmetry or matrix-level fixed-sector structure.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 01:13:26)

_Thought for 15s_

Here is the strongest attack I can make on the involution route.

The right move is to stop saying “find a symmetry” in the abstract and instead prove a **conditional descent theorem** that makes the exact missing pieces explicit.

Let
\[
\Sigma(M)(s):=J_0\,M(-s)\,J_0.
\]
The raw corrected odd germ is supposed to satisfy
\[
\Sigma(M)=M.
\]
The question is whether this descends, after all the quotienting/normalizing, to
\[
\xi\mapsto-\xi
\]
on the nonmixed \(4\)-point chart.

The answer is: **yes, if and only if three specific equivariance statements hold.** That is the clean attack result.

---

## 1. The exact conditional descent theorem

Suppose the one-pair construction passes through these stages:

1. a raw odd-germ space \(\mathcal G_{\mathrm{raw}}\) with involution \(\Sigma\);
2. quotient by a value/gauge subspace \(\mathcal N\subset \mathcal G_{\mathrm{raw}}\);
3. extraction of the fixed-sector coefficients
   \[
   (u_5,v_5,\Delta_7);
   \]
4. passage to the lifted chart
   \[
   x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
   Y=\frac{u_5}{c},\qquad
   S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5};
   \]
5. nonmixed normalization to
   \[
   Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr).
   \]

Then the dream identity
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi)
\]
follows provided all three of the following are true:

### (E1) the value/gauge subspace is \(\Sigma\)-stable

\[
\Sigma(\mathcal N)=\mathcal N.
\]

Then \(\Sigma\) descends to the quotient.

### (E2) the fixed-sector extraction is equivariant

On the quotient, \(\Sigma\) acts on the extracted scalar data by
\[
u_5\mapsto -u_5,\qquad
v_5\mapsto -v_5,\qquad
\Delta_7\mapsto \Delta_7.
\]

This is the exact parity pattern needed: \(u_5,v_5\) odd, \(\Delta_7\) even.

### (E3) the nonmixed chart normalization is chosen equivariantly

The normalization sending the lifted germ to
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr)
\]
is compatible with the induced involution, so that on the normalized chart
\[
\xi\mapsto-\xi.
\]

If (E1)–(E3) hold, then automatically
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi).
\]

---

## 2. Why this theorem is true

This is just bookkeeping, but important bookkeeping.

If \(u_5,v_5\) are odd and \(\Delta_7\) is even under the descended involution, then
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}
\]
changes sign, while
\[
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}
\]
has the same parity as \(x^{-1}\) times an even numerator; after the nonmixed normalization \(S\sim \xi^2\), this forces the involution to act by
\[
\xi\mapsto-\xi.
\]

And then
\[
Y=\frac{u_5}{c}
\]
inherits odd parity, so in normalized coordinates
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi).
\]

Equivalently,
\[
\mathcal Z(\xi)=\frac{Y''}{S''}\in \xi\,\mathbf C\{\xi^2\},
\qquad
\mathcal R(\xi)=\mathcal Z'(\xi)\in \mathbf C\{\xi^2\}.
\]

So the dangerous nonmixed list
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}
\]
collapses immediately.

---

## 3. What this means for the current draft

This is the key attack conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The problem is no longer “find a symmetry.” The problem is to prove (E1), (E2), and (E3).}
}
\]

And now we can say exactly where the current draft stands.

### On (E1): plausible but not formalized
This is likely true, but the file does not yet cleanly separate the raw pure-value odd-jet space from the normalization/gauge directions. Without that separation, \(\Sigma\)-stability of the gauge family is not a theorem yet.

### On (E2): partially visible
This is the most promising part. The file already has the fixed/anti-fixed decomposition
\[
\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},\qquad
\mathfrak a=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\},
\]
and the swap involution should act by \(+1\) on \(\mathfrak f\) and \(-1\) on \(\mathfrak a\). But the actual parity action on the extracted scalars
\[
(u_5,v_5,\Delta_7)
\]
is not yet proved as a theorem.

### On (E3): completely missing
This is the real gap. The file does not yet prove that the chart normalization can be made equivariant so that the descended involution acts by
\[
\xi\mapsto-\xi.
\]

So the involution hunt is now reduced to an exact three-lemma program.

---

## 4. The exact lemmas to attack next

If you want to keep pushing this route, the next lemmas should be stated exactly like this.

### Lemma A. Raw/gauge separation
Define the raw pure-value odd-jet space and the value/gauge subspace separately, and prove
\[
\Sigma(\mathcal N)=\mathcal N.
\]

### Lemma B. Fixed-sector parity
Prove that after quotienting by \(\mathcal N\), the induced involution acts on the fixed-sector coefficients by
\[
u_5\mapsto -u_5,\qquad
v_5\mapsto -v_5,\qquad
\Delta_7\mapsto \Delta_7.
\]

This is the critical fixed-sector parity statement.

### Lemma C. Equivariant normalization
Show the nonmixed normalization to
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr)
\]
can be chosen so that the induced involution is
\[
\xi\mapsto-\xi.
\]

Then the dream identity follows formally.

---

## 5. The strongest honest conclusion

So the continuation of the attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The source-level involution route is still viable, but only as an equivariant-descent theorem through the normalization stack.}
}
\]

That is much sharper than before. It tells you exactly what to prove and exactly where the current draft still falls short.

The exact attack result is this:

## Conditional descent package

If one could prove the following three source-level equivariance facts, then the dream identity for the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch would follow formally.

First, **raw/gauge separation with \(\Sigma\)-stability**:
the value/gauge subspace must be defined separately from the raw pure-value odd-jet space, and the gauge subspace must satisfy
\[
\Sigma(\mathcal N)=\mathcal N.
\]

Second, **scalar parity after descent**:
around a reflected one-pair basepoint, the descended involution must act on the scalar coefficient package by
\[
c(-\xi)=c(\xi),\qquad
v_5(-\xi)=-v_5(\xi),\qquad
u_5(-\xi)=-u_5(\xi),\qquad
\Delta_7(-\xi)=-\Delta_7(\xi).
\]
This is the parity package that actually matters. If it holds, then
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}\ \text{is odd},\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c}\ \text{is odd},\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}\ \text{is even}.
\]

Third, **equivariant nonmixed normalization**:
the affine normalization sending the lifted germ to
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr)
\]
must be chosen compatibly with the descended involution, so that the involution acts by
\[
\xi\mapsto -\xi.
\]

If these hold, then automatically
\[
\widetilde Y(-\xi)=-\widetilde Y(\xi),
\]
hence
\[
\mathcal Z(\xi)=\frac{Y''}{S''}\in \xi\,\mathbf C\{\xi^2\},
\qquad
\mathcal R(\xi)=\mathcal Z'(\xi)\in \mathbf C\{\xi^2\}.
\]
So the dangerous nonmixed low-order list
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}
\]
collapses immediately. This is the right source-level form of the dream identity. turn220file16

and it clearly treats \(u_5,v_5,u_7,v_7,\Delta_7\) as the right fixed-sector one-pair scalars.
