Here is the strongest clean continuation I can make on the involution route.

It does **not** prove the source-level reflection theorem, but it shows that if you can get the scalar parity package
\[
c(-\xi)=c(\xi),\qquad
v_5(-\xi)=-v_5(\xi),\qquad
u_5(-\xi)=-u_5(\xi),\qquad
\Delta_7(-\xi)=-\Delta_7(\xi),
\]
then the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch collapses formally. So this is the exact payoff lemma the involution hunt is aiming for. It sharpens the search a lot. turn203file0

Write
\[
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
\qquad
J_f(\xi):=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & f\\
c' & v_5' & f'\\
c'' & v_5'' & f''
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'.
\]
If \(c\) is even and \(v_5,\Delta_7\) are odd, then \(\lambda=\Delta_7/v_5\) is even. So under the desired descended involution, the scalar parities would be
\[
c \text{ even},\qquad
v_5 \text{ odd},\qquad
u_5 \text{ odd},\qquad
\lambda \text{ even}. \; \; \text{turn203file0}
\]

Now compute the determinant parity. If \(c\) is even and \(v_5\) is odd, then
\[
c' \text{ odd},\ c'' \text{ even},\qquad
v_5' \text{ even},\ v_5'' \text{ odd}.
\]
If \(f\) is odd, then
\[
f' \text{ even},\qquad f'' \text{ odd},
\]
and every Leibniz term in \(J_f\) is odd. Hence
\[
f \text{ odd}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad J_f \text{ odd}.
\]
If \(f\) is even, then
\[
f' \text{ odd},\qquad f'' \text{ even},
\]
and every Leibniz term in \(J_f\) is even. Hence
\[
f \text{ even}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad J_f \text{ even}.
\]
So under the scalar parity package above,
\[
J_{u_5}\ \text{is odd},
\qquad
J_\lambda\ \text{is even}. \; \; \text{}
\]

Therefore
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\]
is odd, and
\[
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'
\]
is even. This is exactly the nonmixed dream identity, now derived from a concrete scalar parity package. So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the descended involution makes }c\text{ even and }u_5,v_5,\Delta_7\text{ odd, then }\mathcal Z\text{ is odd and }\mathcal R\text{ is even.}
}
\]

That immediately kills the dangerous nonmixed low-order list
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
\]
because an even analytic germ cannot vanish to odd order at the origin. So the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch would be closed at once. turn220file13

This also gives a **partial** mixed consequence. Since \(\mathcal Z\) would be odd, its vanishing order at a mixed point would be odd. That automatically rules out the mixed dangerous case
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(2,2),
\]
equivalently
\[
(m,n)=(4,6).
\]
But it does **not** kill the other two dangerous mixed cases
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3),
\]
equivalently
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),
\]
since those already have odd \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\). So parity alone would fully solve the nonmixed branch, and partially solve the mixed branch, but not finish mixed. 

So the involution route has now been sharpened to one precise theorem target:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Prove that the raw swap symmetry descends so that }c\text{ is even and }u_5,v_5,\Delta_7\text{ are odd in the normalized reflected coordinate.}
}
\]

If you get that theorem, then:
- nonmixed \(4\)-pair closes immediately;
- mixed loses the \((4,6)\) case immediately;
- and only the two mixed odd-order cases \((3,4)\) and \((3,6)\) remain.

\[

then it does **not** just kill the nonmixed branch and the mixed \((4,6)\) case.

It kills the **entire low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list**. That is the strongest continuation I can justify. turn202file0turn203file0

Here is the exact argument.

Define
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}=\frac{\lambda}{c},
\qquad
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'.
\]
Under the parity package above:

- \(x=v_5/c\) is odd;
- \(\lambda=\Delta_7/v_5\) is even;
- \(S=\lambda/c\) is even.

So immediately,
\[
\boxed{S(-\xi)=S(\xi).}
\]

That matters because the lifted curvature numerator satisfies
\[
K_v=(x')^3S''.
\]
Since \(x\) is odd, \(x'\) is even, hence \((x')^3\) is even. Since \(S\) is even, \(S''\) is even. Therefore

\[
\boxed{K_v \text{ is even in the reflected coordinate.}}
\]

So at any reflected center, the vanishing order
\[
r:=\ord_0 K_v
\]
must be **even**.

That is the key new payoff.

Now look at the dangerous mixed low-order list, which in the current reduction is
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\},
\]
equivalently
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6).
\]
The two cases
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3)
\]
are now impossible immediately, because \(r=1\) is odd, while \(K_v\) is even under the parity package. So \((3,4)\) and \((3,6)\) die at once. turn203file0

Now for the remaining mixed case \((r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(2,2)\), equivalently \((m,n)=(4,6)\):

Since \(u_5\) is odd and \(\lambda\) is even, the determinant parity gives
\[
J_{u_5}\ \text{odd},\qquad J_\lambda\ \text{even},
\]
hence
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}
\]
is odd, and therefore
\[
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'
\]
is even. In particular, \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) must be odd whenever \(\mathcal Z\not\equiv 0\). So
\[
\ord_0\mathcal Z=2
\]
is impossible. Thus \((4,6)\) dies too. turn220file13

So the full conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the source-level involution descends so that }c\text{ and }S\text{ are even while }u_5,v_5,\Delta_7\text{ are odd, then the entire low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list collapses.}
}
\]

- all dangerous nonmixed cases, because \(\mathcal R\) is even, so
  \[
  \ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}
  \]
  is impossible;
- both mixed \(r=1\) cases, because \(K_v\) is even so \(r\) must be even;
- the remaining mixed \((4,6)\) case, because \(\mathcal Z\) is odd so \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) must be odd.

So the involution route is stronger than it looked.

_Thought for 1m 17s_

Here is the sharpest attack I can make on the raw/gauge separation bottleneck.

The clean observation is this:

if the raw corrected odd germ really carries the swap involution
\[
\Sigma(M)(s)=J_0\,M(-s)\,J_0,
\]
then the first odd coefficient cannot live in all of \(M_2(\mathbf C)\). It is forced into the \(J_0\)-anti-fixed subspace. Since the file also treats the first-order value family as all of \(M_2(\mathbf C)\), the only consistent resolution is that the current first-order package is already a **direct sum of a raw involutive piece and a gauge piece**. And, up to the natural fixed/anti-fixed splitting, there is really only one candidate for that decomposition. 

Let
\[
C(X):=J_0XJ_0.
\]
Then
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak f\oplus \mathfrak a,
\]
where
\[
\mathfrak f=\{X:\ C(X)=X\}=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},
\qquad
\mathfrak a=\{X:\ C(X)=-X\}=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\}.
\]
Now suppose a raw odd-germ family has expansion
\[
M(s)=M_1s+M_3s^3+\cdots
\]
and satisfies \(\Sigma(M)=M\), i.e.
\[
M(-s)=J_0M(s)J_0.
\]
Comparing the \(s^{2k+1}\)-coefficients gives
\[
-M_{2k+1}=C(M_{2k+1}),
\]
so every odd coefficient lies in \(\mathfrak a\). In particular,
\[
M_1\in\mathfrak a.
\]

That is the key hard fact. It means the raw first odd jet is at most \(2\)-dimensional, not \(4\)-dimensional. So if the current draft also insists that the first-order value family is
\[
V_{\mathrm{val}}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C),
\]
then that first-order package cannot be a single raw involutive family. It must split as
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}\oplus V_{\mathrm{gauge}}^{(1)},
\]
with
\[
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}\subseteq \mathfrak a.
\]
Since \(\dim \mathfrak a=2\), the only dimensionally consistent possibility is
\[
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}=\mathfrak a,
\qquad
V_{\mathrm{gauge}}^{(1)}=\mathfrak f.
\]

So the strongest exact attack result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the raw swap involution is real, then the only viable raw/gauge separation at first order is }
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus\mathfrak f
\text{ with raw }=\mathfrak a,\ \text{gauge }=\mathfrak f.
}
\]

That is not a proof that the draft already does this, but it is a very strong restriction: there is essentially **no other candidate splitting**. turn221file6

Why is this useful for the \(4\)-point dream identity?

Because once you make that split, the parity route becomes much more concrete.

- The raw odd deformation parameter should live in the anti-fixed sector \(\mathfrak a=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\}\), so under the descended reflection it should be **odd**.
- The normalization/gauge corrections should live in the fixed sector \(\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\}\), so they should be **even**.
- Therefore any scalar extracted from the first genuinely nontrivial fixed-sector response to an odd raw deformation should inherit controlled parity.

That is exactly the kind of mechanism needed to get
\[
c \text{ even},\qquad
u_5,v_5,\Delta_7 \text{ odd},
\]
which in turn would force
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}\ \text{odd},\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}\ \text{even},\qquad
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\ \text{odd},\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z' \ \text{even}.
\]
And that parity package kills the entire explicit low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list. turn202file0turn203file0

So the next theorem to attack is now extremely specific:

> **First-order raw/gauge separation theorem.**  
> Show that the current first-order generator map decomposes canonically as
> \[
> M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus\mathfrak f,
> \]
> where the raw pure-value odd-jet directions are exactly \(\mathfrak a\) and the normalization/gauge directions are exactly \(\mathfrak f\).

If that theorem is proved, then the involution hunt is no longer vague. The remaining work becomes:

1. prove the quotient by the gauge family is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant;
2. prove the fixed-sector scalars \(u_5,v_5,\Delta_7\) inherit the odd parity under the descended reflected coordinate;
3. conclude \(\mathcal Z\) odd and \(\mathcal R\) even.

So the strongest continuation result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the source-level involution route has now reduced to one very rigid algebraic bottleneck: prove the first-order package splits as raw anti-fixed directions } \mathfrak a \text{ plus gauge fixed directions } \mathfrak f.
}
\]

That is the sharpest surviving attack I can honestly formulate.

The strongest exact theorem I can isolate is this conditional one:

## Conditional parity-descent theorem

Suppose near a reflected one-pair basepoint there is a local coordinate \(\xi\) and a descended involution such that:

1. the scalar normalization \(c\) is equivariant and satisfies
   \[
   c(-\xi)=c(\xi);
   \]

2. the quintic fixed-sector extractor is equivariant and its output
   \[
   A_5^{\mathfrak f}(\xi)=u_5(\xi)I+v_5(\xi)S
   \]
   is odd:
   \[
   A_5^{\mathfrak f}(-\xi)=-A_5^{\mathfrak f}(\xi),
   \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
   u_5,v_5 \text{ odd;}
   \]

3. the septic fixed-sector extractor is equivariant and its output
   \[
   A_7^{\mathfrak f}(\xi)=u_7(\xi)I+v_7(\xi)S
   \]
   is even:
   \[
   A_7^{\mathfrak f}(-\xi)=A_7^{\mathfrak f}(\xi),
   \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
   u_7,v_7 \text{ even.}
   \]

Then
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7
\]
is odd, because
\[
\text{even}\cdot\text{odd}-\text{odd}\cdot\text{even}
\]
is odd. Hence
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}\ \text{is odd},\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}\ \text{is even},\qquad
S=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}\ \text{is even}.
\]

Now write
\[
J_f=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & f\\
c'& v_5'& f'\\
c''&v_5''&f''
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'.
\]
Under the parity package above,
\[
J_{u_5}\ \text{is odd},\qquad
J_\lambda\ \text{is even},
\]
so
\[
\mathcal Z \text{ is odd},\qquad
\mathcal R \text{ is even.}
\]
Also
\[
K_v=(x')^3S''
\]
is even, since \(x'\) and \(S''\) are even.

Therefore the entire explicit low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list dies:
- nonmixed dangerous cases die because \(\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}\) is impossible for an even germ;
- mixed cases with \(r=1\) die because \(K_v\) is even, so \(r\) must be even;
- the remaining mixed \((r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(2,2)\) dies because an odd germ \(\mathcal Z\) cannot vanish to even order.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If you can prove }c\text{ even, }A_5^{\mathfrak f}\text{ odd, and }A_7^{\mathfrak f}\text{ even under the descended reflected coordinate, then the whole currently dangerous low-order \(4\)-point obstruction list collapses.}
}
\]

That is the clean payoff theorem.

---

\text{the quintic parity problem can be reduced to parity of the lower matrix coefficients }X_1\text{ and }X_3.
}
\]

The draft gives the fixed-sector quintic/septic package
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}=u_5 I+v_5 S,\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}=u_7 I+v_7 S,\qquad
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7,
\]
and one explicit quintic identity
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w
\]
for
\[
X_1=\begin{pmatrix}u&v\\ v&w\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha&\beta\\ \beta&\gamma\end{pmatrix}. \; \; \text{turn226file11}
\]

So here is the strongest exact attack I can make now.

If the descended reflected coordinate \(\xi\) satisfies the matrix-level parity pattern
\[
X_1(-\xi)=-X_1(\xi),
\qquad
X_3(-\xi)=X_3(\xi),
\]
then
\[
u(-\xi)=-u(\xi),\qquad w(-\xi)=-w(\xi),\qquad
\alpha(-\xi)=\alpha(\xi),\qquad \gamma(-\xi)=\gamma(\xi),
\]
and therefore
\[
v_5(-\xi)=\gamma(-\xi)u(-\xi)-\alpha(-\xi)w(-\xi)
=-(\gamma u-\alpha w)(\xi).
\]
So:

\[
\boxed{
X_1 \text{ odd and }X_3 \text{ even } \Longrightarrow v_5 \text{ odd.}
}
\]

That is a real partial theorem.

It matters because once you also had
\[
u_5 \text{ odd},\qquad c \text{ even},\qquad A_7^{\mathfrak f} \text{ even},
\]
the whole low-order dangerous \(4\)-point list would die formally, as we already worked out. But the current draft does **not** yet give enough explicit formulas to prove the full package. In particular:

- it gives an explicit formula for the quintic \(S\)-component \(v_5\);  
- it does **not** yet give a comparably explicit total formula for \(u_5\) in the same reflected-coordinate parity language;  
- and it explicitly says the **total** septic fixed-sector pair \((u_7,v_7)\) is still not known in closed form. 

So the exact bottleneck is now very specific:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the current file plausibly supports proving }v_5\text{ odd from reflected parity of }X_1,X_3,
\text{ but it does not yet support the full parity package for }u_5\text{ and }A_7^{\mathfrak f}.
}
\]

That gives the sharpest surviving sub-attacks.

The first is:

The third, and real bottleneck, is still:

> **Total septic parity lemma.**

So the strongest honest continuation is:

}

## User (2026-04-20 02:14:36)

Yes. Here is the actual groundwork you can use.

The right move is to work in the **centered symmetric-placement variable**
\[
t_\pm=h\pm \frac{s}{2},
\]
and treat \(s\mapsto -s\) as swapping the two microscopic points. Then every \(2\times 2\) symmetric coefficient matrix
\[
X_n(s)=\begin{pmatrix}a_n(s)&b_n(s)\\ b_n(s)&c_n(s)\end{pmatrix}
\]
satisfies
\[
a_n(-s)=c_n(s),\qquad b_n(-s)=b_n(s),\qquad c_n(-s)=a_n(s).
\]

So define the even/odd half-sum/half-difference variables
\[
\bar a_n:=\frac{a_n+c_n}{2}\quad\text{(even)},\qquad
\delta a_n:=\frac{c_n-a_n}{2}\quad\text{(odd)},
\]
and similarly for every diagonal pair, while the off-diagonal entry is always even.

Concretely, for the matrices already in the paper:
\[
X_1=\begin{pmatrix}u&v\\v&w\end{pmatrix},\quad
X_2=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\b&c\end{pmatrix},\quad
X_4=\begin{pmatrix}p&r\\r&s\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
X_5=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha_5&\beta_5\\ \beta_5&\gamma_5\end{pmatrix},\quad
X_6=\begin{pmatrix}\mu_6&\nu_6\\ \nu_6&\lambda_6\end{pmatrix},
\]
set
\[
\bar u=\frac{u+w}{2},\quad \delta u=\frac{u-w}{2},\qquad
\bar a=\frac{a+c}{2},\quad \delta a=\frac{c-a}{2},
\]
\[
\bar p=\frac{p+s}{2},\quad \delta p=\frac{p-s}{2},\qquad
\bar\alpha_5=\frac{\alpha_5+\gamma_5}{2},\quad \delta\alpha_5=\frac{\gamma_5-\alpha_5}{2},
\]
\[
\bar\mu_6=\frac{\mu_6+\lambda_6}{2},\quad \delta\mu_6=\frac{\lambda_6-\mu_6}{2},
\]
and for the baseline diagonal block
\[
X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2),\qquad
\bar x=\frac{x_1+x_2}{2},\quad \delta x=\frac{x_2-x_1}{2}.
\]

Then the parity is:

- even:
\[
\bar x,\ \bar u,\ v,\ \bar a,\ b,\ \bar p,\ r,\ \bar\alpha_5,\ \beta_5,\ \bar\mu_6,\ \nu_6
\]
- odd:
\[
\delta x,\ \delta u,\ \delta a,\ \delta p,\ \delta\alpha_5,\ \delta\mu_6.
\]

That much is formal from centered symmetric placement.

Now rewrite the canonical quintic and septic transport formulas in these variables.

## 1. Quintic fixed-sector package

From the file,
\[
u_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}=v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w),
\qquad
v_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

Using
\[
\gamma-\alpha=2\,\delta\alpha,\qquad
u-w=2\,\delta u,
\]
and
\[
\gamma u-\alpha w
=
2(\bar\alpha\,\delta u+\delta\alpha\,\bar u),
\]
you get
\[
\boxed{
u_5=2\bigl(v\,\delta\alpha+\beta\,\delta u\bigr),
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
v_5=2\bigl(\bar\alpha\,\delta u+\delta\alpha\,\bar u\bigr).
}
\]

So both \(u_5\) and \(v_5\) are **odd functions of \(s\)**.

This is the first real Taylor groundwork fact.

Write the even variables as
\[
E(s)=E_0+E_2 s^2+E_4 s^4+E_6 s^6+\cdots
\]
and the odd variables as
\[
O(s)=O_1 s+O_3 s^3+O_5 s^5+O_7 s^7+\cdots.
\]

Then
\[
u_5(s)=U_1 s+U_3 s^3+U_5 s^5+U_7 s^7+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{
U_1=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_1\bigr),
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
U_3=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_3+v_2\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_3+\beta_2\,\delta u_1\bigr),
}
\]
and similarly
\[
\boxed{
U_5=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_5+v_2\,\delta\alpha_3+v_4\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_5+\beta_2\,\delta u_3+\beta_4\,\delta u_1\bigr).
}
\]

Likewise
\[
v_5(s)=V_1 s+V_3 s^3+V_5 s^5+V_7 s^7+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{
V_1=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_0\bigr),
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
V_3=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_2\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_3\,\bar u_0\bigr),
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
V_5=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_5+\bar\alpha_2\,\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_4\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_4+\delta\alpha_3\,\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_5\,\bar u_0\bigr).
}
\]

So the quintic canonical package has a fully explicit **odd Taylor expansion** in centered \(s\).

## 2. Septic \(K_1\)-transport package

From the exact formulas in the file,
\[
u_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6
+
\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)
+
b(p-s)+r(c-a)
\Bigr),
\]
\[
v_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6
+
\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w
+
cp-as
\Bigr).
\]

Rewrite each term.

Since
\[
x_2-x_1=2\delta x,\quad
u-w=2\delta u,\quad
\gamma_5-\alpha_5=2\delta\alpha_5,\quad
p-s=2\delta p,\quad
c-a=2\delta a,
\]
you get
\[
\boxed{
u_{7,K_1}
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x\,\nu_6+\beta_5\,\delta u+v\,\delta\alpha_5+b\,\delta p+r\,\delta a
\bigr).
}
\]

For the \(S\)-component, use
\[
x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6
=
2(\delta x\,\bar\mu_6-\bar x\,\delta\mu_6),
\]
\[
\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w
=
2(\bar\alpha_5\,\delta u+\delta\alpha_5\,\bar u),
\]
\[
cp-as
=
2(\bar a\,\delta p+\delta a\,\bar p).
\]
So
\[
\boxed{
v_{7,K_1}
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x\,\bar\mu_6-\bar x\,\delta\mu_6
+\bar\alpha_5\,\delta u+\delta\alpha_5\,\bar u
+\bar a\,\delta p+\delta a\,\bar p
\bigr).
}
\]

Therefore \(u_{7,K_1}\) and \(v_{7,K_1}\) are also **odd functions of \(s\)**.

Write
\[
u_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat U_1 s+\widehat U_3 s^3+\widehat U_5 s^5+\cdots,
\qquad
v_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat V_1 s+\widehat V_3 s^3+\widehat V_5 s^5+\cdots.
\]

Then the leading coefficients are

\[
\boxed{
\widehat U_1
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\,\nu_{6,0}
+\beta_{5,0}\,\delta u_1
+v_0\,\delta\alpha_{5,1}
+b_0\,\delta p_1
+r_0\,\delta a_1
\bigr),
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\widehat V_1
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\,\bar\mu_{6,0}
-\bar x_0\,\delta\mu_{6,1}
+\bar\alpha_{5,0}\,\delta u_1
+\delta\alpha_{5,1}\,\bar u_0
+\bar a_0\,\delta p_1
+\delta a_1\,\bar p_0
\bigr).
}
\]

The cubic coefficients are

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\widehat U_3
=
2\kappa\bigl(
&\delta x_1\nu_{6,2}+\delta x_3\nu_{6,0}
+\beta_{5,0}\delta u_3+\beta_{5,2}\delta u_1
+v_0\delta\alpha_{5,3}+v_2\delta\alpha_{5,1}\\
&+b_0\delta p_3+b_2\delta p_1
+r_0\delta a_3+r_2\delta a_1
\bigr),
\end{aligned}
}
\]

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\widehat V_3
=
2\kappa\bigl(
&\delta x_1\bar\mu_{6,2}+\delta x_3\bar\mu_{6,0}
-\bar x_0\delta\mu_{6,3}-\bar x_2\delta\mu_{6,1}\\
&+\bar\alpha_{5,0}\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_{5,2}\delta u_1
+\delta\alpha_{5,1}\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_{5,3}\bar u_0\\
&+\bar a_0\delta p_3+\bar a_2\delta p_1
+\delta a_1\bar p_2+\delta a_3\bar p_0
\bigr).
\end{aligned}
}
\]

So the septic canonical package is also fully explicit as an odd Taylor series in centered \(s\).

## 3. The determinant \(\Delta_7\)

Since the current file proves
\[
\Delta_7=\Delta_{7,K_1}=u_{7,K_1}v_5-u_5v_{7,K_1},
\]
and each factor \(u_5,v_5,u_{7,K_1},v_{7,K_1}\) is odd in \(s\), it follows formally that

\[
\boxed{
\Delta_7(s)\ \text{is even in }s.
}
\]

More precisely, with
\[
u_5=U_1 s+U_3 s^3+U_5 s^5+\cdots,\quad
v_5=V_1 s+V_3 s^3+V_5 s^5+\cdots,
\]
\[
u_{7,K_1}=\widehat U_1 s+\widehat U_3 s^3+\widehat U_5 s^5+\cdots,\quad
v_{7,K_1}=\widehat V_1 s+\widehat V_3 s^3+\widehat V_5 s^5+\cdots,
\]
you get
\[
\Delta_7(s)=D_2 s^2+D_4 s^4+D_6 s^6+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{
D_2=\widehat U_1V_1-U_1\widehat V_1,
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
D_4=\widehat U_1V_3+\widehat U_3V_1-U_1\widehat V_3-U_3\widehat V_1,
}
\]
\[
\boxed{
D_6=\widehat U_1V_5+\widehat U_3V_3+\widehat U_5V_1-U_1\widehat V_5-U_3\widehat V_3-U_5\widehat V_1.
}
\]

These are the actual Taylor coefficients you asked to get to.

## 4. What this tells you

This is the strongest concrete groundwork you can extract from the current formulas:

- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is **odd** in the centered symmetric-placement separation variable \(s\);
- \(A_{7,K_1}^{\mathfrak f}\) is **odd** in \(s\);
- \(\Delta_7\) is **even** in \(s\), with explicit coefficients \(D_2,D_4,D_6\) above.

So there is no longer any ambiguity about the Taylor-coefficient structure in the centered separation variable. You now have the first three obstruction coefficients on the septic side explicitly.

## 5. Important caveat

This is in the **symmetric-placement separation variable \(s\)**.

It is not yet the same thing as the local coordinate on a good \(v_5\)-patch used later for the lifted \(4\)-point branch, and that mismatch is exactly why the earlier “odd \(\Delta_7\)” dream package was off. What we have now is the correct raw Taylor groundwork in the natural symmetric variable.

So the next translation step, if you want to keep pushing, is:

- relate the centered separation variable \(s\) to the good-patch coordinate \(x=v_5/c\),
- then rewrite the coefficients \(D_2,D_4,D_6\) as coefficients in \(x\).

That is now an honest concrete calculation rather than a vague hope.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 02:23:00)

_Thought for 1m 11s_

Here is the strongest concrete attack I can make from your \(s\)-expansions.

The key next step is exactly what you said: pass from the centered separation variable \(s\) to the good-patch coordinate
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c}.
\]

If we assume the natural centered-placement parity for \(c\),
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots,\qquad C_0\neq 0,
\]
then your formulas already imply:

- \(u_5(s)\) is odd,
- \(v_5(s)\) is odd,
- \(u_{7,K_1}(s)\) is odd,
- \(v_{7,K_1}(s)\) is odd,
- \(\Delta_7(s)\) is even. 

That is enough to do the actual coordinate conversion.

## 1. \(x(s)\) is an odd local coordinate

Write
\[
v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots
\]
and
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots.
\]
Then
\[
x(s)=\frac{v_5(s)}{c(s)}
\]
is automatically odd:
\[
x(s)=X_1s+X_3s^3+X_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{X_1=\frac{V_1}{C_0},}
\]
\[
\boxed{X_3=\frac{V_3C_0-V_1C_2}{C_0^2},}
\]
\[
\boxed{X_5=\frac{V_5C_0^2-V_3C_0C_2+V_1(C_2^2-C_0C_4)}{C_0^3}.}
\]

So, provided
\[
V_1\neq 0,
\]
equivalently \(x'(0)\neq 0\), this is an odd local reparametrization. Its inverse is also odd:
\[
s(x)=\sigma_1x+\sigma_3x^3+\sigma_5x^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{\sigma_1=\frac{C_0}{V_1},}
\]
\[
\boxed{\sigma_3=-\frac{C_0(V_3C_0-V_1C_2)}{V_1^4},}
\]
and so on.

So parity in \(s\) transfers directly to parity in \(x\). That is the first real bridge.

## 2. \(\lambda=\Delta_7/v_5\) is odd, with explicit coefficients

Since
\[
\Delta_7(s)=D_2s^2+D_4s^4+D_6s^6+\cdots
\]
and
\[
v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots,
\]
the quotient
\[
\lambda(s):=\frac{\Delta_7(s)}{v_5(s)}
\]
is odd:
\[
\lambda(s)=L_1s+L_3s^3+L_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
\boxed{L_1=\frac{D_2}{V_1},}
\]
\[
\boxed{L_3=\frac{D_4V_1-D_2V_3}{V_1^2},}
\]
\[
\boxed{L_5=\frac{D_6V_1^2-D_4V_1V_3+D_2(V_3^2-V_1V_5)}{V_1^3}.}
\]

Because \(s(x)\) is odd, \(\lambda(x)\) is also odd.

So after passage to the good-patch coordinate, the raw parity package becomes:

\[
u_5(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
v_5(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
\Delta_7(x)\ \text{even},\qquad
\lambda(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}\ \text{odd}.
\]

That is already a major correction to the earlier “dream parity package.” It points the other way.

## 3. Consequence for \(Y,S,\mathcal Z,\mathcal R\)

Assuming \(c(x)\) stays even under the descended reflected coordinate, we get
\[
Y(x)=\frac{u_5}{c}\ \text{odd},
\qquad
S(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5}=\frac{\lambda}{c}\ \text{odd}.
\]

Now differentiate with respect to the odd coordinate \(x\):

- if \(F\) is odd, then \(F'\) is even, \(F''\) is odd, \(F'''\) is even.

So
\[
Y''(x)\ \text{odd},
\qquad
S''(x)\ \text{odd}.
\]
Hence
\[
\mathcal Z(x):=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{even},
\]
and therefore
\[
\mathcal R(x):=\mathcal Z'(x)\ \text{odd}.
\]

This is the crucial attack result:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the centered symmetric-placement Taylor structure points to }
\mathcal Z \text{ even and } \mathcal R \text{ odd,}
}
\]
not to the earlier dream identity “\(\mathcal Z\) odd and \(\mathcal R\) even.”

That means the naive involution route, if pushed through the centered separation variable, seems to predict the **opposite parity package**.

## 4. What this does to the dangerous list

Your current low-order dangerous list was:

- nonmixed dangerous:
  \[
  \ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\};
  \]
- mixed dangerous:
  \[
  (r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
  \]

If the centered-separation parity package is the correct descended one, then:

- \(\mathcal R\) odd does **not** kill the nonmixed list; it is perfectly compatible with orders \(1,3,5\);
- \(\mathcal Z\) even kills the mixed cases with odd \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\), namely
  \[
  (1,1),\ (1,3),
  \]
  but it does **not** kill
  \[
  (2,2).
  \]

So this route would only eliminate the mixed \((3,4)\) and \((3,6)\) cases, not the nonmixed list, and not the mixed \((4,6)\) case.

## 5. What this means

This is the strongest honest conclusion I can draw from your formulas:

\[
\boxed{
\text{once you translate the raw centered symmetric-placement parity into the good-patch coordinate }x=v_5/c,\text{ the induced parity appears to be }
u_5,v_5,\lambda,S,Y \text{ odd},\ \Delta_7 \text{ even},\ \mathcal Z \text{ even},\ \mathcal R \text{ odd.}
}
\]

So the old “dream identity”
\[
\mathcal Z \text{ odd},\qquad \mathcal R \text{ even}
\]
looks incompatible with the natural centered-separation involution.

That is a real attack result. It means one of two things must be true:

1. the centered symmetric-placement variable \(s\) is **not** the same descended reflection that controls the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch; or  
2. the old dream parity package was simply the wrong target.

## 6. The next exact thing to do

The concrete next calculation is now:

- rewrite \(K_v=(x')^3S''\) and \(T_v=K_v^2\mathcal R\) in the \(s\)-series,
- compute the first few coefficients of \(K_v(s)\) and \(T_v(s)\) using your explicit \(V_1,V_3,\dots\) and \(D_2,D_4,\dots\),
- check directly whether the remaining dangerous mixed case \((r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(2,2)\) is compatible with those explicit coefficient formulas.

That is now an honest finite computation, not a heuristic.

S(x)=A_1x+A_3x^3+A_5x^5+\cdots

A_1=\frac{S_1}{X_1},
\qquad

and \(x'(s)\) is even with \(x'(0)=X_1\neq 0\), the vanishing order of \(K_v\) at the reflected center has the same parity as the vanishing order of \(S''\), hence is always **odd**. So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{in the centered symmetric-placement setting, }r:=\ord_0 K_v\text{ must be odd.}
}
\]

That already kills the remaining dangerous mixed case
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(2,2),

equivalently \((m,n)=(4,6)\), because \(r=2\) is impossible. It also kills the other two dangerous mixed cases

(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3)

only if we can rule out odd \(r=1\) by some further constraint — but parity alone does **not** do that. So the parity package alone kills \((4,6)\), not all mixed cases. That is the corrected conclusion. turn203file0

Now do the same for \(Y\):
\[
Y(x)=B_1x+B_3x^3+B_5x^5+\cdots
\]
with

B_1=\frac{Y_1}{X_1},
\qquad
B_3=\frac{Y_3X_1-Y_1X_3}{X_1^4},

is an **even** germ, provided it is analytic at the point, and

because those are exactly odd orders and are compatible with \(\mathcal R\) being odd. What it does give is:

- \((r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1)\) impossible because \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) cannot be odd;
- \((r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,3)\) impossible for the same reason;

\Delta_7(s)=D_2s^2+D_4s^4+D_6s^6+\cdots,

v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots,

you get

with

L_1=\frac{D_2}{V_1},
\qquad
L_3=\frac{D_4V_1-D_2V_3}{V_1^2},
\qquad
L_5=\frac{D_6V_1^2-D_4V_1V_3+D_2(V_3^2-V_1V_5)}{V_1^3}.
\]
So \(S=\lambda/c\) is odd with explicitly computable coefficients, and the remaining mixed obstruction is no longer qualitative: it has been reduced to concrete coefficient tests through \(D_2,D_4,D_6\).

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the centered symmetric-placement parity really is the descended symmetry relevant to the one-pair chart, then all three currently dangerous low-order mixed \(4\)-point cases are eliminated, but the nonmixed odd-order cases survive.}
}
\]

- the mixed low-order list is plausibly dead from centered-\(s\) parity alone;

## Assistant (2026-04-20 02:47:22)

Here is the strongest continuation I can make from the centered-\(s\) expansion.

The big correction is this:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the centered symmetric-placement parity package does \emph{not} support the old dream identity }
\mathcal Z\ \text{odd},\ \mathcal R\ \text{even}.
}
\]

It supports the opposite parity:

\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z \text{ even},\qquad \mathcal R \text{ odd},\qquad K_v \text{ odd}.
}
\]

And that is enough to kill the **entire explicit low-order mixed list**, but not the nonmixed odd-order list. The raw \(s\)-expansions you gave already imply
\[
u_5,\ v_5,\ u_{7,K_1},\ v_{7,K_1}\ \text{odd in }s,
\qquad
\Delta_7\ \text{even in }s,
\]
with explicit Taylor coefficients \(U_j,V_j,\widehat U_j,\widehat V_j,D_{2j}\). 

Assume
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots,\qquad C_0\neq 0.
\]
Then
\[
x(s)=\frac{v_5(s)}{c(s)}=X_1s+X_3s^3+X_5s^5+\cdots
\]
is odd, with
\[
X_1=\frac{V_1}{C_0}.
\]
So whenever \(V_1\neq 0\), \(x\) is an odd local coordinate with odd inverse \(s(x)\). This means parity in \(s\) transfers to parity in \(x\). 

Now the key point: since
\[
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}
\]
is even divided by odd, \(\lambda\) is odd in \(s\), hence odd in \(x\). Therefore
\[
S(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}=\frac{\lambda(x)}{c(x)}
\]
is odd in \(x\). Likewise
\[
Y(x)=\frac{u_5(x)}{c(x)}
\]
is odd in \(x\). So both \(Y\) and \(S\) are odd germs in the good-patch coordinate.

From that,
\[
Y''(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad S''(x)\ \text{odd},
\]
hence
\[
\mathcal Z(x):=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{even},
\qquad
\mathcal R(x):=\mathcal Z'(x)\ \text{odd}.
\]
Also
\[
K_v=(x')^3S''(x),
\]
and since \(x'(x)\) is even and \(S''\) is odd, \(K_v\) is odd. So:

\[
\boxed{
\ord_0 K_v \text{ must be odd},\qquad
\ord_0 \mathcal Z \text{ must be even whenever finite},\qquad
\ord_0 \mathcal R \text{ must be odd whenever finite.}
}
\]

That gives a very clean elimination of the currently dangerous **mixed** low-order cases. The dangerous mixed list was
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
\]
But under the parity package above:

- \((1,1)\) is impossible because \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) cannot be odd;
- \((1,3)\) is impossible for the same reason;
- \((2,2)\) is impossible because \(r=\ord_0K_v\) cannot be even.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the centered symmetric-placement parity package kills the entire explicit low-order mixed \(4\)-point list }(3,4),(3,6),(4,6).
}
\]

That is the strongest real positive result I see now. It is stronger than what I said before. The coefficient formulas for
\[
D_2=\widehat U_1V_1-U_1\widehat V_1,
\quad
D_4=\widehat U_1V_3+\widehat U_3V_1-U_1\widehat V_3-U_3\widehat V_1,
\quad
D_6=\widehat U_1V_5+\widehat U_3V_3+\widehat U_5V_1-U_1\widehat V_5-U_3\widehat V_3-U_5\widehat V_1
\]
make this concrete at the Taylor-coefficient level. 

But the **nonmixed** branch survives this attack. Since \(\mathcal R\) is odd, the dangerous nonmixed orders
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}
\]
are perfectly compatible with the centered-\(s\) symmetry. So this route does **not** kill the nonmixed odd-order list. It only kills mixed low-order.

So the honest state is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{mixed low-order \(4\)-point obstructions are plausibly dead from centered symmetric-placement parity alone, but the nonmixed odd-order branch needs a different mechanism.}
}
\]

That means the \(4\)-point hunt now splits cleanly.

For the mixed branch, the next exact thing to do is write this as a theorem in the draft:

> centered symmetric-placement parity implies \(x\) odd, \(Y,S\) odd, hence \(K_v\) odd and \(\mathcal Z\) even, so the low-order mixed list is excluded.

For the nonmixed branch, the centered-\(s\) involution is no longer the right dream identity. The next serious attack there has to be something else — either a different reflected normalization not identical to the centered separation variable, or a deeper source/matrix-level identity that constrains the odd germ \(\mathcal R\) more than parity alone does.

## User (2026-04-20 03:52:52)

ok, here's the latest paper draft. can you summarize everything you have learned and explored since this draft? please capture all key structures, calculations, goodies, etc. in math form.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 03:53:21)

I’m pulling together the post-draft discoveries against the current file so the summary is organized by what is now closed, what is only locally classified, and what the real remaining bottlenecks are.

_Thought for 50s_

Yes. Here is the clean mathematical summary of what we have learned and explored **since this draft state**.

## 1. The \(2\)-pair branch is no longer a vague analytic problem

The right object is the **raw corrected two-pair interaction remainder**
\[
\mathcal I(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
:=
\mathcal F_2(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)
-\mathcal F_1(a_1,h_1)-\mathcal F_1(a_2,h_2).
\]
If this source-level object is analytic, symmetric under swapping the two atoms, vanishes when one amplitude is zero, and collapses on the diagonal \(h_1=h_2\), then the midpoint/separation argument gives the exact factorization
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal I
=
a_1a_2(h_1-h_2)^2\,\mathcal J
}
\]
for an analytic \(\mathcal J\). Consequently, every raw cubic/quintic/septic defect is \(a_1a_2\,O(\delta^2)\), \(\delta:=|h_1-h_2|\), and after normalization by \(a_1\) one gets
\[
E_{12}^{(3)}=O(\delta^2),\qquad
E_{12}^{(5)}=O(\delta^2),\qquad
\overline E_{12}^{(7;1)}=O(\delta^2).
\]
Combined with the quantitative strengthened-coincidence theorem and the lower Lipschitz bound coming from
\[
x(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad x'(h)=\frac{M(h)}{c(h)^2}\neq 0,
\]
this gives \(\delta\lesssim \delta^2\), so the two-point bad-core branch is excluded on compact good patches, conditional only on that source-level structural proposition. So the remaining work there is now purely **definition-level**: formalize the corrected quotient-germ map at source level and verify the collapse/symmetry identities. 

## 2. The lifted \(3\)-point branch is now locally classified, but not globally closed

On a good \(v_5\)-patch,
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5},
\qquad \Lambda_v=(x,S).
\]
For the exact lifted \(3\)-point determinant
\[
\Delta_3(x_1,x_2,x_3)
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&x_1&S(x_1)\\
1&x_2&S(x_2)\\
1&x_3&S(x_3)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
one has the factorization
\[
\Delta_3=V_3\,S[x_1,x_2,x_3].
\]
Hence shrinking exact lifted \(3\)-point circuits can accumulate only at
\[
K_v=0,
\]
and there is uniform small-diameter exclusion away from \(\{K_v=0\}\). If one pair collapses, then
\[
\frac{\Delta_3(a,b,u)}{b-a}
\to
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&a&S(a)\\
0&1&S'(a)\\
1&u&S(u)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
so the collapse limit is a tangent-line contact configuration for \(\Lambda_v\). Near a finite-order zero of \(K_v\), after normalization
\[
S(x)=x^m+O(x^{m+1}),
\]
the local opposite-side tangent-contact branch is completely classified:
- \(m\) even: no branch;
- \(m\) odd: exactly one branch, with asymptotic slope \(\lambda_m>1\) determined by
  \[
  \lambda^m-m\lambda-(m-1)=0.
  \]
So the lifted \(3\)-point branch is not globally closed, but its local defect-free picture is now very sharp: shrinking circuits concentrate into \(K_v=0\), repeated-node limits are tangent-line contacts, and finite-order local branches are explicitly classified. The remaining issues there are global recurrence and infinite-order flatness, not local first-contact ambiguity. turn171file0

## 3. Exact \(4\)-point identities on a good \(v_5\)-patch

With
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},\qquad
S=\sigma_v=\frac{\Delta_7}{cv_5},
\qquad
Q_v=(Y,x,S),
\]
the exact lifted invariants satisfy
\[
\boxed{K_v=(x')^3S''(x),}
\]
and
\[
\boxed{
T_v=(x')^6\bigl(S''Y'''-S'''Y''\bigr)
=(x')^6S''^{\,2}\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{Y''}{S''}\right).
}
\]
So on the nonmixed region \(K_v\neq 0\), the canonical scalar invariants are
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z:=\frac{Y''}{S''},\qquad
\mathcal R:=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}.
}
\]
These are better than \(Y\) itself because they are invariant under the affine gauge
\[
Y\mapsto Y+\alpha+\beta x+\gamma S,
\]
indeed \(\mathcal Z\mapsto \mathcal Z+\gamma\) and \(\mathcal R\) stays fixed. So the \(4\)-point branch is really about \((\mathcal Z,\mathcal R)\), not about \(Y\) in an arbitrary chart. turn203file0

## 4. Shrinking exact lifted \(4\)-point circuits concentrate into \(T_v=0\)

For
\[
\Delta_4(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)
=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
1&Y(x_1)&x_1&S(x_1)\\
1&Y(x_2)&x_2&S(x_2)\\
1&Y(x_3)&x_3&S(x_3)\\
1&Y(x_4)&x_4&S(x_4)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
one has
\[
\Delta_4=V_4\,\mathcal D_4,
\qquad
\mathcal D_4\to \frac1{12}W_x(1,Y,x,S)=\frac1{12}T_v
\]
as the four nodes coalesce. So shrinking exact lifted \(4\)-point circuits can accumulate only at
\[
T_v=0,
\]
and if one pair collapses, the limit determinant is the tangent-plane contact determinant for the lifted space curve \(Q_v\). This is the exact \(4\)-point analogue of the tangent-line bridge on the \(3\)-point side. turn171file0

## 5. Nonmixed \(4\)-point branch: exact reduction and local model

At a nonmixed point \(K_v(0)\neq 0\), after normalization one may write
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\qquad
\widetilde Y(0)=\widetilde Y'(0)=\widetilde Y''(0)=0.
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal Z(\xi)=\frac12\widetilde Y''(\xi),
\qquad
\mathcal R(\xi)=\frac12\widetilde Y'''(\xi).
\]
So the dangerous nonmixed low-order cases are exactly
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
\]
equivalently leading terms of \(\widetilde Y\) in degrees \(4,6,8\). Moreover, the local exact odd-order nonmixed branch is real: the normalized model
\[
Q(\xi)=(\xi^n,\xi,\xi^2),\qquad n\ \text{even},
\]
supports a unique smooth local exact branch sheet of opposite-side coplanar quadruples. So generic local geometry does **not** kill the nonmixed odd-order branch. turn202file0

## 6. Mixed \(4\)-point branch: exact reduction and explicit dangerous list

At a mixed point \(K_v(0)=T_v(0)=0\), let
\[
r:=\ord_0 K_v,\qquad s:=\ord_0 T_v.
\]
Then
\[
S''(x)=\kappa x^r+O(x^{r+1}),
\]
so after integrating twice
\[
S(x)=\frac{\kappa}{(r+1)(r+2)}x^{r+2}+O(x^{r+3}),
\]
hence the mixed exponent is
\[
m=r+2.
\]
If the first term of \(Y\) not absorbed by \(\operatorname{span}\{1,x,S\}\) is
\[
Y(x)=\alpha x^n+O(x^{n+1}),
\qquad n>r+2,
\]
then substitution into
\[
T_v=(x')^6(S''Y'''-S'''Y'')
\]
gives
\[
s=n+r-3,\qquad n=s-r+3.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\ord_0\mathcal Z=s-2r+1,
\qquad
\ord_0\mathcal R=s-2r.
\]
So the dangerous mixed low-order list is finite and explicit:
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3),\ (2,2),
\]
or
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal R)=(1,0),\ (1,2),\ (2,1).
\]
That is the sharpest mixed reduction we found. turn203file0

## 7. Strong negative theorem: no new \(4\)-point obstruction exists at the current lifted-variable level

A realization lemma shows that for arbitrary analytic germs \((\widetilde Y,\widetilde S)\), one can realize
\[
Q_v(\xi)=(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\widetilde S(\xi))
\]
on a good \(v_5\)-patch by taking, for example,
\[
c\equiv 1,\qquad
v_5=1+\xi,\qquad
u_5=\widetilde Y,\qquad
\Delta_7=(1+\xi)\widetilde S.
\]
So no identity involving only
\[
c,\ v_5,\ u_5,\ \Delta_7
\]
and their derivatives can exclude the dangerous low-order \(4\)-point patterns. The same negative conclusion survives even after passing to the sharper invariants \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\mathcal R\): arbitrary analytic \(\mathcal Z\) can be realized at the current level. So any real \(4\)-point obstruction must come from deeper source-level or matrix-level structure, not from the current lifted-variable package alone. turn202file0turn205file0

## 8. Below-lifted reformulation: Wronskian ratios

We found the exact identity
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
}
\]
where
\[
J_f(h):=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c(h)&v_5(h)&f(h)\\
c'(h)&v_5'(h)&f'(h)\\
c''(h)&v_5''(h)&f''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Also,
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R=\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right)
}
\]
with \(M=cv_5'-c'v_5\). So the \(4\)-point branch can be phrased as a parity/divisibility question for the ratio \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\), rather than for \(Y\) abstractly. 

## 9. A second below-lifted curvature ratio and why it still does not close the branch

We also found the projective ratio
\[
\Theta:=\frac{W_{u_5}}{W},
\]
and in the gauge \(c\equiv1,\ v_5=x\) the exact relation
\[
\Theta=\mathcal Z\,\frac{S''}{xS''+2S'},
\qquad
(\mathcal Z-x\Theta)S''=2\Theta S'.
\]
But this still does not force the dream identities, because whenever
\[
\frac{2\Theta}{\mathcal Z-x\Theta}
\]
is analytic, one can solve for \(S\) and then recover \(Y\) from \(Y''=\mathcal ZS''\). So even the pair \((\mathcal Z,\Theta)\) is locally almost arbitrary at the current level. This kills attacks based on “some generic differential relation between \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\)” as too weak. Any useful future theorem must directly force parity or order-gap of \(\mathcal Z\), not merely relate \(\mathcal Z\) and \(\Theta\). 

## 10. Centered symmetric-placement variable \(s\): concrete Taylor parity package

Working in
\[
t_\pm=h\pm \frac{s}{2},
\]
and using the centered half-sum / half-difference variables, we found that for symmetric \(2\times2\) coefficient matrices:
- diagonal half-sums are even in \(s\),
- diagonal half-differences are odd in \(s\),
- off-diagonal entries are even in \(s\).

Using the file’s explicit formulas, this gives:
\[
u_5(s),\ v_5(s)\ \text{odd},
\]
\[
u_{7,K_1}(s),\ v_{7,K_1}(s)\ \text{odd},
\]
and therefore
\[
\Delta_7(s)=u_{7,K_1}(s)v_5(s)-u_5(s)v_{7,K_1}(s)
\]
is even in \(s\). Moreover, the first obstruction coefficients are explicit:
\[
u_5(s)=U_1s+U_3s^3+U_5s^5+\cdots,\qquad
v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots,
\]
\[
u_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat U_1s+\widehat U_3s^3+\cdots,\qquad
v_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat V_1s+\widehat V_3s^3+\cdots,
\]
\[
\Delta_7(s)=D_2s^2+D_4s^4+D_6s^6+\cdots
\]
with
\[
D_2=\widehat U_1V_1-U_1\widehat V_1,
\]
\[
D_4=\widehat U_1V_3+\widehat U_3V_1-U_1\widehat V_3-U_3\widehat V_1,
\]
\[
D_6=\widehat U_1V_5+\widehat U_3V_3+\widehat U_5V_1-U_1\widehat V_5-U_3\widehat V_3-U_5\widehat V_1.
\]
So the septic side now has concrete Taylor obstruction coefficients, not just qualitative parity. 

## 11. Translating \(s\)-parity to the good-patch coordinate \(x=v_5/c\)

Assuming
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots,\qquad C_0\neq 0,
\]
one gets
\[
x(s)=\frac{v_5(s)}{c(s)}=X_1s+X_3s^3+X_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
X_1=\frac{V_1}{C_0},
\]
so \(x\) is an odd local coordinate whenever \(V_1\neq 0\). Then parity in \(s\) transfers to parity in \(x\). This leads to the striking parity package
\[
Y(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
S(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
\lambda(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}\ \text{odd},
\]
hence
\[
Y''(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
S''(x)\ \text{odd},
\]
so
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z(x)=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{even},\qquad
\mathcal R(x)=\mathcal Z'(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
K_v=(x')^3S''\ \text{odd}.
}
\]
This is the opposite of the earlier dream parity package \(\mathcal Z\) odd, \(\mathcal R\) even. And it has a very strong consequence:

- \(\ord_0 K_v\) must be odd,
- \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) must be even.

Therefore the entire explicit low-order **mixed** dangerous list
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}
\]
is excluded:
- \((1,1)\), \((1,3)\) fail because \(\ord_0\mathcal Z\) cannot be odd;
- \((2,2)\) fails because \(r=\ord_0K_v\) cannot be even.

So centered symmetric-placement parity appears to kill the whole explicit low-order mixed \(4\)-point list, though it leaves the nonmixed odd-order list alive. That is one of the biggest new positive results since the draft. 

## 12. Source-level involution hunt: what survived and what failed

We found a genuine precursor involution on the corrected odd germ,
\[
\Sigma(M)(s)=J_0\,M(-s)\,J_0.
\]
At the first residual step, this already forces the quotient-level quintic ambiguity into the \(J_0\)-anti-fixed space, so the involution route is real at quintic level. But once one tries to descend it all the way to fixed-sector scalars and the nonmixed chart, the situation forks.

Using the explicit commutator-channel formulas
\[
u_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}=v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w),
\qquad
v_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}=\gamma u-\alpha w,
\]
with
\[
X_1=\begin{pmatrix}u&v\\v&w\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha&\beta\\ \beta&\gamma\end{pmatrix},
\]
we found:

- if \(X_1\) odd and \(X_3\) **odd**, then the commutator-channel \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is **even**;
- if \(X_1\) odd and \(X_3\) **even**, then the commutator-channel \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is **odd**.

The current draft does not resolve whether \(X_3\) is the raw cubic coefficient of the descended odd family, or an already-normalized/equivariant correction term. So the involution route is still viable in principle, but only as an **equivariant descent theorem through the normalization stack**, and the decisive fork is the parity nature of \(X_3\). turn228file6turn230file4

## 13. Strongest current bottlenecks

At this point, the remaining bottlenecks are very sharp:

1. **Two-point**: formalize the source-level corrected quotient-germ map and verify the collapse/symmetry axioms. Then \(2\)-pair is done. 

2. **Three-point**: global recurrence / infinite-order flatness, since the local defect-free geometry is now classified. 

3. **Four-point mixed**: probably already dead at low order from centered-\(s\) parity, but this should be written cleanly as a theorem in the \(x\)-chart. 

4. **Four-point nonmixed**: still open. The current scalar / below-lifted package is exhausted, and the only plausible remaining routes are:
   - an equivariant descent of the raw swap symmetry through the one-pair normalization stack;
   - or a total explicit septic fixed-sector formula strong enough to force parity/divisibility of \(J_{u_5}/J_\lambda\). turn214file11

That is the cleanest mathematical summary of what we have learned beyond the draft.

## Assistant (2026-04-20 04:03:31)

_Thought for 24s_

Yes. There is useful new material in it, and a few pieces are especially worth keeping. The most valuable additions are these. 

First, the summary’s best upgrade is the shift from qualitative “dangerous low-order cases” to a **finite coefficient obstruction list**. The nonmixed side being controlled by explicit coefficients
\[
z_2,\ z_4,\ z_6
\]
and the mixed side by
\[
\kappa_{1,1},\ \kappa_{1,3},\ \kappa_{2,2}
\]
is a real sharpening. Even if some of the long formulas still need line-by-line checking before going into the paper, this is the right form of the problem: a finite jet vanishing problem, not a vague local-geometry problem. That is genuinely new/useful.

Second, the claim
\[
\Delta_7=\Delta_{7,K_1}
\]
is very useful if it is really already proved in the current draft. That would remove a lot of ambiguity about “hidden septic sectors” and make the septic scalar much cleaner than we had been treating it. This is one of the biggest things I’d want to verify carefully against the actual TeX, because if true it substantially simplifies the \(4\)-point story. 

Third, the centered symmetric-placement analysis looks useful in exactly the way the note says: it seems to give an **odd/odd local form at the centered diagonal base**, and from that a punctured-neighborhood exclusion of \(K_v=0\) and \(T_v=0\) near that base. That is stronger than the earlier loose parity discussion. The key value here is not just the parity itself, but the local exclusion consequence. If that checks out, it should definitely be recorded. 

Fourth, the note’s clarification that the centered \(s\)-symmetry is the **wrong symmetry for the old nonmixed dream identity** is important. That matches the direction our later calculations were heading: the centered-separation parity seems to kill the explicit low-order **mixed** list, but it does not kill the nonmixed odd-order list. That is useful because it prevents us from pushing the wrong involution story in the paper. 

Fifth, the explicit witness coefficients for the quintic and septic transport slices are useful as concrete anchors. Even if they do not close the \(4\)-point branch, they are good “hard data” and likely worth preserving in remarks or work-in-progress notes. 

What I would treat more cautiously is the long exact formulas for \(z_4,z_6,\kappa_{1,3},\kappa_{2,2}\). They are the right kind of formulas, but before relying on them in the paper I would want to verify them directly from the current draft definitions. Same for the punctured-neighborhood exclusion: plausible and useful, but I would still check the order argument carefully before stating it as theorem-level.

So my short verdict is:

- **Definitely useful:** the finite obstruction coefficients, the centered-base odd/odd local form, the punctured-neighborhood exclusion idea, and the clarification that \(\Delta_7\) may already reduce to the \(K_1\)-transport part.
- **Probably right but should be checked carefully before patching:** the full explicit \(z_4,z_6,\kappa\)-formulas and the exact “punctured neighborhood free of \(K_v=0,T_v=0\)” statement.
- **Conceptually important:** the centered \(s\)-involution is not the same thing as the nonmixed dream symmetry, so those routes should be separated cleanly. 

If you want, the next best move is for me to turn this into a “keep / verify / don’t overclaim” patch plan against `proof_of_rh(133).tex`.

The strongest useful point is the parity package in the centered symmetric-placement variable (s). The summary makes explicit that, in the diagonal-centered regime,
[
u_5(s),\ v_5(s),\ u_{7,K_1}(s),\ v_{7,K_1}(s)
]
are odd, while
[
\Delta_7(s)=u_{7,K_1}(s)v_5(s)-u_5(s)v_{7,K_1}(s)
]
is even, with explicit coefficients
[
D_2,\ D_4,\ D_6.

[
D_2=\widehat U_1V_1-U_1\widehat V_1,\qquad
D_4,\qquad D_6
]
are worth keeping. They are concrete invariants you can later track under any change from the collision variable (s) to a patch variable. 

A second useful point is the below-lifted identity
[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
]
and the companion projective ratio (\Theta=W_{u_5}/W). I already had the first identity, but the summary’s exact relation between (\mathcal Z) and (\Theta) is helpful conceptually because it confirms that (\Theta) does not introduce a new independent obstruction. It sharpens the conclusion that the (4)-point branch is really governed by (\mathcal Z) and (\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'), not by some extra projective invariant. 

A third useful point is the very clean finite mixed obstruction list written in all equivalent forms:
[
(m,n)=(3,4),(3,6),(4,6),
]
[
(r,s)=(1,2),(1,4),(2,5),
]
[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),(1,3),(2,2),
]
[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal R)=(1,0),(1,2),(2,1).
]
That equivalence class is worth preserving exactly as stated. It is a good way to keep the mixed branch finite and explicit. 

The most interesting claim in the other summary is the centered-(s) parity implication
[
Y(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad S(x)\ \text{odd}
]
after passing to
[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
]
and then
[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{even},\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'\ \text{odd},\qquad
K_v=(x')^3S''\ \text{odd}.
]
If that passage is correct, then the mixed low-order dangerous list is indeed killed immediately, because
[
\ord_0 K_v
]
would always be odd and
[
\ord_0\mathcal Z
]
would always be even. That is potentially very valuable. But this is also the part I would treat with the most caution, because it crosses from the symmetric diagonal collision variable (s) into the good-patch (x=v_5/c) language, and that bridge is exactly where parity arguments have been slippery before. I would not promote that to theorem-level yet without rechecking every step of the coordinate change and the domain where (x) is actually the right local parameter. 

The source-level (2)-pair summary is aligned with what I already found: the right theorem is the structural factorization
[
\mathcal I=a_1a_2(h_1-h_2)^2\mathcal J,
]
conditional on source-level symmetry, one-amplitude collapse, and diagonal collapse. That is useful mostly as confirmation rather than something new. 

The most useful net additions, in my view, are:

* keep the explicit septic Taylor coefficients (D_2,D_4,D_6);
* keep the exact mixed obstruction list in all four equivalent parameterizations;
* keep the (\mathcal Z)-vs-(\Theta) identity as a “no extra invariant here” result;
* treat the claim that centered-(s) parity kills the full low-order mixed (4)-point list as promising but still provisional.

If you want, I’ll turn the genuinely solid parts of that summary into a merged “post-draft findings” section with clear labels: established, conditional, and still-needs-checking.

# 1) Insert the below-lifted Wronskian-ratio package

### Where to insert

Insert this block **immediately after**
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Canonical scalar invariants $\mathcal Z$ and $\mathcal R$ on a nonmixed lifted patch]
\label{prop:canonical-Z-R-invariants-lifted-four-point}
...
\end{proposition}
```

and **before**
```latex
\begin{corollary}[Local defect-free four-point exclusion on lifted ECT subintervals]
\label{cor:local-four-point-exclusion-lifted-vpatch}
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Below-lifted Wronskian-ratio formulas for $\mathcal Z$ and $\mathcal R$]
\label{prop:below-lifted-wronskian-ratio-formulas}
On a good $v_5$-patch, write
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
\lambda:=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5},
\qquad
Y=\frac{u_5}{c},
\qquad
S=\frac{\lambda}{c}.
\]
For any scalar germ $f$, define
\[
J_f(h):=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c(h)&v_5(h)&f(h)\\
c'(h)&v_5'(h)&f'(h)\\
c''(h)&v_5''(h)&f''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\!\left(\frac{f}{c}\right)
=
\frac{c^3}{M^3}\,J_f,
\qquad
M:=cv_5'-c'v_5.
\]
In particular,
\[
Y''=\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_{u_5},
\qquad
S''=\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_\lambda,
\]
so
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}.
}
\]
Moreover,
\[
\boxed{
\mathcal R=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2}
=\frac{d\mathcal Z}{dx}
=
\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right).
}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Set
\[
\phi:=\frac{f}{c}.
\]
Since
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\qquad
x'=\frac{M}{c^2},
\]
one has
\[
\frac{d}{dx}=\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}.
\]
A direct calculation gives
\[
\phi''=\frac{c^3}{M^3}
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&f\\
c'&v_5'&f'\\
c''&v_5''&f''
\end{pmatrix}
=
\frac{c^3}{M^3}J_f.
\]
Applying this with $f=u_5$ and $f=\lambda$ yields the stated formulas for
$Y''$, $S''$, and $\mathcal Z$. The formula for $\mathcal R$ follows by
differentiating with respect to $x$ and using $d/dx=(c^2/M)\,d/dh$.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Projective curvature ratio and no extra independent obstruction]
\label{rem:projective-curvature-ratio-no-extra-obstruction}
Define the projective scalar graph
\[
\nu:=\frac{u_5}{v_5},
\qquad
\mu:=\frac{c}{v_5}=\frac1x,
\]
and the projective curvature ratio
\[
\Theta:=\frac{\nu_{\mu\mu}}{\lambda_{\mu\mu}}.
\]
In the convenient gauge $c\equiv 1$, $v_5=x$, one has
\[
\Theta=\mathcal Z\,\frac{S''}{xS''+2S'},
\]
equivalently
\[
(\mathcal Z-x\Theta)S''=2\Theta\,S'.
\]
Thus $\Theta$ is not an independent new obstruction: it is tied to
$\mathcal Z$ by a first-order compatibility relation. In particular, generic
relations between $\mathcal Z$ and $\Theta$ do not by themselves force the
missing parity / order-gap statements for the lifted $4$-point branch.
\end{remark}
```

# 2) Insert the centered symmetric-placement Taylor package

### Where to insert

Insert this block **immediately after**
```latex
\begin{remark}[Status of the order-$7$ fixed-sector package]
\label{rem:status-full-septic-fixed-sector}
...

```

and **before**
```latex
\begin{remark}[Local gauge-fixing for raw septic representatives]
\label{rem:local-gauge-fixing-u7-v7}
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{remark}[Centered symmetric-placement Taylor package for the canonical quintic/septic scalars]
\label{rem:centered-symmetric-placement-taylor-package}
Work in the centered symmetric-placement variable
\[
t_\pm=h\pm \frac{s}{2},
\]
so that $s\mapsto -s$ exchanges the two microscopic points. For a symmetric
matrix
\[
X_n(s)=
\begin{pmatrix}
a_n(s)&b_n(s)\\
b_n(s)&c_n(s)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
one has
\[
a_n(-s)=c_n(s),\qquad b_n(-s)=b_n(s),\qquad c_n(-s)=a_n(s).
\]
Hence the diagonal half-sums are even and the diagonal half-differences are odd.
For the matrices
\[
X_1=\begin{pmatrix}u&v\\ v&w\end{pmatrix},\qquad
X_2=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\ b&c\end{pmatrix},\qquad
X_4=\begin{pmatrix}p&r\\ r&s\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
X_5=\begin{pmatrix}\alpha_5&\beta_5\\ \beta_5&\gamma_5\end{pmatrix},\qquad
X_6=\begin{pmatrix}\mu_6&\nu_6\\ \nu_6&\lambda_6\end{pmatrix},
\]
write
\[
\bar u=\frac{u+w}{2},\qquad \delta u=\frac{u-w}{2},
\qquad
\bar a=\frac{a+c}{2},\qquad \delta a=\frac{c-a}{2},
\]
\[
\bar p=\frac{p+s}{2},\qquad \delta p=\frac{p-s}{2},
\qquad
\bar\alpha_5=\frac{\alpha_5+\gamma_5}{2},\qquad \delta\alpha_5=\frac{\gamma_5-\alpha_5}{2},
\]
\[
\bar\mu_6=\frac{\mu_6+\lambda_6}{2},\qquad \delta\mu_6=\frac{\lambda_6-\mu_6}{2},
\]
and similarly for the baseline diagonal block
\[
X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2),\qquad
\bar x=\frac{x_1+x_2}{2},\qquad
\delta x=\frac{x_2-x_1}{2}.
\]
Then
\[
\bar x,\ \bar u,\ v,\ \bar a,\ b,\ \bar p,\ r,\ \bar\alpha_5,\ \beta_5,\ \bar\mu_6,\ \nu_6
\]
are even in $s$, while
\[
\delta x,\ \delta u,\ \delta a,\ \delta p,\ \delta\alpha_5,\ \delta\mu_6
\]
are odd in $s$.

In these variables, the explicit fixed-sector formulas become
\[
u_5
=
2\bigl(v\,\delta\alpha+\beta\,\delta u\bigr),
\qquad
v_5
=
2\bigl(\bar\alpha\,\delta u+\delta\alpha\,\bar u\bigr),
\]
so both $u_5(s)$ and $v_5(s)$ are odd in $s$.

Likewise, the direct septic $K_1$-transport formulas become
\[
u_{7,K_1}
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x\,\nu_6+\beta_5\,\delta u+v\,\delta\alpha_5+b\,\delta p+r\,\delta a
\bigr),
\]
\[
v_{7,K_1}
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x\,\bar\mu_6-\bar x\,\delta\mu_6
+\bar\alpha_5\,\delta u+\delta\alpha_5\,\bar u
+\bar a\,\delta p+\delta a\,\bar p
\bigr),
\]
so $u_{7,K_1}(s)$ and $v_{7,K_1}(s)$ are also odd in $s$.

Writing
\[
u_5(s)=U_1s+U_3s^3+U_5s^5+\cdots,
\qquad
v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots,
\]
the first coefficients are
\[
U_1=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_1\bigr),
\]
\[
U_3=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_3+v_2\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_3+\beta_2\,\delta u_1\bigr),
\]
\[
U_5=2\bigl(v_0\,\delta\alpha_5+v_2\,\delta\alpha_3+v_4\,\delta\alpha_1+\beta_0\,\delta u_5+\beta_2\,\delta u_3+\beta_4\,\delta u_1\bigr),
\]
and
\[
V_1=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_0\bigr),
\]
\[
V_3=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_2\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_3\,\bar u_0\bigr),
\]
\[
V_5=2\bigl(\bar\alpha_0\,\delta u_5+\bar\alpha_2\,\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_4\,\delta u_1+\delta\alpha_1\,\bar u_4+\delta\alpha_3\,\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_5\,\bar u_0\bigr).
\]

Similarly, writing
\[
u_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat U_1s+\widehat U_3s^3+\cdots,
\qquad
v_{7,K_1}(s)=\widehat V_1s+\widehat V_3s^3+\cdots,
\]
one gets
\[
\widehat U_1
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\,\nu_{6,0}
+\beta_{5,0}\,\delta u_1
+v_0\,\delta\alpha_{5,1}
+b_0\,\delta p_1
+r_0\,\delta a_1
\bigr),
\]
\[
\widehat V_1
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\,\bar\mu_{6,0}
-\bar x_0\,\delta\mu_{6,1}
+\bar\alpha_{5,0}\,\delta u_1
+\delta\alpha_{5,1}\,\bar u_0
+\bar a_0\,\delta p_1
+\delta a_1\,\bar p_0
\bigr),
\]
and
\[
\widehat U_3
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\nu_{6,2}+\delta x_3\nu_{6,0}
+\beta_{5,0}\delta u_3+\beta_{5,2}\delta u_1
+v_0\delta\alpha_{5,3}+v_2\delta\alpha_{5,1}
+b_0\delta p_3+b_2\delta p_1
+r_0\delta a_3+r_2\delta a_1
\bigr),
\]
\[
\widehat V_3
=
2\kappa\bigl(
\delta x_1\bar\mu_{6,2}+\delta x_3\bar\mu_{6,0}
-\bar x_0\delta\mu_{6,3}-\bar x_2\delta\mu_{6,1}
+\bar\alpha_{5,0}\delta u_3+\bar\alpha_{5,2}\delta u_1
+\delta\alpha_{5,1}\bar u_2+\delta\alpha_{5,3}\bar u_0
+\bar a_0\delta p_3+\bar a_2\delta p_1
+\delta a_1\bar p_2+\delta a_3\bar p_0
\bigr).
\]

Since Theorem~\ref{thm:quotient-septic-closure} gives
\[
\Delta_7=\Delta_{7,K_1}=u_{7,K_1}v_5-u_5v_{7,K_1},
\]
and each factor is odd in $s$, it follows formally that
\[
\Delta_7(s)=D_2s^2+D_4s^4+D_6s^6+\cdots
\]
is even in $s$, with
\[
D_2=\widehat U_1V_1-U_1\widehat V_1,
\]
\[
D_4=\widehat U_1V_3+\widehat U_3V_1-U_1\widehat V_3-U_3\widehat V_1,
\]
\[
D_6=\widehat U_1V_5+\widehat U_3V_3+\widehat U_5V_1-U_1\widehat V_5-U_3\widehat V_3-U_5\widehat V_1.
\]

These coefficients are the first concrete septic obstruction coefficients in the
centered symmetric-placement variable.
\end{remark}
```

This captures the useful explicit formulas without overclaiming theorem-level status.

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\begin{remark}[Provisional centered-to-patch parity bridge]
\label{rem:provisional-centered-to-patch-parity-bridge}
The centered symmetric-placement variable $s$ is not automatically the same as
the good-patch coordinate
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\]
so parity transfer from $s$ to $x$ must be treated carefully.

Nevertheless, if one assumes
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots,\qquad C_0\neq 0,
\]
and
\[
v_5(s)=V_1s+V_3s^3+V_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with \(V_1\neq 0\), then
\[
x(s)=\frac{v_5(s)}{c(s)}=X_1s+X_3s^3+X_5s^5+\cdots
\]
is an odd local coordinate in $s$, hence has odd inverse $s(x)$.

Under this hypothesis, the centered parity package transfers to the $x$-chart:
\[
u_5(x),\ v_5(x),\ \lambda(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5(x)},\ S(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{c\,v_5}(x)
\]
are odd in $x$, while
\[
\Delta_7(x)
\]
is even in $x$. Consequently,
\[
Y(x)=\frac{u_5}{c}(x)\ \text{is odd},
\qquad
S(x)\ \text{is odd},
\]
so
\[
Y''(x)\ \text{and}\ S''(x)\ \text{are odd}.
\]
Hence
\[
\mathcal Z(x)=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{is even},
\qquad
\mathcal R(x)=\mathcal Z'(x)\ \text{is odd},
\qquad
K_v=(x')^3S''\ \text{is odd}.
\]

If this parity transfer is valid on the relevant good-patch domain, then the
entire explicit low-order mixed bad list
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}
\]
is excluded immediately:
\[
\ord_0 K_v \text{ must be odd},\qquad \ord_0\mathcal Z \text{ must be even}.
\]

At present this bridge should be treated as a promising local calculation,
not yet as a theorem, because the exact relationship between the centered
symmetric-placement variable and the later good-patch coordinate still needs to
be checked carefully in the current normalization stack.
\end{remark}
```

This is the place to preserve the “promising but still-needs-checking” result.

# 4) Replace the current dream-identity remark

The current one still says the dream nonmixed identity is \(\mathcal R\) even and the mixed one is \(\ord \mathcal Z\ge 4\). That is still a fair statement for the nonmixed live route, but it no longer reflects that the **centered \(s\)-parity** points a different way and is now mainly relevant to the **mixed** branch.

### Find
```latex
\begin{remark}[Work in progress: the dream identities for $\mathcal Z$ and $\mathcal R$]
\label{rem:wip-dream-parity-identity-four-point}
...
\end{remark}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{remark}[Work in progress: two distinct parity mechanisms on the $4$-point branch]
\label{rem:wip-dream-parity-identity-four-point}
There are now two distinct parity stories for the lifted $4$-point branch.

First, on the nonmixed side, after normalization
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\]
the natural dream identity remains:
\[
\mathcal R(\xi)=\frac12\,\widetilde Y'''(\xi)\ \text{is even in }\xi,
\]
equivalently \(\mathcal Z\) odd up to the affine coplanarity gauge. This would
eliminate the explicit nonmixed low-order bad list
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\}.
\]

Second, on the centered symmetric-placement side, the raw Taylor package points
to a different parity pattern: in the centered separation variable \(s\),
\[
u_5,\ v_5,\ u_{7,K_1},\ v_{7,K_1}\ \text{are odd},\qquad \Delta_7\ \text{is even}.
\]
If this parity transfers through the local coordinate change
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},
\]
then one gets in the $x$-chart
\[
\mathcal Z\ \text{even},\qquad
\mathcal R\ \text{odd},\qquad
K_v\ \text{odd},
\]
which would eliminate the entire currently explicit low-order mixed bad list
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
\]

Thus the centered symmetric-placement parity does not currently support the
old nonmixed dream identity; instead it points to a likely mixed-branch
exclusion mechanism. The nonmixed odd-order branch remains the live $4$-point
bottleneck.
\end{remark}
```

### Replace with
```latex
\begin{remark}[What the four-point branch now reduces to]
\label{rem:next-algebraic-target-four-point}
The lifted $4$-point branch is now organized by the canonical invariant pair
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{Y''}{S''}=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\mathcal Z'=\frac{T_v}{K_v^2},
\qquad
\lambda=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}.
\]

On the nonmixed side, after normalization to
\[
Q_v(\xi)=\bigl(\widetilde Y(\xi),\,\xi,\,\xi^2\bigr),
\]
the explicit low-order dangerous list is
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
\]
equivalently the first nontrivial even Taylor coefficients of
\(\widetilde Y\) occur in degrees \(4,6,8\).

On the mixed side, the explicit low-order dangerous list is
\[
(m,n)=(3,4),\ (3,6),\ (4,6),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,s)=(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,5),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)=(1,1),\ (1,3),\ (2,2),
\]
equivalently
\[
(r,\ord_0\mathcal R)=(1,0),\ (1,2),\ (2,1).
\]

By Corollary~\ref{cor:no-new-jet-obstruction-current-lifted-level}, no
identity involving only
\[
(c,v_5,u_5,\Delta_7)
\]
and their derivatives can eliminate these patterns in general.

The most promising new development is the centered symmetric-placement Taylor
package from Remark~\ref{rem:centered-symmetric-placement-taylor-package},
which suggests a parity mechanism eliminating the whole explicit low-order
mixed list once it is transferred carefully to the good-patch coordinate
\(x=v_5/c\). At present that transfer should still be treated as provisional.

Accordingly, the live attack routes are now:

\begin{itemize}
\item formalize the source-level two-point interaction factorization and close
the \(2\)-point branch;
\item promote the centered symmetric-placement parity bridge to a theorem and
thereby close the explicit low-order mixed \(4\)-point branch;
\item on the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch, search for a deeper source-level or
matrix-level RH-specific identity forcing extra vanishing/parity of
\(\mathcal R\).
\end{itemize}
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\begin{remark}[Post-draft findings: established, provisional, and live]
\label{rem:post-draft-findings-established-provisional-live}
Since the present draft state, three additional conclusions have emerged.

First, on the $2$-point branch, the correct remaining theorem is now very
sharp: a source-level corrected interaction remainder with swap symmetry,
one-amplitude collapse, and diagonal collapse would force the quadratic
factorization
\[
\mathcal I_2=a_1a_2(h_1-h_2)^2\mathcal J_2,
\]
hence the two-point bad-core branch is conditionally closed on compact good
patches.

Second, on the lifted $4$-point branch, the canonical invariants are best
expressed as
\[
\mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
\qquad
\mathcal R=\frac{c^2}{M}\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda}\right),
\]
and the explicit low-order bad list has been reduced to the six jet
coefficients governing
\[
\ord_0\mathcal R\in\{1,3,5\},
\qquad
(r,\ord_0\mathcal Z)\in\{(1,1),(1,3),(2,2)\}.
\]

Third, the centered symmetric-placement Taylor package now gives a promising
mixed-branch exclusion mechanism: in the centered variable \(s\),
\[
u_5,\ v_5,\ u_{7,K_1},\ v_{7,K_1}\ \text{are odd},\qquad
\Delta_7\ \text{is even},
\]
with explicit septic coefficients \(D_2,D_4,D_6\). What remains to be checked
carefully is the transfer of this parity package into the good-patch
coordinate \(x=v_5/c\).

So the present live frontier is no longer a generic local geometry problem; it
is the combination of a source-level two-point structure theorem, a centered-to-
patch parity bridge for the mixed \(4\)-point branch, and a deeper RH-specific
identity on the nonmixed \(4\)-point branch.
\end{remark}
```

---

# Why this is the right patch

This keeps the genuinely solid parts and marks the slippery part correctly.

It preserves:

- the exact \(J\)-ratio identity
  \[
  \mathcal Z=\frac{J_{u_5}}{J_\lambda},
  \]
- the finite mixed obstruction list in all useful equivalent forms,
- the explicit centered-separation septic coefficients
  \[
  D_2,\ D_4,\ D_6,
  \]
- and the fact that \(\Theta\) gives no new independent obstruction.

It also avoids overclaiming the centered-\(s\) to \(x\) parity transfer as a theorem before that bridge is checked carefully.

If you want, the next step is for me to turn this into a **single ordered edit checklist** with copy-paste-ready boundaries so it is faster to apply.

# Patch 1: add the explicit \(x(s)\) and \(\lambda(s)\) bridge coefficients

### Where to insert

Insert this block inside
```latex
\begin{remark}[Centered symmetric-placement Taylor package and diagonal-base local exclusion]
\label{rem:centered-symmetric-placement-taylor-package}
```

**immediately after**
```latex
D_6=\widehat U_1V_5+\widehat U_3V_3+\widehat U_5V_1-U_1\widehat V_5-U_3\widehat V_3-U_5\widehat V_1.
\]
```

### Insert this LaTeX
```latex
\medskip

Assume now that
\[
c(s)=C_0+C_2s^2+C_4s^4+\cdots,
\qquad C_0\neq 0.
\]
Then
\[
x(s)=\frac{v_5(s)}{c(s)}
\]
is an odd series
\[
x(s)=X_1s+X_3s^3+X_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
X_1=\frac{V_1}{C_0},
\]
\[
X_3=\frac{V_3C_0-V_1C_2}{C_0^2},
\]
\[
X_5=\frac{V_5C_0^2-V_3C_0C_2+V_1(C_2^2-C_0C_4)}{C_0^3}.
\]
In particular, if \(V_1\neq 0\), then \(x\) is an odd local coordinate with odd
inverse
\[
s(x)=\sigma_1x+\sigma_3x^3+\sigma_5x^5+\cdots,
\]
where
\[
\sigma_1=\frac{C_0}{V_1},
\qquad
\sigma_3=-\frac{C_0(V_3C_0-V_1C_2)}{V_1^4},
\]
and so on.

Similarly,
\[
\lambda(s):=\frac{\Delta_7(s)}{v_5(s)}
\]
is an odd series
\[
\lambda(s)=L_1s+L_3s^3+L_5s^5+\cdots
\]
with
\[
L_1=\frac{D_2}{V_1},
\]
\[
L_3=\frac{D_4V_1-D_2V_3}{V_1^2},
\]
\[
L_5=\frac{D_6V_1^2-D_4V_1V_3+D_2(V_3^2-V_1V_5)}{V_1^3}.
\]

So, whenever \(x\) is used as the induced odd collision-side coordinate, parity
in \(s\) transfers to parity in \(x\), and one obtains
\[
Y(x)=\frac{u_5}{c}(x)\ \text{odd},
\qquad
\lambda(x)=\frac{\Delta_7}{v_5}(x)\ \text{odd},
\qquad
S(x)=\frac{\lambda}{c}(x)\ \text{odd}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
Y''(x)\ \text{odd},\qquad
S''(x)\ \text{odd},
\]
hence
\[
\mathcal Z(x)=\frac{Y''}{S''}\ \text{even},
\qquad
\mathcal R(x)=\mathcal Z'(x)\ \text{odd},
\qquad
K_v=(x')^3S''\ \text{odd}.
\]
This is the parity package underlying the provisional mixed-branch exclusion
discussed below.
```

This is worth adding because it preserves the actual coefficient bridge you later used.
